Odd Lots · Bloomberg 7 episode(s) · 256 insights

Bloomberg Surveillance 6/9/2026144 min43 insights2026-06-09
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg Surveillance covers a market at a macro inflection point on June 9, 2026: equity futures bounce following Friday's AI-driven selloff as a mega-IPO pipeline (SpaceX $1.8T, Anthropic, OpenAI) tests market capacity, while the Fed's new Chair Kevin Warsh faces his first meeting with inflation near 4% and growing calls for hikes instead of cuts. Layered on top are a partially blockaded Strait of Hormuz keeping Brent crude near $92–98, Taiwan moving to align chip-export controls with the U.S., and a wide debate on whether government stakes in AI companies would distort innovation.
Mega-IPO Supply Wave & Equity Market Capacity · 8Federal Reserve Transition: Warsh's First Meeting · 10Iran Conflict, Strait of Hormuz & Oil Market · 6AI Inflation Dynamic & Rate Sensitivity · 4SpaceX & ARK's AI Infrastructure Thesis · 3Apple AI Strategy & Software Execution Risk · 2Geopolitics: Taiwan Chip Export Controls & AI Arms Race · 4Macro Backdrop: Fiscal Sugar Rush, Labor Market, Concentration Risk · 6
Mega-IPO Supply Wave & Equity Market Capacity 8
SpaceX is pricing at a $1.8 trillion market cap targeting a $75 billion raise on Friday, with institutional orders already exceeding that amount by more than 10x oversubscription, making it the largest IPO in history.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro / Scarlet Fu (hosts)conviction: highSpaceXElon Musk
So what: Absorbing $75B+ in a single week stress-tests institutional liquidity and will force selling of existing public tech holdings to free up capacity.
“SPACEX, THE DEBUT, BIGGEST IPO WE HAVE EVER SEEN, LOOKING TO RAISE $75 BILLION ON A $1.8 TRILLION MARKET CAP.” ▶ 1:00:20
OpenAI confidentially filed for an IPO, with management saying timing is uncertain but the filing gives them the option to go public sooner if optimal; a report indicated OpenAI missed some internal revenue and user-growth targets.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro / Scarlet Fu (hosts)conviction: highOpenAISam Altman
So what: Missed internal metrics suggest OpenAI may want to clean up its numbers before opening its books; the confidential filing is a hedge to keep pace with Anthropic.
≈ “WE HAVE NOT DECIDED ON TIMING YET, IT MAY BE A WHILE. THIS GIVES US THE OPTION TO GO PUBLIC SOONER IF THAT ENDS UP BEING BEST.” paraphrase
Tom Forte (Maxim Group) argues OpenAI is compelled to go public because Anthropic is going public, and hyperscalers holding large contracts with both companies would welcome liquidity and price discovery from both listings.
thesis🗣 Tom Forteconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicTom ForteMaxim Group
So what: Hyperscaler demand for AI services creates a structural buyer base for both IPOs; competitive signaling between OpenAI and Anthropic is accelerating the IPO race.
“WITH ANTHROPIC GOING PUBLIC, OPENAI NEEDS TO GO PUBLIC, THE SOONER THE BETTER.” ▶ 29:39
Gil Luria (DA Davidson) estimates hundreds of billions of dollars of new equity supply will hit the market over the next six months from IPOs (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI) plus secondary equity raises by already-public hyperscalers (Alphabet already priced a $40B+ raise, with Meta, Microsoft, Amazon likely to follow).
data_point🗣 Gil Luriaconviction: highGil LuriaDA DavidsonSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIAlphabetMetaMicrosoftAmazon
So what: Public tech stocks — especially mid-cap and tangential AI plays — are most exposed as investors sell to fund IPO allocations; the market is finite.
“HUNDREDS OF BILLIONS OF DOLLARS OF NEW EQUITY. MICROSOFT, AMAZON, META JUST TOOK NOTE THAT ALPHABET JUST HAD A VERY SUCCESSFUL EQUITY RAISE.” ▶ 1:53:15
Gil Luria identifies the most vulnerable securities in a supply-driven selloff as tangential AI-trade companies — optical, smaller semis, nuclear, quantum — that investors bought as proxies for OpenAI/Anthropic; core AI names (Nvidia, Microsoft, Micron) still carry reasonable multiples.
thesis🗣 Gil Luriaconviction: highGil LuriaNvidiaMicrosoftMicronAMD
So what: As IPO access opens for direct AI exposure, proxy trades in nuclear, optical, quantum computing may face the sharpest de-rating.
“SOME OF THE CORE AI TRADES ARE NOT THAT EXPENSIVE. IF YOU LOOK AT NVIDIA, MICROSOFT, MICRON, THESE ARE COMPANIES THAT ARE CRITICAL FOR THE DEPLOYMENT OF AI. THEIR MULTIPLES ARE STILL LOW.” ▶ 1:57:38
Gil Luria notes OpenAI's gamesmanship in going second to Anthropic on IPO timing: whoever files first sets the metrics narrative, but going after Anthropic risks a depleted capital pool; OpenAI's confidential filing is an attempt to preserve optionality.
causal🗣 Gil Luriaconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicSam AltmanGil Luria
So what: Narrative framing of metrics (ARR definition, margin treatment) will be a major valuation lever — the first IPO filer gets to define what 'good' looks like for AI companies.
“WHOEVER GOES FIRST GETS TO DRIVE THE NARRATIVE ON WHAT METRICS ARE IMPORTANT, HOW TO RECORD THEIR NUMBERS.” ▶ 1:58:29
Matthew (third-hour guest, macro strategist) warns that the equity market at 2x GDP in market cap means 'markets are wagging the economy' and that the tidal wave of IPO supply plus global central bank tightening could create a liquidity crunch that derails economic improvement faster than macro data would suggest.
causal🗣 Matthew (macro strategist, unnamed firm)conviction: medium
So what: Liquidity, not earnings or fundamentals, is the pivotal variable for the remainder of 2026; a 'liquidity event' could trigger a correction faster than soft macro data would predict.
“GIVEN THE TITLE WAVE OF IPOS THAT WILL BE HITTING THE MARKET OVER THE NEXT SEVERAL MONTHS, THE NEED FOR LIQUIDITY IS MASSIVE.” ▶ 1:39:35
Jack Caffrey (JPMorgan Asset Management) is constructive on equities driven by earnings — 22% EPS growth expected this year with mid-10% growth in 2027 — and sees pullbacks as short but sharp rather than regime-changing.
thesis🗣 Jack Caffreyconviction: highJack CaffreyJPMorgan Asset Management
So what: Earnings-driven bull case intact for now; any AI-related pullback represents a tactical entry rather than a structural top.
“LOOKING AT $22 PER EARNINGS GROWTH THIS YEAR AND LOOKING TO NEXT YEAR WE THINK THAT TURNS INTO MID 10'S EARNINGS GAME.” ▶ 4:13
Federal Reserve Transition: Warsh's First Meeting 10
BNP Paribas (Guneet Dhingra) made a new house call that the Fed will reverse all three 2025 insurance cuts at sequential meetings beginning December 2026, with no guarantee it stops at three hikes; trigger was the blowout May payrolls report on top of persistent above-target inflation.
prediction🗣 Guneet Dhingraconviction: highGuneet DhingraBNP ParibasFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: If BNP's call is correct, front-end rates reprice sharply higher; mortgage market, levered credit, and rate-sensitive equities face headwinds into H2 2026.
“WE EXPECT THE FED TO REVERSE 2025'S INSURANCE CUTS AS SEQUENTIAL MEETINGS BEGINNING IN DECEMBER, THERE CAN BE NO GUARANTEE IT WOULD STOP AT THREE.” ▶ 1:26:50
Guneet Dhingra argues TIPS breakevens are structurally underpricing true inflation risk by 20–30 basis points because the breakeven market underperforms in high-risk-off environments, creating a 'faulty fire alarm' that gives Fed officials and investors false comfort on long-run inflation expectations.
contrarian🗣 Guneet Dhingraconviction: highGuneet DhingraBNP ParibasFederal Reserve
So what: If true, the market and Fed are systematically misjudging inflation expectations — a re-anchoring could trigger a violent repricing of the entire rates complex.
≈ “I WOULD THINK OF THE BREAKEVEN MARKET AS A FAULTY FIRE ALARM. YOU RELY ON IT FOR THE SIGNAL. INFLATION EXPECTATIONS AND A TIGHT LABOR MARKET ARE SIGNALING CAUSE FOR CONCERN.” paraphrase
Guneet Dhingra warns the 30-year Treasury yield is becoming 'de-anchored' because ~90% of overseas demand now comes from financial-sector buyers (not central banks), making flows macro-sensitive and relative-value-sensitive rather than yield-level-sensitive — implying more volatility and no historical support levels to lean on above 5%.
causal🗣 Guneet Dhingraconviction: highGuneet DhingraBNP ParibasU.S. Treasury
So what: The long bond can overshoot above 5% without traditional demand stopping it; 5.25% may be the real 'Treasury put' level, not 5.0%.
“BECAUSE THE FINANCIAL SECTORS ARE DRIVING THOSE FLOWS, IT IS NOT AS YIELD LEVEL SENSITIVE BUT A LOT MORE MACRO SENSITIVE AND RELATIVE VALUE SENSITIVE.” ▶ 1:32:41
Goldman Sachs removed its forecast for any Fed rate cuts in 2026, and the broader Street has shifted toward expecting hikes; meanwhile BNP expects three sequential hikes beginning December.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro (host) / Scarlet Fuconviction: highGoldman SachsBNP ParibasFederal Reserve
So what: Consensus is moving rapidly toward a hiking scenario; any Fed communication that preserves even a neutral bias will be seen as dovish relative to market pricing.
David Seif (Nomura) forecasts core CPI for the upcoming print at 0.2% MoM (below consensus 0.3%), driven by fading rent legacy effects from an October shutdown; however, core PCE is projected to rise to 3.4% YoY — inflation is above target and moving in the wrong direction.
prediction🗣 David Seifconviction: mediumDavid SeifNomura
So what: A softer CPI print could give Warsh cover to merely drop the easing bias rather than signal hikes; watch PCE as the more hawkish signal.
“WE WOULD ACTUALLY SAY NOT ONLY IS OUR FORECAST 0.2% BUT WE THINK 0.1% WOULD BE MORE LIKELY THAN 0.3% FOR CORE CPI.” ▶ 2:05:55
David Seif argues the hyperscaler AI capex buildout — rate-insensitive demand — is a separate and durable inflationary force from the Iran conflict, and the Fed should not 'look through' it as it does energy shocks.
thesis🗣 David Seifconviction: highDavid SeifNomuraFederal Reserve
So what: If correct, the Fed faces a structural demand-side inflation problem that blunt rate hikes cannot cleanly address without inflicting collateral damage on credit-sensitive sectors.
“THE RATE INSENSITIVE PURCHASERS, THE HYPERSCALERS, THAT REALLY HITS THE NAIL ON THE HEAD...IT IS VERY HARD TO THINK OF A GOOD JUSTIFICATION FOR LOOKING THROUGH WHAT SIMPLY APPEARS TO BE DEMAND OUTPACING SUPPLY.” ▶ 2:09:49
Reports indicate Kevin Warsh may decline to submit his own dot in the dot plot at the upcoming FOMC meeting, and may use the press conference to begin eliminating forward guidance entirely — signaling a much less communicative Fed regime.
prediction🗣 David Seif / Jonathan Ferroconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal ReserveDavid SeifNomura
So what: Removing the dot plot and forward guidance would increase policy uncertainty and bond-market volatility; the terminal rate becomes harder to price without Fed signposting.
Nisha Patel (Parametric) argues the market is 'fairly far away' from actual hikes — the upcoming meeting is a communication reset (dropping easing bias = restoring optionality), not a signal of imminent tightening; she sees bonds as attractive at current yields even without a near-term cutting cycle.
contrarian🗣 Nisha Patelconviction: mediumNisha PatelParametricFederal Reserve
So what: The bond bear case may be overdone short-term; if growth scares re-emerge in H2 as fiscal support fades, bonds provide both income and hedge value at current yields.
“INVESTORS DO NOT NEED AN IMMEDIATE CUTTING CYCLE TO LET BONDS.” ▶ 2:17:34
Nisha Patel identifies the threshold for actual Fed hikes as requiring multiple consecutive weak payroll prints plus inflation running persistently above 3%; she notes Warsh may quietly accept a 3% informal inflation target rather than 2%.
framework🗣 Nisha Patelconviction: mediumNisha PatelParametricKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: If 3% becomes the de facto inflation tolerance, the bond market's hiking repricing is premature — until payrolls go negative, hikes are unlikely.
Saleha (Bloomberg Washington reporter) notes the Supreme Court is expected to rule imminently on whether the president can fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook, which will define legal limits on presidential interference with the Fed.
data_point🗣 Saleha (Bloomberg reporter)conviction: highLisa CookFederal ReserveDonald TrumpSupreme Court
So what: If the court sides with Trump, Fed independence is structurally weakened; potential market shock to long bonds and dollar if the ruling removes the institutional safeguard.
Iran Conflict, Strait of Hormuz & Oil Market 6
Jack Caffrey (JPMorgan AM) argues oil prices are 'wrong' — futures curves imply lower prices but tankers are not transiting (blockade ~100% effective), physical inventories are declining, and even if flows resumed tomorrow it would be 45 days before supply reached demand centers. This sets up a potential spike to $125–$150/bbl.
contrarian🗣 Jack Caffreyconviction: highJack CaffreyJPMorgan Asset ManagementBrent crudeWTI
So what: Crude at $89–92 materially underprices physical scarcity risk; airline stocks at all-time highs while also exposed to this tail risk represent a specific long/short opportunity.
“OIL PRICES ARE WRONG...TANKERS AREN'T REALLY TRANSITING. THE BLOCKADE THIS MORNING REMAINS 100% EFFECTIVE...YOU HAVE THE SCENARIOS WHERE NOT TO 90 NOT TO ONE HUNDRED BUT POTENTIALLY TO 125 50.” ▶ 9:38
Societe Generale's Michael Haig notes the loss in oil supply from the Iran conflict is double the magnitude of the 1973 shock, yet Brent is only ~30% higher YTD; China has been the key balancing force — reducing consumption from 11.7 million b/d in February to under 9 million b/d by late May as its energy transition accelerates.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro (host, citing SocGen report)conviction: highSociete GeneraleMichael HaigChinaBrent crude
So what: China's structural shift to clean energy is suppressing global oil demand unexpectedly; any reversal in Chinese demand or further supply disruption removes the dampener.
≈ “THE LOSS IN SUPPLY IS DOUBLE THE 1973 SHOCK, SO WHY IS CRUDE ONLY UP BY 30% SO FAR THIS YEAR? CHINA IS ACTING AS THE BALANCING FORCE FROM THE 11.7 MILLION BARRELS A DAY IN FEBRUARY TO JUST UNDER NINE BY LATE MAY.” paraphrase
Lt. Gen. Karen Gibson (Academy Securities) says the Strait of Hormuz is not completely blocked — some tankers are transiting the Omani side with unofficial U.S. assistance — but even under best-case peace conditions, it will take a 'long time' before flows return to pre-conflict levels.
data_point🗣 Karen Gibsonconviction: highKaren GibsonAcademy SecuritiesStrait of HormuzOman
So what: Market hopes for a rapid supply recovery upon any deal announcement are likely premature; physical oil markets will remain tight well past any ceasefire.
Gibson notes Iran has mined the Strait; France and the U.K. are expected to announce a de-mining plan at an upcoming G7 meeting, but timing depends on Iran disclosing mine types and locations. 2,000+ ships are stuck.
data_point🗣 Karen Gibsonconviction: mediumKaren GibsonAcademy SecuritiesIranFranceUKG7
So what: Even post-deal, de-mining is a multi-month process; physical oil tightness has a long tail that energy markets and inflation data have not yet fully discounted.
Gibson identifies a widening negotiation agenda as the key risk: the original three pillars (nuclear, missile program, proxy funding) have been joined by Lebanon/Hezbollah — at Iran's insistence — expanding the complexity and timeline for any deal.
causal🗣 Karen Gibsonconviction: highKaren GibsonAcademy SecuritiesIranIsraelHezbollah
So what: A comprehensive deal is harder and farther away than Trump's rhetoric implies; energy markets should price geopolitical premium more durably, not less.
“THE DIPLOMATIC AGENDA AND OTHER THINGS THAT NEED TO BE NEGOTIATED ARE EXPANDING FASTER THAN DIPLOMATS AND NEGOTIATORS CAN HANDLE.” ▶ 16:12
George Saravelos (Deutsche Bank) identifies the combination of oil prices staying near $100 while fiscal policy remains easy and AI capex continues as the 'most hawkish possible outcome' for global central banks.
framework🗣 George Saravelosconviction: highGeorge SaravelosDeutsche Bank
So what: Central banks globally face an inflationary cocktail where the largest components are not rate-sensitive; blunt rate policy risks K-shaped economic damage.
“IF YOU HAVE THIS CONVERSATION OF ENERGY PRICES HIGH AND GROWTH HOLDING IN AS WELL AS THE AI STORY -- AS THE AI CAPEX STORY IS THE MOST HAWKISH CONVERSATION YOU CAN GET FOR CENTRAL BANKS.” ▶ 47:20
AI Inflation Dynamic & Rate Sensitivity 4
George Saravelos (Deutsche Bank) argues AI is currently acting as a positive demand shock, not a deflationary force; Anthropic's publicly released usage data shows no negative labor market impact but clear demand-side inflation contribution.
data_point🗣 George Saravelosconviction: highGeorge SaravelosDeutsche BankAnthropic
So what: The AI-as-deflation narrative is not yet validated by data; investors relying on AI productivity to justify current valuations and Fed easing are ahead of the evidence.
“FOR NOW AT LEAST WE CAN VERY CLEARLY SEE THERE IS NO NEGATIVE IMPACT ON THE LABOR MARKET AND IT IS MOSTLY ON THE INFLATIONARY SIDE.” ▶ 41:13
Saravelos distinguishes two AI phases: the capex 'build' phase (inflationary, ongoing) and the 'usage' phase (potentially disinflationary). The dominant force right now is capex-driven demand inflation; the disinflationary usage wave is not yet visible in data.
framework🗣 George Saravelosconviction: highGeorge SaravelosDeutsche Bank
So what: Investors expecting AI productivity to offset inflation should watch for when usage-phase data begins to emerge; that would be the fundamental catalyst for rate cuts.
“WE NEED TO BREAK IT DOWN TO TWO STAGES, BUILDING THE SO-CALLED AI FACTORIES...THINK OF IT AS BUILDING THE RAILWAY TRACKS. THE SECOND STAGE IS WHEN YOU USE THE TECHNOLOGY.” ▶ 42:32
Jack Caffrey notes that at the scale of $700–900 billion in hyperscaler capex over the next few years, a meaningful portion flows to grid buildout and power infrastructure rather than semiconductors alone, benefiting utilities and energy infrastructure.
causal🗣 Jack Caffreyconviction: mediumJack CaffreyJPMorgan Asset Management
So what: Electric grid and power infrastructure are underappreciated second-order beneficiaries of AI capex; utility and infrastructure exposure offers a way to play AI build-out with lower valuation risk.
“ELECTRIC DEMAND GROWING MATERIALLY SINCE THE 70'S. I THINK THERE IS MAY BE THAT TRICKLE-DOWN PART OF THE ECONOMY.” ▶ 5:48
Bank of America raised its price target on Oracle to $240, citing robust demand for cloud infrastructure; TD Cowen boosted Alphabet to $475 on expected sharp growth in enterprise and consumer AI demand — while Barclays kept Apple underweight, saying its AI features are insufficient to drive an upgrade cycle.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro (host, citing morning calls)conviction: mediumBank of AmericaOracleTD CowenAlphabetBarclaysApple
So what: Street differentiation is emerging: cloud infrastructure (Oracle, Google) is getting upgraded while Apple's software AI execution is viewed skeptically.
SpaceX & ARK's AI Infrastructure Thesis 3
Tasha Keeney (ARK Invest) values SpaceX's AI opportunity around ground-level AI compute rates of ~$5B per gigawatt per year, with potential to double by selling model services; deals with Anthropic and Google are already struck at higher rates, validating terrestrial AI infrastructure revenue.
data_point🗣 Tasha Keeneyconviction: mediumTasha KeeneyARK InvestSpaceXxAIAnthropicGoogle
So what: SpaceX's data center monetization via xAI is already generating revenue; orbital compute is the longer-term option value that may re-rate the stock beyond traditional launch/connectivity metrics.
ARK Invest's fund has SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI as its top three holdings; the fund grew 30% over the past three years. ARK estimates the AI enterprise software market at ~$7 trillion by 2030 with three to four dominant players.
data_point🗣 Tasha Keeneyconviction: highTasha KeeneyARK InvestSpaceXAnthropicOpenAI
So what: ARK's concentrated bet on the AI IPO wave means its fund performance is tightly coupled to IPO success and post-listing valuations — a useful sentiment proxy.
“ARK HAS THE POTENTIAL FOR A.I. ENTERPRISE SOFTWARE IN 2030 AT ROUGHLY $7 TRILLION.” ▶ 1:20:55
ARK expects SpaceX's Starship full-reusability program to succeed roughly within the next year, which would drive launch costs from ~$2,000/kg today to low hundreds of dollars/kg — the enabling condition for orbital data centers.
prediction🗣 Tasha Keeneyconviction: mediumTasha KeeneyARK InvestSpaceXStarship
So what: Starship full reusability is a binary technology milestone: success unlocks a new orbital compute market; failure delays the key long-term revenue driver.
“OUR RESEARCH SUGGESTS THEY'LL GO TO THE LOW HUNDREDS OF DOLLARS PER KILOGRAM.” ▶ 1:17:19
Apple AI Strategy & Software Execution Risk 2
Apple's WWDC 2026 keynote unveiled a redesigned Siri app using Google Gemini technology under license; investors were unimpressed, sending the stock down ~2% on concerns about the long-delayed AI strategy and dependence on Google.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro / Scarlet Fuconviction: highAppleSiriGoogleGeminiTim CookJohn Ternus
So what: Apple's licensing of Gemini raises concerns about giving Google access to Apple's device data and user base — a potential long-term competitive risk despite near-term convenience.
“SOME APPEAR TO BE RACING FORWARD, SEEMINGLY PURSUING AI FOR THE SAKE OF AI. WE BELIEVE TRULY HELPFUL AI IT MUST BE CENTERED AROUND YOU AND YOUR NEEDS.” ▶ 27:30
Tom Forte (Maxim Group) notes Apple's iPhone sales grew 10%+ in the March quarter without a compelling AI story; new AI features require specific newer hardware and will gate some capabilities behind iCloud subscriptions, effectively monetizing AI in a model comparable to OpenAI/Anthropic subscriptions.
causal🗣 Tom Forteconviction: mediumTom ForteMaxim GroupAppleOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Apple's hardware-upgrade and subscription monetization approach could be underappreciated by bears; the installed base of billions of devices is a durable moat even if AI software lags.
“I AM SURPRISED AND IMPRESSED HOW WELL IPHONE SALES HAVE BEEN WITHOUT GETTING THE AI RIGHT. MORE THAN 10% GROWTH IN THE MARCH QUARTER.” ▶ 34:47
Geopolitics: Taiwan Chip Export Controls & AI Arms Race 4
Taiwan is weighing export controls on AI chips that would align with U.S. restrictions, potentially enabling Taiwan to criminally prosecute unauthorized AI chip exports to China — the first time Taiwan could enforce chip smuggling as a criminal violation.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro / Scarlet Fu (hosts, citing Bloomberg reporting)conviction: highTaiwanChinaNvidiaUnited States
So what: If enacted, Taiwan's alignment closes the smuggling loophole that has partially circumvented U.S. export controls; significant negative for any entity (including Chinese cloud providers) reliant on unofficially sourced Nvidia chips.
Lt. Gen. Gibson warns that the Taiwan Strait is being under-watched because all attention is on the Middle East; strategic risks in the Taiwan Strait haven't changed fundamentally but the optics of U.S. resolve have been complicated by the Iran negotiations.
contrarian🗣 Karen Gibsonconviction: mediumKaren GibsonAcademy SecuritiesTaiwanChina
So what: The market's focus on Iran is creating a window of relative complacency on Taiwan risk; investors should maintain position hedges against Taiwan geopolitical escalation.
“WE SHOULD GET SO FIXATED WITH TACTICAL DEVELOPMENTS THAT DON'T CHANGE A LOT STRATEGICALLY...THERE ARE SO MANY OTHER THINGS GEOPOLITICALLY THAT WE NEED TO PAY ATTENTION TO.” ▶ 23:04
Jennifer Huddleston (Cato Institute) warns that Trump administration discussions of taking government equity stakes in OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX — an 'odd horseshoe' alignment of Trump and Bernie Sanders on sovereign wealth fund-style AI investment — risk politicizing AI development and picking winners at a dynamic inflection point.
thesis🗣 Jennifer Huddlestonconviction: highJennifer HuddlestonCato InstituteOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXDonald TrumpBernie Sanders
So what: Government equity stakes could constrain future AI product decisions, deter smaller competitors, and introduce political risk into AI company governance — a new class of regulatory risk for the sector.
“A BIG CONCERN WITH GOVERNMENT INVESTMENT IS A RISK THAT ONLY THOSE LARGE COMPANIES CAN BE THE ONES THAT COULD POTENTIALLY NAVIGATE THOSE THINGS.” ▶ 1:05:24
The DOD is winding down its Anthropic contract within six months and switching to other partners; the 'supply chain designation' of Anthropic by the Pentagon raised free-speech and government-influence concerns beyond the typical contract dispute.
data_point🗣 Annmarie Hordern / Jennifer Huddlestonconviction: highAnthropicDODPentagon
So what: Defense AI contract dynamics are in flux; companies with clean national-security positioning (no free-speech concerns) gain a structural advantage in competing for DOD AI contracts.
Macro Backdrop: Fiscal Sugar Rush, Labor Market, Concentration Risk 6
Matthew (macro strategist) argues the current U.S. economic strength is a 'fiscal sugar rush' — driven by corporate tax cuts, tariff refunds, and strategic petroleum reserve drawdowns at a record pace — and will fade in H2 2026, potentially coinciding with global central bank tightening.
thesis🗣 Matthew (macro strategist)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveU.S. Treasury
So what: H2 2026 could see a growth scare as fiscal tailwinds fade just as central banks tighten; this setup would favor bonds and defensive equities over momentum tech.
“WE JUST CUT CORPORATE TAXES, GAVE A BUNCH OF TAX REFUNDS, TARIFF REFUNDS. WE ARE DROPPING THE STRATEGIC PETROLEUM RESERVES AT A RECORD PACE...THE OTHER SIDE OF THE SUGAR RUSH IS THIS HANGOVER EFFECT.” ▶ 1:43:53
Matthew argues the 'strong' May jobs report is partly a World Cup sugar rush: approximately 70,000 of the new jobs were in leisure and hospitality (likely World Cup-related), plus 35,000 healthcare and 50,000 government; he calls these 'shorter-term things' that will fade, while Indeed and Linkup job opening data are already falling.
contrarian🗣 Matthew (macro strategist)conviction: medium
So what: The payrolls headline overstates structural labor market strength; if World Cup and fiscal effects reverse together in H2, the Fed's hiking bias could quickly need reversal.
“IF YOU LOOK AT THE LAST JOBS REPORT, 70,000 OF THE DRUMS WERE IN LEISURE AND HOSPITALITY. THAT IS THE WORLD CUP LIKELY.” ▶ 1:47:12
Matthew warns global equity market concentration risk is extreme: every major equity index worldwide is now essentially a technology index, with semiconductors permeating even EM, mid-cap, and value indices — making the traditional diversification trades (value, EM, international) effectively redundant.
data_point🗣 Matthew (macro strategist)conviction: high
So what: True diversification is nearly impossible within equities; meaningful risk reduction requires rotating into bonds (5%+) or real assets rather than cross-equity 'diversification'.
“EVERY EQUITY MARKET IN THE WORLD IS NOW BASICALLY A TECHNOLOGY STOCK INDEX...VALUE IS NOW SEMICONDUCTOR STOCKS.” ▶ 1:41:03
Barbara Reinhard (Voya Investment Management) recommends healthcare and financials as cheap defensive hedges — they are 'much cheaper than they have been in the past' relative to mega-cap tech — as insurance ahead of potential market stress.
thesis🗣 Barbara Reinhardconviction: mediumBarbara ReinhardVoya Investment Management
So what: Defensive rotation into healthcare and financials offers cheap optionality; buying the insurance before a storm is cheaper than reacting after.
“WHEN THE DEFENSIVE GET CHEAP ENOUGH, IT IS WORTH IT TO HAVE AN INSURANCE POLICY, AND YOU WANT THE INSURANCE POLICY BEFORE THE STORM HITS.” ▶ 57:45
Small business optimism fell to its lowest since October 2024 and consumer sentiment remains depressed, reflecting the cumulative toll of 5+ years of above-target inflation on purchasing power rather than current conditions — a sentiment-data bifurcation from strong hard economic data.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Ferro / Scarlet Fuconviction: high
So what: Soft data / hard data divergence signals underlying consumer fragility; a growth scare could materialize quickly if real disposable income continues to decline.
George Saravelos argues a potential Ukraine-Russia peace deal is an underappreciated FX variable; he stayed away from dollar-bearish views in recent months and sees the current rate dynamic (market pricing ~3 Fed hikes) as fundamentally dollar-bullish relative to the ECB's inflation-driven hiking cycle.
thesis🗣 George Saravelosconviction: mediumGeorge SaravelosDeutsche BankECBFederal Reserve
So what: Dollar positioning may be underweight relative to the rate differential outlook; EUR weakness trade could re-emerge if the ECB hikes into weakness while the Fed moves hawkish on strength.
China to Spend $295B on Nationwide AI Buildout | The China Show 6/10/2026102 min38 insights2026-06-10
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's China Show, broadcast live from Bloomberg Invest Hong Kong, covers China's reported $295B (¥2 trillion over 5 years) AI data center buildout plan, its market implications, and a wide range of live interviews with the Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan, E Fund's Global Equities CIO Jeff Li, BlackRock's Asia-Pacific head, and ChinaAMC CEO on topics spanning AI investment themes, geopolitical risks from the Iran conflict, Hong Kong's regulatory crackdown on cross-border capital flows, private markets, and portfolio construction shifts.
China $295B AI Infrastructure Buildout · 6China Macro & AI Valuation — Scarcity Trade Framework · 6Hong Kong Financial Hub — Cross-Border Regulation & IPO Pipeline · 7Middle East Conflict — Macro & Oil Market Implications · 4BlackRock Asia-Pacific: Portfolio Construction & Private Markets · 5Asia Private Markets & Emerging Tech Bets · 5ChinaAMC & ETF Market Dynamics · 5
China $295B AI Infrastructure Buildout 6
China is preparing to spend approximately $295 billion (¥2 trillion) over five years to build a nationwide network of interconnected data centers, funded primarily via sovereign debt, national investment funds, and private investors, with roughly 80% of suppliers expected to be domestic.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low (Bloomberg China correspondent)conviction: highChinaHuaweiNvidiaAMD
So what: Confirms a massive multi-year tailwind for Chinese domestic AI hardware and infrastructure suppliers; Nvidia and AMD are structurally excluded, creating a near-total Huawei-led supply chain.
“About $300 billion to create this interconnected data center network across the country, so it is pulling together the fragmented resources.” ▶ 9:10
China's annual AI data center spending under the plan (~$60B/year) is still less than one-tenth of the U.S.'s annual AI infrastructure spend of more than $700 billion, indicating a large absolute gap remains.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low (Bloomberg China correspondent)conviction: highChinaUnited States
So what: Despite the headline size, China's AI capex still meaningfully lags the U.S.; Chinese AI equity premiums should reflect this constrained but growing budget, not parity.
“$300 billion sounds like a lot, but spread over five years. Divide that by five, it is about $60 billion a year. When you consider what the U.S. is spending in a year, more than $700 billion.” ▶ 9:37
Chinese telecom operators (e.g., China Telecom) are identified as key beneficiaries of the AI buildout plan because state-run data centers will need to sell AI compute capacity cheaply, reigniting revenue growth for telcos whose core service revenue growth has already flattened.
causal🗣 Bloomberg Intelligence analyst (unnamed)conviction: mediumChina TelecomBloomberg Intelligence
So what: China Telecom and peer state telcos are a direct, lower-risk expression of the buildout versus pure-play AI names; core telecom revenue stagnation makes the AI boost proportionally more impactful.
“We think telcos can do well. We think it will allow them to sell capacity that AI services run over much more cheaply, and that should allow them to grow that sort of revenue. The revenue growth from core telecom services flattened out, so you need a sort of AI boost in order to get the top line growing.” ▶ 1:18:27
Taiwan announced it would restrict chip exports to China in solidarity with U.S. technology controls, accelerating China's push to source 80% of AI infrastructure from domestic suppliers like Huawei — indirectly benefiting the Chinese domestic chip ecosystem.
causal🗣 Anthony Stephens (Bloomberg markets reporter)conviction: highTaiwanHuaweiChinaUnited States
So what: Each new Western or allied chip restriction tightens the moat for Chinese domestic chipmakers and Huawei; investors should view allied restrictions not as headwinds for China plays but as accelerants for indigenous substitution.
“Overnight, Taiwan said they would restrict chip exports to China in solidarity with the U.S. effort. That is really lining up to benefit the Chinese tech ecosystem.” ▶ 1:01:53
Lenovo was highlighted by analysts as a beneficiary of the China AI buildout as a domestic AI server ecosystem proxy, with its shares surging nearly 5% on the day.
data_point🗣 Avril Hong (Bloomberg markets anchor)conviction: mediumLenovo
So what: Lenovo's server/infrastructure segment stands to gain direct order flow from the state-led plan; the 5% intraday move suggests the market is actively pricing this in.
≈ “Analysts also putting out a localized AI server ecosystem proxies could benefit. They highlighted the likes of Lenovo as a beneficiary today. Part of the ecosystem, you see the stocks surging almost 5%.” paraphrase
Biwin inked a nearly $2 billion deal to purchase enterprise NAND flash, signaling the supply chain for domestic AI storage is mobilizing alongside the announced buildout.
data_point🗣 Avril Hong (Bloomberg markets anchor)conviction: highBiwin
So what: Biwin deal validates that Chinese AI supply chain procurement is already accelerating ahead of formal plan approval; enterprise NAND demand will compound alongside the data center ramp.
“We got word that the findings of Biwin inked a nearly $2 billion deal to buy enterprise NAND flash.” ▶ 26:54
China Macro & AI Valuation — Scarcity Trade Framework 6
Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO) argues Chinese AI supply chain leaders are trading at ~10x earnings — not bubble territory — because markets are discounting fundamental durability, but those fundamentals will likely last far longer than consensus believes, putting us in the 'early innings of a bubble forming.'
framework🗣 Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO)conviction: highE Fund
So what: 10x P/E on fast-growing supply chain leaders is an actionable entry thesis; the market's skepticism about duration is the source of the mispricing — the trade is long duration on Chinese AI supply chain.
“If we look at the leaders, and Chinese supply chain leaders in this environment, in this market, their valuation is still very reasonable. We are talking about, you know, 10 times GDP. I would not call that a bubble valuation... By looking at the prices of certain assets, certain sector moves, I believe we could be in the early innings of a bubble forming.” ▶ 14:29
Jeff Li's primary trade recommendation is the 'scarcity trade' — overweighting AI supply chain components that are in severe shortage, from CCU to fiber/optical communication — because scarcity rotates up and down the supply chain and staying with it is the highest-probability strategy.
thesis🗣 Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO)conviction: highE Fund
So what: Investors should not just own AI broadly but track where the physical bottleneck is in the supply chain (currently optics/fiber), then rotate as scarcity migrates; the alpha comes from anticipating the scarcity shift.
“The overweight will always be with the supply chain. The point of the supply are suffering from the severe shortage... So, the scarcity will change, move around, up and down the supply chain.” ▶ 15:30
Jeff Li says what has changed the AI investment case in 2026 versus 2025 is that ROI anxiety has faded: leading AI model companies are now generating the fastest revenue growth in human history and are 'reasonably profitable,' so the ROI question is already close to resolved for many investors.
causal🗣 Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO)conviction: highE Fund
So what: The shift from ROI uncertainty to ROI visibility is the key regime change justifying higher multiples and greater capital flows into AI infrastructure; this reduces the 'what if capex doesn't pay off' tail risk.
“The leading model companies are generating huge amounts of growth. It's the fastest growing company in human history. And they are reasonably profitable. So the ROI looks, it is already close to many investors.” ▶ 16:33
Jeff Li frames China's structural shift as moving from being a 'cost/scale leader' to being an 'innovation ecosystem leader' — now producing the most chips (including AI models) and becoming the largest engineering ecosystem, with EVs, robotics, cloud, and batteries as proof points where China is now a global leader rather than a follower.
thesis🗣 Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO)conviction: highChinaE Fund
So what: Index-level Chinese earnings look flat, masking massive divergence between innovating tech companies and traditional macro-driven businesses; stock-pickers who own innovators vs. the index will see significantly different returns.
“The second largest economy have become the largest engineering ecosystem. That means the ability to innovate, commercialize, and scale up at lightning speed.” ▶ 17:41
Jeff Li estimates SpaceX and two or three big AI companies combined will raise approximately $200 billion in upcoming IPOs, which the market should be able to digest as a 'rational choice by capital' given global AI ROI visibility.
data_point🗣 Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO)conviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAI
So what: $200B+ in forthcoming mega-IPO supply is a known potential headwind to public market liquidity; confidence in digestion depends on AI ROI narrative holding — any cracks there could cause outsized IPO-supply disruption.
≈ “Some people worry about the Spacex IPO and the three big companies. They will raise something like $200 billion, which is something, hopefully digestible by the market.” paraphrase
Pre-Iran conflict, the macro environment was 'smiling' — loose liquidity and accommodative fiscal policy. Post-conflict, the macro is 'slightly tighter' due to elevated oil prices and potential Fed hikes, but not tight enough to kill the AI fundamental trade; technology fundamentals will continue to drive markets.
framework🗣 Jeff Li (E Fund Global Equities CIO)conviction: mediumE FundFederal ReserveIran
So what: The macro regime shift from easy to moderately tight means the days of rising-tide sector moves are over; alpha will concentrate in companies with earnings power strong enough to sustain despite tighter liquidity.
≈ “Before the U.S. and Iran conflict, the macro was smiling, liquid was loose, fiscal policy was accommodative. After that, now, the macro environment might be tighter.” paraphrase
Hong Kong Financial Hub — Cross-Border Regulation & IPO Pipeline 7
Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan confirmed that Hong Kong's AUM grew to exceed Switzerland's, with capital flows into various funds exceeding $400 billion USD in the prior year — a significant increase year-on-year — driven partly by Middle East and ASEAN inflows, not solely mainland China.
data_point🗣 Paul Chan (Hong Kong Financial Secretary)conviction: highHong KongSwitzerland
So what: Hong Kong's AUM leadership over Switzerland is a structural milestone that attracts more global mandates; diversification of inflows beyond China (Middle East, ASEAN) reduces single-jurisdiction concentration risk.
“Capital flow coming in. Last year, temporary flow into our various funds. Over $400 billion U.S. So it is quite a significant increase compared with the year before.” ▶ 34:28
Beijing's crackdown on cross-border trading targeted illicit outflows, not legitimate channels — Paul Chan framed it as 'cleaning house' to restore confidence with mainland authorities regarding foreign exchange reserves, arguing compliant inflows remain encouraged.
thesis🗣 Paul Chan (Hong Kong Financial Secretary)conviction: highHong KongChinaBeijing
So what: Compliance-oriented wealth managers and brokers who route capital through official Wealth Management Connect and ETF Connect channels should see no lasting disruption; firms that relied on gray-channel flows face structural risk.
≈ “Number one, the federal authorities are very supportive of Hong Kong. They want Hong Kong to succeed as an international financial center, no doubt about it. Number two, the recent crackdown mainly on those people who move their funds to Hong Kong for different investments, if the capital inflows are in the proper channel, they still are encouraged.” paraphrase
Major global banks reacted to China's cross-border crackdown with precautionary steps: one Hong Kong bank suspended new accounts for mainland clients at its Shanghai branch, some Chinese banks in Hong Kong suspended offshore accounts, UBS postponed a wealth event in China, and HSBC discouraged its Hong Kong-based private bankers from traveling to the mainland.
data_point🗣 Yvonne Man (Bloomberg anchor)conviction: highUBSHSBCHong Kong
So what: The compliance freeze at major banks suggests near-term wealth management revenue headwinds, particularly for private banking units with large Chinese HNW client bases; risk is front-loaded before policy clarity.
“UBS, for example, has postponed a wealth event in China. HSBC has talked to their Hong Kong-based private bankers, saying they are discouraging them from traveling to the mainland for now.” ▶ 41:53
Paul Chan stated Hong Kong's 2026 IPO fundraising will definitively surpass the already high 2025 bar ('of course'), with over 400 companies in the IPO pipeline and active exploration of dual listings with Korea, the Middle East, and ASEAN including ETFs.
prediction🗣 Paul Chan (Hong Kong Financial Secretary)conviction: highHong KongHKEX
So what: Continued HKEX volume growth supports exchange fees and ecosystem services; sectors noted as hot include robotics and hard tech, suggesting listing rule reforms to accommodate pre-revenue deep-tech companies are near-term catalysts.
“The ideal market, for example, the number of companies for lease income over 400 major companies.” ▶ 33:57
Chinese and Hong Kong investors were barred from participating in the upcoming SpaceX IPO due to U.S. national security concerns — the first known instance in a commercial context of Hong Kong investors being lumped together with mainland Chinese investors for exclusion from a U.S. deal.
data_point🗣 Yvonne Man (Bloomberg anchor) / Paul Chanconviction: highSpaceXHong KongChinaUnited States
So what: Precedent-setting exclusion of Hong Kong investors from U.S. critical-tech IPOs could redirect capital toward Hong Kong listings, benefiting HKEX, but also signals escalating U.S. extraterritorial application of national security designations.
“This is detrimental to U.S. interests. Why don't you welcome liquidity from all over the world?” ▶ 51:03
Hong Kong's office property market has been in managed oversupply since 2023, with the government deliberately stopping new office land sales to let the market digest excess capacity and encouraging conversion of some office buildings to student hostels.
data_point🗣 Paul Chan (Hong Kong Financial Secretary)conviction: highHong Kong
So what: Commercial real estate in Hong Kong remains a value trap as supply absorption will take years; conversion activity creates niches for student housing operators but doesn't resolve the structural office glut quickly.
“Since 2023, we stopped rolling out any office land, because the demand-supply situation. We let the market digest the oversupply of offices.” ▶ 48:25
China plans to issue ¥15 billion in sovereign bonds in Hong Kong (maturities 2028, 2029, 2031, 2036), frontloaded with ¥9 billion in 2028 and 2029, signaling continued use of Hong Kong as an offshore RMB financing hub.
data_point🗣 David Ingles (Bloomberg anchor)conviction: highChinaHong Kong
So what: Ongoing sovereign bond issuance in Hong Kong deepens the offshore RMB bond market, providing benchmark pricing for Chinese corporate issuers and reinforcing Hong Kong's fixed-income hub status.
Middle East Conflict — Macro & Oil Market Implications 4
On the day of broadcast, U.S. Central Command completed retaliatory strikes on Iranian air defense sites near the Strait of Hormuz entrance, following Iran's drone strike on the U.S. Fifth Fleet in Bahrain — the most violent period since the initial April cease-fire between the U.S. and Iran.
data_point🗣 Jill Disis (Bloomberg managing editor)conviction: highUnited StatesIranStrait of HormuzU.S. Fifth Fleet
So what: Escalation near the Strait of Hormuz is the most direct supply-risk scenario for oil; Brent at $92-93 handles suggests the market is pricing meaningful but not catastrophic risk — further escalation could spike to $100+.
≈ “U.S. Central Command has essentially put out a statement saying that these retaliatory strikes have been completed.” paraphrase
Brent crude briefly spiked to $93.20 on news of U.S. strikes completing before paring gains; prior to the Iran conflict escalation, Brent was below $90, and market positioning increasingly reflects bets on a Fed rate hike as early as September due to the conflict's inflationary pass-through.
data_point🗣 Avril Hong (Bloomberg Singapore anchor)conviction: highBrent crudeFederal ReserveIran
So what: Oil-driven CPI is repricing the Fed path from cuts to potential hikes; rising rate expectations combined with Middle East risk is creating stagflation-lite dynamics, most bearish for duration assets and EM carry.
“We know some traders have been putting in more hawkish production and some positioning for a hike as soon as September.” ▶ 3:31
JD Vance stated U.S.-Iran peace resolution could take 'weeks, maybe months,' diverging from Trump's stated preference for resolution within 'days, if not weeks' — suggesting significant internal U.S. uncertainty on the timeline and outcome of talks.
data_point🗣 Jill Disis (Bloomberg managing editor)conviction: highJD VanceDonald TrumpIranUnited States
So what: Divergent U.S. administration timelines suggest oil market uncertainty has a longer tail than the market may be pricing; any diplomatic breakthrough would be a sharp reversal trade in oil and a significant risk-on catalyst for Asia equities.
“JD Vance, Vice President of the United States, was on earlier today, saying it could be weeks, maybe months for something to ultimately be resolved here.” ▶ 7:30
Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan noted Hong Kong's energy supply is primarily renewables sourced cross-border, partially insulating the city from oil price rises, and revised the 2026 CPI inflation forecast up to approximately 2% (still 'manageable') from the original forecast of earlier in the year.
data_point🗣 Paul Chan (Hong Kong Financial Secretary)conviction: highHong Kong
So what: Hong Kong's partial renewable energy insulation reduces direct oil pass-through, but imported inflation through supply chains and the HKD-USD peg (forcing Hong Kong to mirror Fed hikes) remains a meaningful macro headwind.
“The inflationary pressure will be building up... we revise the outlook to about 2%, so it is still manageable.” ▶ 35:58
BlackRock Asia-Pacific: Portfolio Construction & Private Markets 5
BlackRock's Asia-Pacific head stated that China is the most undervalued market in Asia on a relative basis — reversing the 'China is not investable' narrative of 18 months ago — and Asian credit is the second-highest-conviction opportunity, offering high yields despite underdeveloped secondary market liquidity.
thesis🗣 BlackRock Asia-Pacific head (unnamed)conviction: highBlackRockChina
So what: BlackRock's allocation shift to China and Asian credit from a major institutional allocator is a leading indicator for broader global fund flows; secondary market illiquidity in Asian credit is the friction that creates the yield premium.
“I think China because we went 18 months ago where globally, the world was like, China is not investable. Today, we are seeing a lot more attention on China. The valuations are on a relative basis cheaper.” ▶ 1:09:53
BlackRock's Asia-Pacific head argued the traditional 60/40 portfolio cannot generate adequate retirement outcomes in Asia due to demographic headwinds and longevity risk, and investors are shifting toward a 50/30/20 construct with 20% in private markets (infrastructure and private credit).
framework🗣 BlackRock Asia-Pacific head (unnamed)conviction: highBlackRock
So what: Structural demand for private market products in Asia will grow as savings migrate from cash/property to capital markets; infrastructure and private credit managers with Asia-specific capabilities are positioned to capture this secular shift.
“Some of you may hear people say and 50/30/20, of which 20 is in private markets. There is a blend. We are seeing more and more of that whole portfolio holistic look.” ▶ 1:07:33
China's equity market delivered 6.5% annualized returns over the past 20 years — described as 'extraordinary' and implying more than 300x compounding — used to argue that remaining in cash is a definitively inferior strategy for Asian savers.
data_point🗣 BlackRock Asia-Pacific head (unnamed)conviction: mediumBlackRockChina
So what: The compounding argument is a powerful behavioral tool for shifting Asian cash deposits into equity and fund products; asset managers who can credibly present this data to retail and HNW clients in China and Asia will capture growing AUM.
“If you invested one dollar, right? Into China's equity markets over the past 20 years, he would have on average generated 6.5% per annum, ok? That is extraordinary. That is more than 300 times over the past 20 years.” ▶ 1:08:47
BlackRock's Asia-Pacific head acknowledged pockets of stress in private credit but said overall default rates remain 'very, very small' and that issues are idiosyncratic — warning investors not to overgeneralize negative press coverage of private credit into a systemic concern.
contrarian🗣 BlackRock Asia-Pacific head (unnamed)conviction: highBlackRock
So what: If the private credit stress narrative is overblown, current risk premium and secondary discounts in private credit funds may represent a buying opportunity for investors with long enough duration; the idiosyncratic vs. systemic framing is key to due diligence.
“There are pockets of issues in private credit, but it is really very idiosyncratic, and it is very unique... the default rates are still very, very small.” ▶ 1:06:13
BlackRock's Asia-Pacific head said the AI investment theme will migrate downstream — moving beyond pure infrastructure into healthcare, biotech, and other AI-enabled sectors — arguing investors should diversify into these second-order beneficiaries.
prediction🗣 BlackRock Asia-Pacific head (unnamed)conviction: mediumBlackRock
So what: Healthcare and biotech are the most frequently cited AI downstream plays; investors overweight AI infrastructure may want to begin rotating or adding positions in sector-specific AI application plays as the infrastructure trade matures.
“The AI theme will continue to play out, but it will start going downstream. Think about the downstream effect and what other segments within AI and what other sectors are going to benefit. Obvious ones will be health care, biotech, etc.” ▶ 1:11:55
Asia Private Markets & Emerging Tech Bets 5
Asia-Pacific represents 37% of global GDP but remains significantly underweighted in private market allocations by global investors, who historically prioritize U.S. then Europe before Asia — suggesting a structural catch-up opportunity in Asia private markets.
data_point🗣 Private markets panel speaker (unnamed, Bloomberg Invest)conviction: medium
So what: Systematic underallocation to Asia private markets relative to GDP share is a reversion-to-mean trade; Asia-focused private equity and credit managers should benefit as global LPs rebalance.
“AIPAC is at 37% of GDP.” ▶ 1:42:06
A private markets panel speaker at Bloomberg Invest Hong Kong identified quantum computing (potentially arriving within 3 years, not 10), nuclear energy (for AI data center power demands), and commercial space computing as the top 3-to-10-year emerging investment themes beyond AI.
prediction🗣 Private markets panel speaker (unnamed, Bloomberg Invest)conviction: mediumSpaceX
So what: Quantum computing and nuclear energy are becoming investable with near-term timelines rather than science-fiction horizons; early-stage funds or public comps with exposure to these areas merit watchlist consideration.
“One area is quantum computing. I always start 10 years later. Where I'm seeing the talent and engineering and startup that's coming within the quantum computing, it may be here in less than three years. Number two, in terms of energy, a lot of conversations about energy. Nuclear energy.” ▶ 1:38:26
Private credit is described as a superior Asian private market vehicle over pure equity because it can capture both high-growth tech companies and traditional-to-new-tech transformation companies using non-dilutive capital, and is characterized as an underserved and underappreciated asset class.
thesis🗣 Private markets panel speaker (unnamed, Bloomberg Invest)conviction: medium
So what: Asian private credit managers with expertise in corporate transformation lending can earn premium yields with lower binary risk vs. equity; especially relevant where IPO exit pipelines remain uncertain.
≈ “Private credit is a better asset if you are looking at Asia because it helps to capture not just a high companies but also the transformation that would change traditional into newtech transform companies.” paraphrase
One private equity manager reported 6 IPOs across Asian markets in the last 12 months and has 20 companies lined up for IPO in the next 18 months across Hong Kong, India, Japan, and the U.S. — signaling a robust multi-market exit pipeline.
data_point🗣 Private markets panel speaker (unnamed, Bloomberg Invest)conviction: highHong KongIndiaJapan
So what: A concrete 20-company IPO pipeline over 18 months across 4 markets is a tangible leading indicator of deal volume and secondary-market supply; markets need to remain open and stable for these exits to clear successfully.
“Across our equity funds, we've had six IPOs in the last 12 months across the Asian markets. We've achieved close to 20 companies for the next 18 months.” ▶ 1:37:39
Korean retail investors showed waning sentiment in robotics stocks after three consecutive days of large losses, suggesting that the Korea robotics trade — one of the hottest in Asia earlier in 2026 — is undergoing a significant sentiment correction.
data_point🗣 Anthony Stephens (Bloomberg markets reporter)conviction: highKoreaKOSPI
So what: Retail-driven momentum trades in Korean robotics are reverting; this may create a re-entry opportunity at lower valuations for investors with longer time horizons, or signal broader risk-off rotation from high-beta AI adjacents.
“Robotics in Korea is enduring a third straight day of really big losses, so retail is losing some sentiment.” ▶ 1:01:20
ChinaAMC & ETF Market Dynamics 5
ChinaAMC (China's largest ETF manager) launched the first fully tokenized gold fund, targeting digital asset holders seeking exposure to traditional assets rather than traditional investors seeking tokenization — a demand-side framing that positions tokenized products as an on-ramp from crypto to traditional finance.
thesis🗣 Yimei Li (ChinaAMC CEO)conviction: mediumChinaAMC
So what: If digital asset holders are the primary demand for tokenized traditional fund products, the addressable market is tied to crypto adoption growth rather than traditional wealth migration; this is a niche but growing channel for AUM.
“It is more like people who hold digital assets, and they invest in tokenized products.” ▶ 1:25:54
Mainland China's domestic retail investor migration from bank deposits to investment products (particularly ETFs) continues but remains slow, driven by regulation-compliant channels: ETF Connect, Mutual Recognition, and area/scheme Connect — all of which regulators are actively expanding.
data_point🗣 Yimei Li (ChinaAMC CEO)conviction: highChinaAMCChina
So what: The savings-to-investment migration is a secular multi-year AUM tailwind for Chinese asset managers, but it is gradual and regulatory-paced; managers with approved products in each Connect channel will benefit disproportionately.
“We have definitely seen gradual inflow from deposits into the investment side of the business, but it is kind of slow. But some investors are really getting involved with ETF's.” ▶ 1:21:27
ChinaAMC confirmed active ETFs do not yet exist in mainland China's market, though they are expected to arrive in the future, with the current innovation focused on more active-style index ETFs and leveraged products — mirroring the development arc seen in Korea and Hong Kong.
data_point🗣 Yimei Li (ChinaAMC CEO)conviction: highChinaAMCChina
So what: The upcoming approval of active ETFs in China will be a structural positive for domestic asset managers; when approved, product innovation cycles and fee pools will expand significantly in the world's second-largest ETF market.
“Definitely we are going to get active ETFs in the future, but as to the means in the local market, that will really determine when and how we are going to push that kind of product.” ▶ 1:26:48
Indonesia's central bank executed an off-cycle 25 basis point rate hike to 5.5% in a single day to arrest Indonesian rupiah depreciation, with market analysts suggesting a further 75 basis point hike next week may be needed — signaling the rupiah is in a currency crisis requiring aggressive defense.
data_point🗣 Marcus Wong / Avril Hong (Bloomberg anchors)conviction: highBank IndonesiaIndonesian rupiah
So what: Emergency rate hikes to defend the rupiah compress Indonesian growth and equity multiples; EM investors with IDR exposure face a stagflationary squeeze; defensive positions in USD-denominated Indonesian assets or shorts on IDR-sensitive banks may be warranted.
≈ “Just a day ago, 25 basis points to 5.5 percent. We also had our colleagues talking about how a 75 basis point hike next week might be needed to stay further currency depreciation in the Indonesian rupiah.” paraphrase
Gold broke its 200-day moving average and traded below $4,185 per ounce, driven by a dollar rally — interpreted as a regime change where gold is no longer acting as a geopolitical safe haven but is instead being weighed down by the stronger dollar.
data_point🗣 Anthony Stephens (Bloomberg markets reporter)conviction: highgold
So what: Gold's failure to rally on Middle East escalation is a bearish technical signal; the dollar-versus-gold relationship is reasserting itself, meaning any continued Fed hawkishness or dollar strength will pressure gold further despite ongoing geopolitical risk.
“Gold broke the 200-day moving average and continues to move lower. It seems to be bearing the brunt of the dollar rally here.” ▶ 1:02:27
Markets Weigh AI Rotation, US 'Self-Defense' Strikes | The Asia Trade 6/10/202695 min41 insights2026-06-10
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's Asia Trade (June 10, 2026) covers simultaneous shocks: US self-defense strikes on Iran threatening a fragile peace accord and spiking oil, a sharp AI/tech-led equity rotation out of crowded momentum longs creating a liquidity vacuum ahead of the SpaceX IPO, and currency stress across Asia as the yen sticks above 160 and Indonesia conducts a surprise rate hike. Underpinning all of this is a massive bifurcated AI capex buildout — US hyperscalers spending $700B+ annually vs China's state-directed $295B five-year plan — with Anthropic, SpaceX, and memory chipmakers at the center of structural debates.
US-Iran Strikes & Fragile Peace Accord · 5AI/Tech Equity Rotation & Crowded Positioning · 7SpaceX IPO & Mega-IPO Liquidity Impact · 6Asian FX, BOJ, and Rate Policy · 6AI Capex Buildout: US Hyperscalers vs China State Plan · 8Anthropic AI Safety & Business Model · 3Australia: Property, Budget, AI Infrastructure & Capital Markets · 4Malaysia Fiscal Stress from Elevated Oil & Subsidy Costs · 2
US-Iran Strikes & Fragile Peace Accord 5
The US military conducted multiple rounds of self-defense strikes against Iran on June 10, 2026, after Iran shot down a US Army helicopter patrolling the Strait of Hormuz, with a third round confirmed by Axios and reports of Iranian drone strikes on the US Fifth Fleet in Bahrain emerging during the broadcast.
data_point🗣 Jill (Bloomberg Managing Editor)conviction: highIranUSStrait of HormuzUS Fifth FleetBahrain
So what: Escalation risk to the ceasefire is material; oil markets are pricing rising probability of a wider conflict; watch the Strait of Hormuz as the key supply-disruption chokepoint.
“WHAT WE SEEN HAPPEN IN THE LAST TWO HOURS, THIS IDEA OF RETALIATORY MEASURES THAT THE U.S. IS TAKING DO RAISE A LOT OF QUESTIONS ABOUT WHETHER IT IS POSING SIGNIFICANT RISKS TO THESE ONGOING NEGOTIATIONS.” ▶ 1:03:19
JD Vance said US-Iran peace negotiations could take weeks if not months but pledged resolution before the November 2026 midterms, revealing a politically-driven timeline that Iran can exploit as leverage.
causal🗣 Jill (Bloomberg Managing Editor)conviction: mediumJD VanceIranUS midterms
So what: The US has a visible political deadline; Iran knows this and can slow-walk talks, maintaining bargaining leverage on uranium stockpiles, deal duration, and sanctions.
Key unresolved sticking points in US-Iran negotiations are: Iran's enriched uranium stockpile, the duration of any deal, and sanctions relief — while the Strait of Hormuz issue is reportedly close to resolution.
data_point🗣 Romy (Bloomberg reporter)conviction: mediumIranUSStrait of Hormuz
So what: A partial deal that reopens Hormuz but leaves enrichment unresolved is the most likely near-term scenario; oil market relief could be temporary if nuclear terms remain contested.
“THE STICKING POINTS REMAIN THE TOP ISSUES FOR A DEAL, WHICH IS THE STOCKPILE OF URANIUM, HOW LONG THE DEAL WOULD BE, AND SANCTIONS.” ▶ 4:17
Dennis Richardson, former Australian Ambassador to the US, argues that even if Iran is militarily defeated it can still win strategically, and that it is in Western interests for the US to emerge as the strategic winner regardless of how the conflict was started or conducted.
thesis🗣 Dennis Richardsonconviction: highIranUSAustraliaHamasHezbollah
So what: A strategically 'good enough' deal from a Western-alliance perspective requires curtailing Iran's nuclear program and confirming unconditional Hormuz access — the bar is higher than a ceasefire alone.
“IRAN HAS BEEN DEFEATED MILITARILY, BUT YOU CAN LOSE MILITARILY AND STILL WIN STRATEGICALLY.” ▶ 40:59
Iran's two main proxy arms — Hamas and Hezbollah — have been significantly weakened by the conflict, materially reducing Iran's ability to project power across the Middle East compared to two years ago.
data_point🗣 Dennis Richardsonconviction: highIranHamasHezbollah
So what: Reduced proxy capability limits Iran's ability to retaliate via asymmetric channels, which slightly moderates the tail risk of a broader regional conflict beyond direct US-Iran exchanges.
AI/Tech Equity Rotation & Crowded Positioning 7
The AI/tech selloff in the US session was driven by a combination of rotation into cyclical sectors (homebuilders up 4-5%, consumer durables), Iran-related oil risk, and a liquidity vacuum ahead of the SpaceX IPO — causing intraday moves with an 11% high-to-low range in key tech names.
causal🗣 Anthony (Bloomberg analyst, Hong Kong)conviction: highSpaceXNasdaqKOSPINikkeiSoftbank
So what: The 11% intraday range signals fragility in the AI trade; when broad markets rotate to cyclicals, the net-net outcome is lower overall indices because cyclical sectors are too small to absorb the money leaving mega-cap tech.
“WHAT HAPPENS WHEN THE MARKETS ROTATE AWAY FROM TECH TO CYCLICAL SECTORS, ON A NET-NET BASIS MARKETS HAVE TO GO LOWER.” ▶ 8:13
Barclays and Goldman Sachs warned that equities are vulnerable to abrupt pullbacks due to crowded positioning on the momentum trade, narrow market breadth, and higher-for-longer interest rate prospects; Goldman specifically noted the momentum long is more crowded than ever while the short side remains under-owned.
data_point🗣 Shery/Haidi (hosts, citing Barclays and Goldman research)conviction: highBarclaysGoldman SachsS&P 500
So what: A crowded long with under-owned shorts is asymmetrically positioned for a fast, sharp unwind; even a modest catalyst (e.g., hot CPI) could trigger a momentum unwind larger than fundamentals alone would justify.
JP Morgan Asset Management's Jack Caffrey is bullish on US stocks, forecasting 22%+ profit growth in 2026 and a similar rate in 2027, and expects pullbacks to be short but sharp — framing this as an earnings-driven rather than sentiment-driven market.
thesis🗣 Jack Caffrey (JP Morgan Asset Management)conviction: highJP Morgan Asset ManagementS&P 500
So what: If the earnings-growth thesis holds, the crowded-positioning risk described by Goldman is manageable and dips are buying opportunities; the key debate is whether 22% EPS growth persists into 2027.
“PULLBACKS WILL BE SHORT BUT SHARP.” ▶ 31:35
Softbank's implied volatility exceeded 100 during the session — described as 'ridiculously high for a single name' — reflecting its pivotal weight in the Japanese market and extreme sensitivity to AI sentiment.
data_point🗣 Anthony (Bloomberg analyst, Hong Kong)conviction: highSoftbankNikkei
So what: Softbank at >100 vol functions as a real-time sentiment gauge for the AI trade; positioning this large in a single name creates systemic risk for the Nikkei whenever AI enthusiasm cools.
“SOFTBANK VOLATILITY IS OVER 100, WHICH IS RIDICULOUSLY HIGH FOR A SINGLE NAME THAT IS PIVOTAL TO THE JAPANESE MARKET.” ▶ 8:47
Samsung and SK Hynix, together comprising roughly half the KOSPI's market weight, fell more than 5% in the session, while the KOSPI volatility index hit a year-long high — illustrating how single-sector concentration amplifies index risk in Korea.
data_point🗣 Shery/Haidi (hosts)conviction: highSamsungSK HynixKOSPISouth Korea
So what: Korea's equity market is structurally over-concentrated in memory chips; any rotation out of AI-adjacent semis creates outsized KOSPI drawdowns, making hedging Korea exposure via puts on SK Hynix/Samsung viable.
A wealth management strategist (Jasmine) recommends overweighting US equities via the energy sector and underweighting ASEAN, arguing that investors are underestimating the impact of elevated oil prices and that ASEAN economies are most exposed as energy importers reliant on Middle East supply.
thesis🗣 Jasmine (wealth management strategist)conviction: highUSASEANIndonesiaenergy sector
So what: Overweight US energy / underweight ASEAN is a concrete, positioned trade for a prolonged Middle East conflict scenario; Indonesia is cited as the most vulnerable ASEAN market.
“WE EXPECT THE MIDDLE EAST CONFLICT TO DRAG ON AND WE SEE A CHANCE OF OIL PRICES STAYING ELEVATED GOING INTO SUMMER.” ▶ 55:13
AI memory chip shortages are becoming a critical bottleneck for AI development, and memory chipmakers currently enjoy very strong earnings visibility with surging demand and prices; however, three risk factors loom: crowded trade, hyperscalers potentially building in-house memory, and possible reduction of memory density in models.
framework🗣 Jasmine (wealth management strategist)conviction: highSK HynixSamsungAImemory chipshyperscalers
So what: Memory chipmaker earnings are strong now but the bull case is threatened by self-supply moves from hyperscalers and model-level efficiency changes; watch for announcements from any hyperscaler about in-house memory R&D.
“THE U.S. HYPERSCALERS MAY BE SAYING WE ARE TIRED OF PAYING CHIPMAKERS HIGH MARGINS. WE WILL START TO MAKE OUR OWN MEMORY CHIPS.” ▶ 51:36
SpaceX IPO & Mega-IPO Liquidity Impact 6
SpaceX's IPO, expected to price Thursday and trade Friday at a $75 billion valuation, is set to be the largest IPO in world history, with institutional books oversubscribed multiple times over and a $22.5 billion retail tranche alone larger than all but two prior IPOs (Saudi Aramco and Alibaba).
data_point🗣 Michael (Bloomberg, San Francisco)conviction: highSpaceXSaudi AramcoAlibabaNasdaqVanguardFidelity
So what: The sheer scale of capital being absorbed creates a near-term liquidity vacuum for existing equities; passive-fund forced buying 15 days post-IPO (due to early Nasdaq index inclusion rule change) will add structural demand regardless of price.
“75 BILLION DOLLARS FOR SPACEX IS EXPECTED TO BE THE LARGEST IPO IN WORLD HISTORY.” ▶ 20:24
Nasdaq changed its index inclusion rules to fast-track SpaceX into the index, meaning passive investors will own approximately 30% of SpaceX shares as early as 15 days after the IPO — creating a mechanically forced demand event.
causal🗣 Michael (Bloomberg, San Francisco)conviction: highSpaceXNasdaq
So what: Rule change creates a date-certain price-support event ~15 days post-IPO as ETFs and mutual funds must buy in; if SpaceX opens at a premium, passive inclusion locks in overpayment risk with governance implications for index providers.
“AT 15 DAYS AFTER THE IPO, ABOUT 30% OF THE SHARES WILL ACTUALLY BE OWNED BY THOSE PASSIVE INVESTORS.” ▶ 22:16
Total IPO fundraising across all upcoming mega-IPOs (including SpaceX) is estimated at around $280 billion — equivalent to approximately two months of aggregate shareholder payouts — creating a manageable but real short-term liquidity crunch for broader markets.
data_point🗣 Jasmine (wealth management strategist)conviction: mediumSpaceXOpenAIAnthropic
So what: The $280B absorption is manageable in the context of total market flows but front-loads volatility in weeks surrounding the IPO dates; investors with concentrated AI-tech longs should consider timing exposure relative to SpaceX pricing.
≈ “THE TOTAL IPO FUNDRAISING AMOUNT, IT IS AROUND $280 BILLION U.S. DOLLARS AND THAT IS A BLEND TO ROUGHLY TWO MONTHS OF SHAREHOLDER PAYOUTS FOR SHAREHOLDERS.” paraphrase
Starlink faces a regulatory block in India because the Indian government, citing the Iran war (Iran attempted to shut down Starlink terminals), wants control over satellite communications entering the country — representing a material risk to SpaceX's largest addressable market.
causal🗣 Michael (Bloomberg, San Francisco)conviction: mediumSpaceXStarlinkIndiaIran
So what: India with its population represents one of Starlink's biggest growth opportunities; geopolitically-driven regulatory denial is not a short-term fix and is a structural overhang on SpaceX's revenue growth narrative in the IPO.
≈ “A LOT OF THE POPULATION OF THE WORLD IS THERE.” paraphrase
Chinese and Hong Kong investors have reportedly been barred from participating in the SpaceX IPO.
data_point🗣 Yvonne (Bloomberg, Hong Kong)conviction: mediumSpaceXChinaHong Kong
So what: Exclusion of Chinese/HK capital from the SpaceX IPO signals heightened geopolitical barriers to cross-Pacific capital flows in tech; this may presage exclusions from OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs as well.
“SOURCES SAY CHINESE AND HONG KONG INVESTORS HAVE BEEN BARRED FROM GETTING A PIECE OF THE IPO.” ▶ 59:48
OpenAI has confidentially filed for a public listing, meaning three major AI companies — SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic — are now all on an IPO track simultaneously, creating an unprecedented concurrent liquidity demand event in the AI space.
data_point🗣 Shery (host)conviction: mediumOpenAISpaceXAnthropicSoftbank
So what: Concurrent AI mega-IPO pipeline signals a peak-liquidity fundraising window; Softbank is notably a major OpenAI investor and its extreme volatility partly reflects this impending liquidity event.
Asian FX, BOJ, and Rate Policy 6
The Japanese yen is trading above 160 against the USD — its weakest level this quarter — while Wells Fargo analysts do not expect Japanese authorities to intervene before the Bank of Japan June 16 policy decision unless USD/JPY spikes sharply beyond 162.
data_point🗣 Ruth Carson (Bloomberg FX)conviction: highBank of JapanWells FargoJPYUSD
So what: The BOJ meeting on June 16 is the key near-term catalyst; any BOJ hike may deliver a temporary yen bounce but the structural carry dynamic will reassert — verbal intervention has lost credibility, and rate differentials still favor USD.
“WE ARE STILL SEEING DOLLAR-YEN AT 160. 162 AS A RESULT ISN'T THAT FAR AWAY.” ▶ 27:16
Japan's May Producer Price Index rose 6.3% year-over-year, beating expectations and accelerating from the prior month — its fastest pace since 2023 — reinforcing market consensus for a BOJ rate hike as early as June 16.
data_point🗣 Shery (host)conviction: highBank of JapanJapan PPIJGB
So what: A June 16 hike is increasingly priced; this PPI print also means the 30-year JGB auction faces headwinds as duration risk reprices — watch JGB yields for spillover into other Asian sovereign bonds.
“PRODUCER PRICE INDEX YEAR ON YEAR AROUND 6.3% FOR THE MONTH OF MAY. WE HAVE NOT SEEN THIS LEVEL OF ACCELERATION SINCE 2023.” ▶ 46:24
South Korean authorities are tightening surveillance of won trading — escalating from monthly to daily/weekly book inspections at major banks — to prevent speculative attacks without direct market intervention, while the KOSPI has swung more than 8% in a single session this week.
data_point🗣 Ruth Carson (Bloomberg FX)conviction: highSouth KoreaKorean wonKOSPI
So what: Heightened scrutiny may temporarily deter speculative shorts on the won but does not address the fundamental rate-differential pressure; the 1600 KRW/USD level is identified as the psychological target for bears.
Bank Indonesia conducted an off-cycle 25bp surprise rate hike to halt the rupiah's decline to new record lows, but markets responded with skepticism — bonds sold off on the news and investors termed it 'smacking of desperation', with further hikes expected at the scheduled decision the following week.
causal🗣 Marcus Wong (Bloomberg EM macro strategist)conviction: highBank of IndonesiaIndonesian rupiahIndonesia
So what: Indonesia's credibility gap — expanded central bank mandate to include growth, government-central bank interdependence, and fiscal erosion — means rate hikes alone cannot stabilize the rupiah without structural reform; bond yields are rising despite the hike.
“NO ONE WANTS TO CATCH A FALLING KNIFE.” ▶ 1:27:08
A wealth manager (Jasmine) forecasts the new US Fed chair will spend his tenure convincing the committee to hold rates steady rather than cut, with rates expected to remain unchanged through end-2027 and the 10-year Treasury yield reaching 4.5–4.8%.
prediction🗣 Jasmine (wealth management strategist)conviction: highFederal ReserveUS Treasury10-year Treasury
So what: If rates stay elevated through 2027, the duration overhang on bond portfolios persists longer than consensus expects; the 4.5–4.8% 10-year target implies further pressure on long-duration equity valuations.
≈ “WE EXPECT THE RATES TO REMAIN UNCHANGED UNTIL THE END OF 2027. WE EXPECT THE 10-YEAR TREASURY YIELD TOUCH THE HIGHER RANGE OF THE FORECAST RANGE, 4.5-4.8%.” paraphrase
Asian economic growth has been almost entirely driven by a single sector — technology — in Japan, China, and Korea, making the region especially vulnerable to a US rate-driven repricing that forces rate hikes into an uneven recovery.
framework🗣 Anthony (Bloomberg analyst, Hong Kong)conviction: highJapanChinaSouth KoreaAsiatech sector
So what: Single-sector dependency means Asian central banks face a painful choice: hike to defend currencies (risking recession in non-tech sectors) or stay accommodative (accepting currency depreciation and imported inflation).
“ASIAN ECONOMIC GROWTH HAS BEEN BOLSTERED BY ONE SECTOR, TECH, BE IT IN JAPAN, CHINA OR KOREA.” ▶ 10:53
AI Capex Buildout: US Hyperscalers vs China State Plan 8
The five largest US tech companies plan to collectively spend approximately $850 billion on AI infrastructure in the next 12 months — more than $1 trillion AUD — with over $200 billion of that infrastructure spend flowing into Australia due to its abundant land, renewable energy, and established infrastructure.
data_point🗣 Richard Wegner (CEO, Morgan Stanley Australia)conviction: highMorgan StanleyBlackstoneAustraliahyperscalersAlphabetMeta
So what: Australia is emerging as a disproportionate recipient of global AI capex; datacenter REITs, renewable energy operators, and infrastructure contractors in Australia are direct beneficiaries.
“FOUR OF THE FIVE LARGE TECH COMPANIES ARE GOING TO SPEND ABOUT $850 BILLION IN THE NEXT 12 MONTHS.” ▶ 13:48
China is preparing to spend approximately $295 billion (2 trillion RMB) over five years to build a nationwide network of interconnected AI data centers, run largely by state firms using domestically sourced AI chips — primarily from Huawei — targeting 80% domestic supplier content by 2028.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low (Bloomberg China correspondent)conviction: highChinaHuaweiAMDNvidiaTencentBaiduLenovo
So what: China's $60B/year pace is ~8% of the US private hyperscaler run-rate ($700B/year), but the 80% domestic-supplier mandate explicitly excludes Nvidia and AMD — reinforcing China's AI self-sufficiency drive and supporting Huawei's domestic chip revenue.
“THE INVESTMENT AMOUNT FROM CHINA STILL PALES IN COMPARISON TO WHAT WE ARE SEEING IN THE U.S. BUT IT IS PART OF THEIR SELF-SUFFICIENCY PUSH.” ▶ 1:22:06
US private hyperscalers are investing more than $700 billion in AI infrastructure in 2026 alone — roughly 12x China's annualized state plan — representing a structural technology investment gap that underpins US AI ecosystem leadership.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low (Bloomberg China correspondent)conviction: highUS hyperscalersChinaAI
So what: The scale differential suggests US AI infrastructure suppliers — semiconductors, cooling, power, networking — remain far better revenue-opportunity plays than their Chinese-market equivalents in the near term.
Taiwan is preparing to match US export controls on advanced semiconductor sales to China, extending restrictions beyond blacklisted entities (Huawei, SMIC) to all Chinese customers, and introducing criminal prosecution for chip smuggling for the first time.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low (Bloomberg China correspondent)conviction: highTaiwanChinaHuaweiSMICTSMC
So what: If enacted, Taiwan aligning with US controls closes a major loophole in the semiconductor embargo; China's domestic AI buildout becomes even more reliant on Huawei-level chips, widening the AI compute quality gap with the US.
“TAIWAN IS LOOKING AT STRENGTHENING THOSE LAW ENFORCEMENT TO CURB CHIP SALES, NOT JUST TO COMPANIES UNDER A BLACKLIST LIKE SMIC OR HUAWEI, BUT TO ALL CHINESE COMPANIES.” ▶ 1:23:36
Anthropic secured a $35 billion loan arrangement allowing it to lease powerful chips from Google at data centers, with Broadcom also involved in the financing — a circular financing structure that underscores the interdependencies among AI-racing tech firms.
data_point🗣 Shery/Haidi (hosts)conviction: highAnthropicGoogleBroadcom
So what: The deal signals Anthropic's aggressive infrastructure scaling ahead of its IPO; Google effectively deepens its strategic lock on Anthropic's compute while Broadcom gains a large, recurring revenue client in the AI infrastructure chain.
