Super Micro Computer announced plans to raise $7 billion through equity offerings to purchase components for AI server orders, with approximately $30–39 billion in outstanding orders — shares fell in extended trading, consistent with dilution concerns.
data_point🗣 Shery/Haidi (hosts)conviction: highSuper Micro ComputerAI servers
So what: Super Micro's large backlog validates persistent enterprise AI server demand but the equity raise signals balance sheet stress in fulfilling that demand; watch gross margin trends as component costs bite.
Tencent raised $4.7 billion from its largest bond offering since 2020, with proceeds earmarked partly for AI product and service development; Tencent has pledged to at least double its AI investments in 2026 and has $3.5 billion in debt maturing this year.
data_point🗣 Shery/Haidi (hosts)conviction: highTencentChinaAI
So what: Tencent is refinancing and simultaneously doubling AI investment — a bullish signal on its own AI competitive positioning but increasing leverage; bond investors should watch free cash flow coverage as AI capex ramps.
Naver agreed to build data centers based on Nvidia's models in a move to entrench AI dominance in Korea, even as Naver shares fell more than 7% in the tech rotation, suggesting the market is discounting near-term dilutive capex over long-term positioning.
causal🗣 Shery (host)conviction: mediumNaverNvidiaSouth KoreaAI
So what: Naver's Nvidia-standard datacenter commitment is a long-term competitive moat play in Korean AI but near-term capex is dilutive; the selloff may be a mis-pricing opportunity for investors with a 2–3 year horizon.
Anthropic AI Safety & Business Model 3
Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei argues that the software industry will get larger overall as AI develops — not smaller — but companies that fail to adapt will go out of business; the 'SaaS-pocalypse' narrative triggered by Claude's coding tools wiping $285 billion in market value overnight represents only the beginning of incumbent disruption.
prediction🗣 Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO)conviction: highAnthropicClaudesoftware industry
So what: Sector-level revenue growth in software can coexist with dramatic share redistribution; investors should differentiate between AI-native platforms and legacy SaaS incumbents that face substitution risk.
≈ “WITH AI, THE PIE IS GETTING BIGGER SO THE EXISTING INCUMBENTS MAY BE SMALLER IN RELATIVE TERMS. SOME OF THEM MAY GO DOWN IN VALUE, SOME MAY GO OUT OF BUSINESS IF THEY DON'T ADAPT IN THE RIGHT WAY.” paraphrase
Dario Amodei frames Anthropic's enterprise B2B model as a deliberate alignment of business incentives with safety values — explicitly rejecting a consumer advertising model as structurally incompatible with responsible AI development, citing the engagement-maximizing incentives of social media and AI video platforms as cautionary examples.
framework🗣 Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO)conviction: highAnthropicClaude
So what: Anthropic's enterprise focus is both a values statement and a structural competitive moat — ad-driven AI competitors face an inherent misalignment between engagement metrics and safe deployment, potentially inviting regulatory risk that Anthropic avoids.
“IF YOU PICK A BUSINESS MODEL THAT FUNDAMENTALLY CONFLICTS WITH YOUR VALUES, YOU WILL HAVE A HARD TIME. EITHER YOU BETRAY YOUR VALUES OR YOU BECOME IRRELEVANT.” ▶ 31:59
Dario Amodei stated that Claude assisted in military decision-making during the Iran war but that a human always made the final call, and that Anthropic is specifically fighting against a future where AI models make autonomous decisions unseen by humans — reinforcing their 'human-in-the-loop' red line.
thesis🗣 Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO)conviction: highAnthropicClaudeUS military
So what: Anthropic's explicit human-oversight policy and military use-case differentiation serves as a regulatory risk-management posture; as AI-in-warfare scrutiny intensifies, companies without these guardrails face reputational and legislative exposure.
“WHAT WE SEE HERE IS CLAUDE ASSIST BUT A HUMAN MAKES THE FINAL CALL. SO A HUMAN MADE THAT FINAL CALL, NOT CLAUDE.” ▶ 1:07:01
Australia: Property, Budget, AI Infrastructure & Capital Markets 4
Morgan Stanley Australia CEO Richard Wegner describes Australian M&A and IPO pipeline as at its best start in years but most transactions have been pushed to H2 2026 or early 2027 due to Iran-related uncertainty, fuel price increases, a budget with unexpected features, and two rate hikes into a pressured consumer.
data_point🗣 Richard Wegner (CEO, Morgan Stanley Australia)conviction: highMorgan Stanley AustraliaAustraliaRBA
So what: Deal activity delayed to H2 2026/2027 represents a concentrated pipeline that could release quickly once geopolitical clarity improves; M&A advisors and capital markets desks should expect a compressed but active second half.
≈ “WHILE THE UNDERLING ACTIVITY HAS BEEN REASONABLE, MOST TRANSACTIONS ARE INTO THE SECOND HALF OF 2026 STORY OR START OF 2027.” paraphrase
Australia's property market faces a multiyear structural transition following the federal budget; investors represent 40% of the real estate market and the near-term outlook is 'alarming' — Wegner expects 'damning pictures' on property in the next 6 months that will define the 2027 outlook.
prediction🗣 Richard Wegner (CEO, Morgan Stanley Australia)conviction: mediumAustraliaAustralian property marketRBA
So what: Investor-driven property downturn in Australia would affect bank loan books and consumer wealth effect; monitor auctions metrics and bank credit quality over the next two quarters.
≈ “IF YOU LOOK AT THE OPTIONS, METRICS, THEY ARE QUITE ALARMING. I WILL SUGGEST WE WILL SEE SOME DAMNING PICTURES ON THE PROPERTY MARKET.” paraphrase
Morgan Stanley Australia is allowing wealth clients to buy shares in unlisted US companies, while Australian high-net-worth investors are showing dramatically increased interest in private equity, VC, and US technology IPOs — signaling a structural shift from domestic property-and-banks to international, AI-adjacent private assets.
causal🗣 Rebecca Hill (CEO, Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Australia)conviction: highMorgan StanleyAustraliaSpaceXOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Australian private wealth is actively re-allocating from property to global AI-adjacent equities and privates; platforms offering access to pre-IPO US tech names gain competitive advantage in capturing AUM rotation.
Australian inflation is expected to remain sticky and elevated into 2026, making infrastructure assets an attractive inflation hedge within wealth management portfolios — and driving a reallocation away from financials/property.
thesis🗣 Rebecca Hill (CEO, Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Australia)conviction: mediumAustraliaRBAinfrastructure assets
So what: Sustained above-target Australian inflation makes listed infrastructure and real assets a preferred defensive allocation; banks and REITs face valuation headwinds in this environment.
Malaysia Fiscal Stress from Elevated Oil & Subsidy Costs 2
Malaysia's fuel subsidy bill surged from 700 million ringgit/month in January 2026 to a peak rate of 7.5 billion/month in April — a 10x increase driven by Iran war oil prices — though it has since fallen back to around 3.5 billion/month; this threatens Malaysia's fiscal deficit target of 3.5–3.7% of GDP.
data_point🗣 Malaysia Second Finance Ministerconviction: highMalaysiaMalaysian ringgitoil
So what: Malaysia's targeted subsidy reform provided more fiscal buffer than untargeted subsidies would have, but a sustained oil price shock could still cause the government to miss its deficit target — watch for credit rating commentary on Malaysia's fiscal trajectory.
“APRIL THERE WERE SOME WEEKS WE WERE SITTING AT A RATE OF 7.5 BILLION PER MONTH.” ▶ 1:31:20
Malaysia deliberately characterized the Iran war as a supply shock (not demand shock) and deployed 15 billion ringgit in SME loans and microfinancing rather than broad stimulus, arguing that demand stimulus into a supply-driven inflation would only worsen price pressures.
framework🗣 Malaysia Second Finance Ministerconviction: highMalaysiaMalaysian SMEs
So what: Malaysia's supply-shock framing is a fiscally disciplined approach that reduces inflation risk vs. peer EM economies; this framework, if adhered to, should support the ringgit's relative outperformance within ASEAN FX.
“THIS IS A SUPPLY CRUNCH, NOT THE DEMAND CRUNCH. PUSHING AND STIMULUS ALONG THE WAY IS ACTUALLY PUSHING MORE RISK OF INFLATIONARY IMPLEMENTS.” ▶ 1:32:56
Stocks Pare Tech-Led Drop as Rotation Gains Speed | The Close 6/9/202690 min47 insights2026-06-09
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's 'The Close' on June 9, 2026 covers a volatile session where the S&P 500 recovered from a 2.3% intraday drop to close down ~0.25%, driven by a sector rotation out of tech and chips into defensives and small caps, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index hitting -9% before recovering to -3%. Interviews with dealmakers, tech CEOs, and strategists at the Mizuho Tech Conference frame the day's action around mega-IPO supply shock (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI), an impending hot CPI print, the debut Fed meeting of new chair Kevin Warsh, and structural supply constraints in indium phosphide lasers that underpin the AI data center buildout.
Sector Rotation & Market Structure · 11Fed Policy, Inflation & Rates · 8IPO Pipeline & Capital Markets · 8AI Infrastructure & Enterprise Adoption · 7Indium Phosphide & Optical AI Supply Chain · 5Sports Finance & Stadium Economics · 4Consumer & Credit Signals · 4
Sector Rotation & Market Structure 11
The S&P 500 fell as much as 2.3% intraday on June 9, 2026, partly triggered by a Trump statement that Iran had downed a U.S. helicopter, but recovered to close down only ~0.25%, illustrating how geopolitical headlines cause sharp but short-lived dislocations.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick / Katie Greifeldconviction: highS&P 500TrumpIran
So what: The speed of recovery reinforces the view that equity markets are currently dominated by AI/earnings narrative, not geopolitics; dip-buyers remain aggressive.
≈ “THE S&P 500 CLIMBED BACK A LOT OF THE LOSSES WE SAW TODAY. AT ONE POINT DOWN BY 2.3% AFTER PRESIDENT TRUMP SAID I RAN HAD DOWNED A U.S. HELICOPTER.” paraphrase
The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index dropped as much as 9% intraday on June 9 before recovering to close down 3%, marking one of the largest single-session swings for chip stocks since Liberation Day.
data_point🗣 Norah Mulindaconviction: highPhiladelphia Semiconductor IndexNasdaq 100
So what: Extreme intraday swings in semis signal elevated fragility; a -9% intraday move in a sector that has been the primary bull-market driver warrants heightened stop-loss vigilance.
“THE PHILADELPHIA SEMICONDUCTOR DOWN 3%, AND I SAY ONLY BECAUSE AT ONE POINT IT WAS DOWN NEARLY 9%.” ▶ 0:58
The Russell 2000 closed up ~0.4% on a day when the S&P 500 fell and the Nasdaq 100 fell ~1%, with small caps up ~16% on a total return basis year-to-date versus ~8% for the S&P 500.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeld / Kevin Gordonconviction: highRussell 2000S&P 500Nasdaq 100
So what: The divergence between small caps and large-cap tech is now persistent, not a one-day anomaly; the Russell 2000's YTD lead suggests a genuine earnings-driven broadening rather than a flight-to-safety rotation.
On a day when the S&P 500 was down ~0.5%, the equal-weight S&P 500 was up ~0.5%, and 371 of 503 index members closed in the green, illustrating that the apparent index loss was almost entirely attributable to mega-cap tech.
data_point🗣 Norah Mulinda / Carol Massarconviction: highS&P 500equal-weight S&P 500
So what: Index-level losses overstate the health of the market; the median stock is performing well, making broad portfolio hedges expensive relative to targeted sector hedges.
≈ “MORE THAN 370 NAMES TO THE UPSIDE WITHIN THAT INDEX. 131 TO THE DOWNSIDE.” paraphrase
Real estate closed up 2% as a sector and was the day's top performer, with materials, healthcare, and industrials also in the green, while tech fell 1.8% as a sector and energy was the only other red sector.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeldconviction: highReal estateTech sectorEnergy sectorS&P 500
So what: The sector rotation is benefiting rate-sensitive and defensive sectors; a sustained rotation into real estate implies markets are beginning to price in rate stability or cuts rather than hikes.
Apple fell 3.6% on June 9 following a poorly received developer conference update the day before, becoming the biggest single drag on the S&P 500 on that day.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeldconviction: highAppleS&P 500
So what: Apple's developer conference disappointed investors despite the AI/tech hype cycle; monitors the risk that the AI re-rating of the Mag 7 could reverse if product pipelines disappoint.
≈ “YOUR BIGGEST WEIGHT ON THE S&P 500 WAS ACTUALLY APPLE... THAT UPDATE NOT GOING OVER TOO WELL, AND YOU CAN SEE SHARES FALLING ANOTHER 3.6% TODAY.” paraphrase
Katy Kaminski of AlphaSimplex argues that when the share of S&P 500 companies outperforming the index over a rolling 3-month period falls to roughly 20%, historically extreme reversals follow — such as the violent churn seen on Friday and Monday.
framework🗣 Katy Kaminski (AlphaSimplex Group)conviction: mediumS&P 500AlphaSimplex Group
So what: The breadth concentration indicator reaching historical extremes last week is a quantifiable signal that the correction/rotation phase has further to run; momentum strategies on tech names face mean-reversion headwinds.
≈ “FOR THE S&P 500 YOU WOULD FALL IN CLOSE TO 20% ROLLING THREE MONTH BASIS, OUTPERFORMING THE INDEX OVER THE PAST THREE MONTHS WHEN YOU GET TO THOSE EXTREMES.” paraphrase
The Amaranth CIO coined 'FOGO' (Fear Of Getting Out) to describe the current investor psychology: clients have large gains from a 3.5-year bull run (S&P doubled from 2022 lows) but are paralyzed and not taking profits.
framework🗣 Baylor (SVP & CIO, Amaranth Investments)conviction: highS&P 500Amaranth Investments
So what: FOGO is a precursor to crowded-exit dynamics; if sentiment shifts, the unwinding of large unrealized gains could produce cascading sell pressure, consistent with Citi's warning on extended Nasdaq bullish positioning.
“WHAT I AM SEEING WITH THE CLIENTS RIGHT NOW IS FEAR OF GETTING OUT, OR FOGO, WHICH IS YOU'VE ALREADY HAD A LOT OF GAINS AND FEEL YOU MAY NOT HAVE MORE TO GO IN THIS CYCLE, BUT YOU ARE NOT TAKING ANY CHIPS OFF THE TABLE.” ▶ 4:57
Citigroup strategists warned that traders are aggressively building short positions in U.S. stocks, and that despite de-risking, Nasdaq bullish positioning remains extended with most longs still in profit, making downside risk skewed toward long liquidation on any disappointment.
causal🗣 Romaine Bostick (citing Citigroup)conviction: mediumCitigroupNasdaqU.S. equities
So what: Crowded long positioning in the Nasdaq is a technical overhang; any earnings miss, CPI shock, or geopolitical escalation could trigger non-linear downside as longs are forced out.
Profit-taking ahead of the SpaceX IPO is being cited by market participants as a partial driver of tech selling, as retail and institutional investors are liquidating existing tech positions to free capital for the IPO allocation.
causal🗣 Norah Mulindaconviction: mediumSpaceXNasdaqtech stocks
So what: IPO supply-absorption risk: mega-IPOs temporarily suck liquidity out of existing tech holdings; historically this clears quickly once allocations are made, offering a tactical re-entry window.
≈ “SOME OF THIS I'M HEARING FROM SOURCES IS PROFIT-TAKING, THINKING MAYBE TECH HAS GONE TOO FAR TO THE MOON. BUT SOME PEOPLE THAT ARE GETTING PREPARED FOR THIS MASSIVE IPO.” paraphrase
Kevin Gordon (Schwab) argues the 'bottom-line risk' (corporate earnings) matters far more to equity markets than 'front-page risk' (geopolitics), and the current small-cap outperformance is specifically driven by an earnings-profile turn for that cohort.
framework🗣 Kevin Gordon (Schwab Financial Research)conviction: highRussell 2000S&P 500Schwab
So what: As long as small-cap earnings growth continues to inflect, the Russell 2000's outperformance over large-cap tech is fundamentally supported and not just a technical rotation.
“THE BOTTOM LINE RISK MATTERS MUCH MORE THAN THE FRONT PAGE RISK.” ▶ 37:36
Fed Policy, Inflation & Rates 8
The 2-year Treasury yield flipped back above the effective Fed funds rate as of June 9, 2026, a dynamic historically associated with the front end leading the Fed — as it did in the 2022–2023 hiking cycle — and the market is now 'flirting with the possibility' the next Fed move could be a hike.
causal🗣 Katie Greifeldconviction: medium2-year TreasuryFed funds rateFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: The 2-year yield inversion reversal is a leading indicator worth monitoring; if it sustains, it prices in hiking risk and could materially re-rate rate-sensitive sectors like real estate and small caps.
“TO YEAR TREASURY YIELDS TRADING ABOVE THE 4.1%, FLIPPING BACK ABOVE THE EFFECTIVE FED FUNDS RATE.” ▶ 3:18
The CPI print on June 10, 2026 is expected to show the headline rate back above 4%, a level not seen in several years, putting acute pressure on new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh at his debut FOMC meeting the following week.
prediction🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: highCPIFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: A 4%+ headline CPI print combined with a still-tight labor market boxes the new Fed Chair into a no-win scenario; watch his debut presser language closely for any signal on a new reaction function.
“MOST PEOPLE EXPECTED TO BE BACK ABOVE 4% ON THE HEADLINE NUMBER.” ▶ 7:44
The Amaranth CIO expects the Fed to adopt the 'Dallas Trimmed Mean' inflation measure (~2.3% currently), which Kevin Warsh has publicly endorsed, as the primary inflation benchmark — allowing the Fed to claim progress toward 2% even if headline and core PCE remain elevated.
prediction🗣 Baylor (SVP & CIO, Amaranth Investments)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveKevin WarshDallas Fed Trimmed MeanPCE
So what: If the Fed formally shifts to the Trimmed Mean, it provides political cover to hold rates steady and removes the market's fear of a hike; a bullish signal for bonds and rate-sensitives if confirmed at the June meeting.
≈ “I THINK WE WILL START HEARING A LOT MORE ABOUT A CONCEPT CALLED THE TREND MEAN INFLATION NUMBER... IF YOU LOOK AT THE MOST RECENT DALLAS TREND MEAN INFLATION, IT IS 2.3%. IF YOU SQUINT A BIT, YOU MIGHT SEE 2% ON THE NUMBER.” paraphrase
The Amaranth CIO favors investment-grade corporate credit at a ~6% yield as an attractive locked-in return, and argues investors should not be afraid of duration at current levels because the Fed ultimately wants to hold rates and will find reasons to do so.
thesis🗣 Baylor (SVP & CIO, Amaranth Investments)conviction: highInvestment-grade creditFederal Reserve
So what: A 6% IG credit yield is the trade this CIO is recommending as a carry opportunity with manageable duration risk; actionable for fixed-income allocation rebalancing.
“IF YOU CAN LOCK IN A 6% YIELD, WHICH IS WHAT WE GET NOW ON IG CREDIT, WE THINK THAT IS AN ATTRACTIVE ENOUGH YIELD. WE ARE OK WITH DURATION HERE.” ▶ 6:14
Kevin Gordon (Schwab) argues the bar to hike rates remains higher than the bar to cut, because the labor market does not show inflationary pressure from wage growth — the critical missing component from the inflation backdrop — keeping the Fed from committing to a hike even if CPI comes in hot.
thesis🗣 Kevin Gordon (Schwab Financial Research)conviction: highFederal Reservelabor marketCPI
So what: If correct, a hot CPI print tomorrow may not translate into significant bond selloff; the non-inflationary labor market provides a floor for bonds and limits the rate hike premium currently being priced.
“THE BAR TO HIKE RATES IS STILL HIGHER THAN THE BAR TO CUT RATES... THE FACT IT IS THE BIGGEST MISSING COMPONENT FROM THE INFLATION BACKDROP, IT IS THE BAR TO COMMIT TO A RATE HIKING CYCLE RELATIVELY HIGH.” ▶ 42:06
Katy Kaminski (AlphaSimplex) observes that energy and Treasuries have been unusually tightly linked during the U.S.-Iran conflict, both driven by the inflation theme, while equities have decoupled and are trading primarily on AI and rotation narratives.
causal🗣 Katy Kaminski (AlphaSimplex Group)conviction: highTreasury bondsoilequitiesIran
So what: The equities/bonds/energy decoupling means traditional cross-asset hedges (e.g., long bonds as equity hedge) may be less effective; investors need to separately model geopolitical risk in energy and the AI/earnings narrative in equities.
“THOSE TWO ASSET CLASSES ARE DECOUPLED IN THEIR NARRATIVES WITH EQUITIES FOCUSING ON ROTATION, A.I. BOOM AND ENERGY MUCH MORE FOCUSED ON INFLATION AND THE POTENTIAL RESOLUTION OF THE U.S.-IRAN CONFLICT.” ▶ 59:34
Core PCE has not been below 2% in over five years as of June 2026, which by almost any definition challenges the notion that elevated inflation is 'transitory.'
contrarian🗣 Baylor (SVP & CIO, Amaranth Investments)conviction: highPCEFederal ReserveCPI
So what: Persistent above-2% inflation over five-plus years structurally resets the risk premium in long-duration fixed income; the expected value of long bonds depends heavily on the new Fed Chair's policy reaction function.
“WE HAVE NOT HAD CORE PCE BELOW 2% IN OVER FIVE YEARS. THAT WOULD CHALLENGE I THINK ALMOST ANYONE'S DEFINITION OF TRANSITORY.” ▶ 8:31
Katy Kaminski (AlphaSimplex) warns that if tomorrow's CPI shows energy pass-through effects into core inflation components, that would 'spook the market even more' because it would signal a longer-lasting inflation dynamic, not just an energy one-off.
prediction🗣 Katy Kaminski (AlphaSimplex Group)conviction: mediumCPIenergyFederal Reserve
So what: Watch the CPI shelter, services ex-energy, and transportation components for pass-through signals; elevated readings there would be the catalyst for a more durable bond selloff and equity re-rating.
≈ “IF WE SEE ANY INDICATIONS TOMORROW THAT THERE IS SOME SORT OF LEAD INTO MEANING ENERGY SPIKES IS CAUSING SOME PASS-THROUGH EFFECTS, THAT IS GOING TO SPOOK THE MARKET EVEN MORE.” paraphrase
IPO Pipeline & Capital Markets 8
OpenAI has confidentially filed for a U.S. IPO, joining Anthropic and SpaceX in an AI-company IPO pipeline now worth at least $3.6 trillion in aggregate, with SpaceX expected to price that week.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXIPO
So what: The $3.6 trillion AI IPO pipeline represents the largest single-cohort IPO supply event in history; how markets absorb SpaceX will set the tone for Anthropic and OpenAI pricing.
“OPENAI HAS BECOME THE THIRD MAJOR AI COMPANY TO CONFIDENTIALLY FILE FOR A PUBLIC LISTING IN THE U.S., JOINING RIVALS ANTHROPIC AND SPACEX IN WHAT IS NOW AN IPO PIPELINE WORTH AT LEAST $3.6 TRILLION.” ▶ 9:46
SpaceX is a 25-year-old company making its IPO — the largest IPO ever — representing the first time a broad investor base (institutional and retail) can invest directly in the company and providing liquidity for long-term private holders.
data_point🗣 Head of U.S. Investment Banking, Evercoreconviction: highSpaceXEvercore
So what: SpaceX's age and scale make this unprecedented; the initial price discovery will set a valuation benchmark for all other AI/deep-tech private unicorns considering going public.
“SPACEX IS A 25-YEAR-OLD COMPANY. IT IS THE FIRST TIME A BROADER INVESTOR BASE WILL HAVE INSTITUTIONAL AND RETAIL WILL HAVE THE ABILITY TO INVEST DIRECTLY IN THE COMPANY.” ▶ 14:06
Global M&A is up 25% year-to-date in 2026 vs. 2025, which was itself a record-breaking year, making 2026 the second-best M&A year on record; the activity has been predominantly corporate-driven with private equity still on the sidelines.
data_point🗣 Head of U.S. Investment Banking, Evercoreconviction: highM&Aprivate equityEvercore
So what: Record M&A activity on top of a record prior year suggests an extended deal cycle; as private equity re-enters (expected H2 2026), M&A advisors and financing banks face sustained fee tailwinds.
≈ “M&A IS UP 25% YEAR TO DATE. COMPARED TO LAST YEAR, THE SECOND-BEST FOR M&A.” paraphrase
AI disruption is now the primary driver of M&A activity: the need to achieve scale rapidly to compete in the AI era is overriding normal caution around tariffs, geopolitics, and valuation mismatches, according to a 30-year banking veteran.
causal🗣 Head of U.S. Investment Banking, Evercoreconviction: highAIM&AEvercore
So what: AI-urgency as M&A driver is structural, not cyclical; companies that delay acquisitions risk falling permanently behind on AI capability, creating a 'buy or be disrupted' dynamic that keeps deal volumes elevated.
“THE RATE OF PACE OF CHANGE, I HAVE NEVER SEEN ANYTHING LIKE IT. I HAVE BEEN A BANKER FOR 30 YEARS IN TECH... THE NEED TO ACT, THE NEED TO GET SCALE IS A HUGE DRIVER FOR M&A.” ▶ 17:58
The Evercore banker argues that successful mega-IPOs (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI) will 'unlock' a significant amount of liquidity that cycles back into supporting the next wave of startup funding and smaller IPOs.
causal🗣 Head of U.S. Investment Banking, Evercoreconviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIEvercoreventure capital
So what: The sequencing matters: SpaceX pricing this week is the gating event; a strong debut means Anthropic and OpenAI can price aggressively, and downstream capital recycling into startups could accelerate Series B/C activity.
“AS SOON AS THESE PLAY THROUGH THE SYSTEM, THERE IS A FAIR AMOUNT OF LIQUIDITY THAT IS UNLOCKED TO SUPPORT THE NEXT WAVE OF IPO'S.” ▶ 14:29
The Mizuho Americas banking head notes that the number of publicly traded companies has halved over the last three decades, and argues that large AI-era IPOs serve both as brand-elevation vehicles and as acquisition currency for further M&A.
framework🗣 Head of Investment & Corporate Banking, Mizuho Americasconviction: mediumMizuho Americaspublic marketsIPO
So what: For acquirers, becoming a public company via IPO also unlocks stock-as-currency for deals — a strategic reason to go public even with access to private capital — watch for AI company acquisitions using newly listed shares.
“THE NUMBER OF PUBLICLY TRADED COMPANIES HAVE HALVED OVER THE LAST THREE DECADES. THE PUBLIC MARKETS HAVE ALWAYS SERVED AS A GREAT PLATFORM... TO PROVIDE ACQUISITION CURRENCY.” ▶ 10:34
80% of the current IPO pipeline for upcoming filings is comprised of AI or AI-adjacent companies, according to Evercore's analysis of pending candidates.
data_point🗣 Head of U.S. Investment Banking, Evercoreconviction: mediumIPOAIEvercore
So what: AI sector concentration in the IPO pipeline means IPO market performance becomes a referendum on AI valuations; a poor SpaceX debut would have outsized negative feedback effects on the entire pipeline.
“I WAS LOOKING AT THE POTENTIAL COMPANIES TO GO PUBLIC, 80% WERE EITHER AI OR AI ADJACENT.” ▶ 14:44
Companies taken private around 2021 at peak valuations face a 'reckoning' on exit multiples: PE sponsors must bridge the gap between 2021 entry multiples and today's market expectations before exiting, creating selective pressure on which companies get sold vs. IPO'd.
causal🗣 Head of Investment & Corporate Banking, Mizuho Americasconviction: mediumprivate equityM&AMizuho Americas
So what: The vintage 2021 PE portfolio overhang is a multi-year supply problem for both M&A and IPO markets; companies growing 15%+ with strong cash flow are the most likely to clear this hurdle as M&A targets rather than IPOs.
“SOME CAPTIVE PORTFOLIO COMPANIES THAT YOU HAVE BEEN TALKING ABOUT WERE TAKEN PRIVATE IN THE 21 TIMEFRAME. THE EXIT FOR THOSE COMPANIES MAY BE DIFFERENT THAN THE ENTRANT MULTIPLE.” ▶ 11:54
AI Infrastructure & Enterprise Adoption 7
IBM CEO Arvind Krishna claims IBM used AI tools to cut 60% of G&A costs on day one of its most recent acquisition, and can now add ~10 percentage points of profit synergy immediately rather than over the typical 1-2 year integration period.
data_point🗣 Arvind Krishna (CEO, IBM)conviction: highIBMAIM&A
So what: If IBM's 60% day-one G&A reduction is replicable, it fundamentally re-rates M&A returns on synergy targets — acquirers using AI integration tools could justify higher deal premiums and shorter payback periods.
“IN THE LAST ACQUISITION WE DID, WE MANAGED TO TAKE ABOUT OUT 60% OF THE GNA ON DAY ONE.” ▶ 25:27
IBM tripled college-graduate hiring in 2026 vs. 2025 on the thesis that AI tools make entry-level workers dramatically more productive — meaning more capacity at a lower per-unit cost — and will gain market share as a result.
contrarian🗣 Arvind Krishna (CEO, IBM)conviction: highIBMAIlabor market
So what: This is a direct counter to the AI-displaces-workers narrative: IBM is betting that AI as a productivity multiplier makes it rational to hire MORE entry-level workers, not fewer; watch for this dynamic to depress entry-level white-collar wages while boosting headcount.
≈ “ON COLLEGE HIRES, WE TRIPLED HIRING IN 2026 COMPARED TO 2025. FOR ENTRY-LEVEL POSITIONS, TRIPLED. WHY? BECAUSE MY BELIEF IS IF THESE TOOLS MAKE PEOPLE WHO ARE COMING IN AND OUT OF COLLEGE MORE PRODUCTIVE, QUICKER, THAT MEANS WE CAN GET MARKET SHARE.” paraphrase
IBM CEO predicts that within 2-3 years, Quantum Processing Units (QPUs) combined with AI cybersecurity will deliver 'incredible returns,' and he likened QPU's current state to where GPUs were in 2015 — just before the AI training explosion.
prediction🗣 Arvind Krishna (CEO, IBM)conviction: highIBMquantum computingGPUAI cybersecurity
So what: If IBM's QPU-to-2015-GPU analogy holds, the next 5-10 years could see quantum hardware demand scale as rapidly as GPU demand did — a multi-hundred-billion-dollar market creation event; IBM's fab investments position it as a key infrastructure beneficiary.
≈ “I LIKEN WHAT WE ARE DOING ON CYBER TO WHERE GPUS WERE IN 2015. EACH ERA OF TECHNOLOGY TENDS TO GO FASTER. QPU PLUS CYBER WILL GO FAST. IN THE NEXT TWO OR THREE YEARS WE WILL SEE INCREDIBLE RETURNS COMING FROM THESE TECHNOLOGIES.” paraphrase
IBM received a $1 billion federal government commitment to open a chip fab in New York focused on quantum, combined with a company-level commitment of $150 billion (referenced as '0 billion' in transcript, likely auto-caption error for $150B) toward this effort.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick (summarizing IBM CEO)conviction: mediumIBMquantum computingU.S. governmentchip fab
So what: Public-private capital co-investment in quantum chip fabs creates high barriers to entry and could replicate the CHIPS Act dynamic for classical semis; watch for IBM to secure similar arrangements with allied governments.
Uniphore CEO argues that enterprises differentiating on AI will win by using proprietary data to fine-tune open-weight small language models — not frontier LLMs — because this dramatically reduces token costs and eliminates model lock-in risk.
thesis🗣 Umesh (CEO, Uniphore)conviction: highUniphoreAI agentsLLMsmall language modelsNvidiaAMDSnowflake
So what: The tokenomics pressure is real: enterprises burning through token budgets on frontier LLMs are an underappreciated cost center; companies enabling small-model fine-tuning on proprietary data (Uniphore, HuggingFace, etc.) are positioned to capture the cost-optimization wave.
“INSTEAD OF GOING AFTER FRONTIER LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS, MOST ENTERPRISES USE CASES WILL BE SERVED BY OPEN WEIGHTS, SMALL LANGUAGE MODELS. WE ALLOW THE ENTERPRISE TO DRIVE TOKEN COSTS DOWN.” ▶ 32:54
Uniphore CEO notes that U.S. enterprises are 12-15 months ahead of European and Asian counterparts in AI agent adoption, driven primarily by Wall Street shareholder pressure on boards — not technology readiness — as the urgency mechanism.
causal🗣 Umesh (CEO, Uniphore)conviction: mediumUniphoreAI agentsenterprise AIEuropeAsia
So what: The shareholder-pressure mechanism means AI adoption speed is a function of capital market intensity — European and Asian companies with more patient capital will lag, creating a durable U.S. productivity edge and favoring U.S. AI software exposure over European.
“THE ONE REASON THAT KEEPS COMING UP IS WALL STREET. SHAREHOLDERS ARE PUTTING PRESSURE ON BOARDS AND — AND THAT IS DRIVING THE URGENCY TO MOVE FAST.” ▶ 34:18
FIS CEO Stephanie Ferris targets $3 billion in free cash flow by 2028, confirmed 2026 has started ahead of expectations, and positioned the company as the only payments provider that works exclusively on behalf of banks — giving it unique visibility into whether a transaction was executed by an AI agent vs. a human.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Ferris (CEO, FIS)conviction: highFISGlobal PaymentsWorldPayAI agentsbanking
So what: FIS's agent-transaction identification capability is a moat in the agentic AI era: as AI agents execute financial transactions autonomously, FIS sits at the authentication chokepoint; watch FCF progress vs. $3B 2028 target as a key valuation catalyst.
“WE ARE THE ONLY PROVIDER THAT WORKS ON BEHALF OF BANKS ONLY, SO WHEN THAT TRANSACTION COMES TO US, WE KNOW ON BEHALF OF YOUR BANK IT IS AN AGENT IN FACT THAT DID THAT TRANSACTION, NOT YOU.” ▶ 1:04:46
Indium Phosphide & Optical AI Supply Chain 5
Lumentum CEO Michael Hurlston confirmed that Nvidia made a 'smart strategic move' to invest in Lumentum and its largest competitor and 'corner the market' on indium phosphide — the key material for the lasers used to transmit data over fiber optic in AI data centers — locking in supply and leaving little room for other hyperscalers.
causal🗣 Michael Hurlston (CEO, Lumentum)conviction: highLumentumNvidiaindium phosphideAI data centers
So what: Nvidia's vertical supply chain cornering of indium phosphide is a structural competitive moat in AI data center connectivity; Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon face delays on their custom ASIC road maps because they cannot source sufficient InP lasers.
“NVIDIA MADE A VERY SMART, STRATEGIC MOVE TO WORK WITH US AND OUR LARGEST COMPETITOR, AND BASICALLY CORNER THE MARKET ON INDIUM PHOSPHITE... THAT'S LEFT VERY LITTLE ROOM TO OPERATE.” ▶ 1:10:20
Building a new indium phosphide fab takes three years and requires highly specialized facilities; Lumentum's North Carolina fab acquisition will take two years to come online, meaning the supply shortage for non-Nvidia customers persists until at least 2028.
data_point🗣 Michael Hurlston (CEO, Lumentum)conviction: highLumentumindium phosphideNorth Carolina fabNvidia
So what: The 2-3 year fab lead time creates a durable supply bottleneck through at least 2028; companies capable of recycling existing InP capacity (Lumentum, II-VI/Coherent) have sustained pricing power during this window.
“TO BUILD AN ENDIUM PHOSPHIDE FAB, THEY'RE COMING OUT OF THE WAFER FABS, WE HAVE VERY SMALL, SPECIALIZED ENDIUM PHOSPHIDE FABS. IT TAKES THREE YEARS.” ▶ 1:10:46
Lumentum CEO predicts an 'optical supercycle' running parallel to the AI supercycle: within five years, Ethernet copper in data center rack backplanes will be almost entirely replaced by fiber optics, representing a 'huge opportunity' for the optical components industry.
prediction🗣 Michael Hurlston (CEO, Lumentum)conviction: highLumentumfiber opticsdata centersCorningAmazon
So what: Copper-to-fiber displacement in data centers is a multi-year secular tailwind for the entire optical supply chain (Lumentum, Coherent, Corning, II-VI); the Amazon-Corning fiber deal announced the same day as emblematic of this transition.
“I THINK YOU CAN SEE A WORLD WHERE ALMOST IN THE ENTIRE BACK PLAIN HAS MOVED TO FIBER OPTICS.” ▶ 1:16:32
Lumentum CEO acknowledged revenue concentration risk around Nvidia as a key concern, noting historical parallels to the telecom boom where similar single-customer concentration led to severe downturns, but argued he is working to diversify by squeezing more non-Nvidia customers into remaining fab capacity.
contrarian🗣 Michael Hurlston (CEO, Lumentum)conviction: mediumLumentumNvidiaMetaMicrosoftAmazon
So what: Lumentum's Nvidia concentration risk is the key bear case for the stock; investors should track the percentage of non-Nvidia revenue as a diversification indicator, especially given the fiber/InP shortage that benefits Nvidia-aligned supply.
≈ “CONCENTRATION IS AN ISSUE... WE'RE REALLY THE ONLY GAME IN TOWN. SO IT PUSHES OUT THEIR ROAD MAP.” paraphrase
Nvidia's stake in Lumentum gives it both an equity return and a strategic long-term supply agreement that effectively shifts the AI optics supply problem downstream onto Nvidia's hyperscaler customers (Meta, Microsoft, Amazon) who need InP for their custom ASICs.
causal🗣 Michael Hurlston (CEO, Lumentum)conviction: highNvidiaLumentumMetaMicrosoftAmazonASICs
So what: Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon's custom AI chip (ASIC) road maps are now capacity-constrained by Nvidia's upstream InP lock-in — a non-obvious competitive risk for hyperscalers trying to reduce their Nvidia GPU dependency.
“THEY'VE PUSHED SOME OF THE SUPPLY PROBLEM DOWN STREAM TO SOME OF THE FOLKS THAT ARE THEIR PARTNERS... IT'S THOSE ASICS THAT ARE AT ISSUE AT THE MOMENT.” ▶ 1:13:24
Sports Finance & Stadium Economics 4
The FIFA World Cup 2026 is projected to generate $3 billion in revenue over 39 days across 16 cities and 104 matches, with the final match expected to draw between 1.5 and 1.8 billion viewers.

Madison Square Garden Sports (MSG Sports) stock is up over 40% year-to-date in 2026, with the Knicks' run to the NBA Finals for the first time since the late 1990s adding $450 million to owner Jim Dolan's net worth; speculation around a Knicks-Rangers spinoff into two separate public companies is a key additional catalyst.

George Pyne (Bruin Capital) argues that stadium-adjacent real estate development is among the most compelling investment cases in sports, because teams can monetize the land around a new stadium as the landlord — with the Bears' Indiana move cited as a potential multi-billion-dollar development opportunity even though the team has not won recently.
thesis🗣 George Pyne (CEO, Bruin Capital)conviction: highBruin CapitalChicago Bearssports stadiumsreal estate

“YOU CAN BUILD A WHOLE REAL ESTATE DEVELOPMENT AND YOU ARE THE LANDLORD — THE VALUE OF THAT IS AMAZING.” ▶ 1:27:25
Bruin Capital's approach to sports investing focuses on cash-generating enabler businesses (media rights, sports betting/gambling technology, virtual technology) rather than team ownership, because the higher team valuations climb, the more valuable the services ecosystem becomes.
framework🗣 George Pyne (CEO, Bruin Capital)conviction: highBruin CapitalFairPlay Mediasports betting

≈ “THE HIGHER THE VALUATIONS, THE MORE IMPORTANT AND VALUABLE SERVICES ARE. OUR BUSINESSES GENERATE CASH AND HAVE HIGH CASH FLOW CONVERSION.” paraphrase
Consumer & Credit Signals 4
JM Smucker posted its best single-day stock performance since 2008 on June 9, 2026, up ~10%, after Q4 organic sales beat expectations and the company guided to maintain a 40-45% adjusted EPS dividend payout, with plans to reduce coffee prices as input costs fall.

Cracker Barrel beat Q3 estimates significantly, reporting $0.27 adjusted EPS vs. an estimated loss of $0.47, and raised its full-year revenue forecast to $3.7 billion vs. the prior $3.24-3.27 billion consensus, signaling a consumer spending recovery at the mid-market casual dining level.

“THIRD QUARTER ADJUSTED E.P.S. COMING IN AT 27 CENTS. THERE HAD BEEN AN ESTIMATED LOSS PER SHARE OF 47 CENTS.” ▶ 51:18
The Amaranth CIO argues that the free cash flow yield on equities on a 4-year basis is ~3%, while the 10-year Treasury yields 4.5%, making the relative value case for fixed income compelling over equities at current levels.
thesis🗣 Baylor (SVP & CIO, Amaranth Investments)conviction: highS&P 50010-year Treasuryfixed income

“THE FREE CASH FLOW YIELD ON A FOUR-YEAR BASIS IS 3% WHERE IS THE 10 YEAR TREASURY YOU GET 4.5%. WE DO THINK THAT ARGUES FOR THE RELATIVE VALUE OF FIXED INCOME.” ▶ 5:29
US & Iran Trade Strikes, Testing Ceasefire Agreement | Daybreak Europe 6/10/202647 min29 insights2026-06-10
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg Daybreak Europe covers the morning of June 10, 2026: fresh US-Iran military exchanges (helicopter shootdown, retaliatory strikes) are pushing Brent above $91 and rattling global equities, while investors brace for a US CPI print expected above 4% that could force Fed rate hikes as early as September. Side stories include ASML's monopoly discount puzzle, Anthropic's dual-model launch strategy, and private credit conditions at the SuperReturn Berlin conference.
US-Iran Escalation and Strait of Hormuz Risk · 4Fed Hike Repricing and US CPI Inflection · 6Global Central Bank Divergence · 6ASML Monopoly Discount and Semiconductor Supply Chain · 3Anthropic AI Model Strategy and AI Industry Dynamics · 4Private Credit Markets and Data Center Financing · 5Asian Equity and Macro Dislocations · 1
US-Iran Escalation and Strait of Hormuz Risk 4
Iran struck 21 US naval and air targets including sites in Jordan and Bahrain in retaliation for US airstrikes on Iran after Iran downed an American military helicopter off the coast of Oman, constituting a sustained tit-for-tat exchange that challenges the existing 60-day ceasefire framework.
data_point🗣 Abeer (Bloomberg Dubai anchor)conviction: highIranUnited StatesJordanBahrainOmanStrait of HormuzCENTCOM
So what: Expanded geography of strikes (Jordan, Bahrain) signals escalation beyond bilateral US-Iran and raises regional contagion risk for energy and military assets across the Middle East.
“Iran state media came out afterwards and said they were areas that were suffering from explosions, six of them as a matter of fact.” ▶ 3:46
President Trump's stated redline is the death of American troops; both helicopter crewmembers survived, which kept the US response calibrated, but Iran's foreign minister indicated that US military presence circling Iran itself constitutes Iran's own redline.
causal🗣 Abeer (Bloomberg Dubai anchor)conviction: highDonald TrumpIranCENTCOM
So what: Dual redline structure means future casualties — even a single fatality — could trigger disproportionate escalation; risk management requires watching crew casualty reports from regional incidents.
“Iran's foreign minister came out and said it constitutes a redline for Iran as well to have American troops circling around the country.” ▶ 5:51
Pakistan is actively mediating broader truce talks between the US and Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and establish a 60-day ceasefire extension, but ongoing strikes make the framework appear fictional on the ground.
thesis🗣 Abeer (Bloomberg Dubai anchor)conviction: mediumPakistanIranUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: A diplomatic back-channel via Pakistan gives markets a potential off-ramp; any credible progress would immediately deflate the oil risk premium currently embedded in Brent at $91.
Brent crude is trading at $91.50/barrel with a 0.6% gain on the morning, driven by the US-Iran exchange of strikes, while gold is counterintuitively slipping below $4,200/oz and below its 200-day moving average due to raised inflation expectations and Fed rate-hike risk.
data_point🗣 Lizzy Burden (host)conviction: highBrent crudegold200-day moving average
So what: The divergence — oil up, gold down — reflects a market pricing inflation/rate-hike fears more heavily than safe-haven demand; gold's break below the 200dma could accelerate technical selling.
≈ “Gold slipping below $4200 an ounce, slumping deeper below the 200 day moving average on the inflation risk and the possibility of rate hikes.” paraphrase
Fed Hike Repricing and US CPI Inflection 6
The US CPI print for May is expected to come in above 4% year-on-year — the first such reading in three years — against the Fed's own prior projection of 2.7%, directly challenging the Fed's credibility on inflation.
data_point🗣 MLIV Strategistconviction: highFederal ReserveUS CPIKevin Warsh
So what: A 4%+ print would be 130bps above Fed projections, forcing a hawkish pivot in the Summary of Economic Projections and likely front-loading market pricing of hikes into September 2026.
“I think 4% is pretty hot and will be the first time in three years that we get a number like that. It will immediately call into question the Fed's credibility.” ▶ 10:04
Bond traders are piling into Fed hike bets with a September 2026 hike as the baseline, but the critical watch variable is the 30-year Treasury yield hovering near 5%; any Fed signal of tolerance for elevated inflation will push long-end investors to demand a higher inflation risk premium.
thesis🗣 MLIV Strategistconviction: highFederal Reserve30-year TreasuryKevin Warsh
So what: The 30-year near 5% is the key threshold; a dovish signal from Fed Chair Kevin Warsh would not be absorbed by the long end and would cause steepening, hurting duration portfolios.
≈ “If the Fed gives any indication that you're willing to ride this out... that fools nobody, definitely not long-term investors and they will demand a higher inflation risk premium.” paraphrase ▶ 11:02
Pilar Gomez-Bravo (MFS Investment Management Co-CIO Fixed Income) identifies a September-October 2026 confluence of risks — oil prices, rate hike pricing, and major IPOs — as the key stress period for the Fed, while current TIPS breakevens do not yet reflect a persistent inflation shock.
framework🗣 Pilar Gomez-Bravoconviction: mediumFederal ReserveMFS Investment ManagementTIPS breakevens
So what: Breakevens being calm creates a window: if oil stays elevated and core CPI tracks above 3%, the inflation repricing in TIPS could be sharp in Q3; a long TIPS / short nominal Treasury trade could benefit.
“In September and October you will have a confluence of risk factors, oil, rate hikes, having to price in more and the wonderful IPO's on the equity market.” ▶ 29:38
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has stated publicly he is more focused on the inflation print than on growth, and markets face the risk he eliminates the dot plot entirely at his first meeting, removing the primary forward-guidance anchor.
prediction🗣 MLIV Strategist / Pilar Gomez-Bravoconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reservedot plot
So what: Removal of the dot plot would increase Fed communication uncertainty and likely widen rate-path probability distributions, lifting options vol and short-tenor swaption premiums.
Pilar Gomez-Bravo expects the US 10-year yield to end the year close to its current level of ~4.53%, arguing that most rate-path volatility is concentrated at the front end where it belongs, while the 30-year at ~5.01% is the more actionable signal.
prediction🗣 Pilar Gomez-Bravoconviction: mediumUS 10-year TreasuryUS 30-year TreasuryMFS Investment Management
So what: A flat 10-year into year-end combined with a volatile front end implies curve flattening trades (long 10yr / short 2yr) are at risk; 30yr is the cleaner expression of long-duration inflation views.
“The 10 year has been surprisingly steady around 4.5%... I think you might end up surprisingly close to where you are today.” ▶ 31:08
Fixed income yields are now higher than S&P 500 earnings yields, making bonds competitively attractive vs equities on a risk-adjusted basis for the first time in years, according to Pilar Gomez-Bravo.
contrarian🗣 Pilar Gomez-Bravoconviction: highS&P 500MFS Investment Managementfixed income
So what: A bond-vs-equity yield crossover is a classic signal for portfolio reallocation away from equities; asset allocators monitoring this threshold should consider overweight fixed income as it persists.
≈ “What really matters is amazing opportunities in fixed income. Yields are higher than earnings in the S&P.” paraphrase
Global Central Bank Divergence 6
The ECB's single-mandate structure (inflation only, no employment mandate) allows it to hike two times in 2026 with a plan to cut again if growth turns sharply negative, creating a more credible and pragmatic inflation-fighting posture than the dual-mandate Fed.
framework🗣 Pilar Gomez-Bravoconviction: highECBEuropean Central Bank
So what: Two ECB hikes with a pre-committed cut option de-risks European inflation expectations; EUR-denominated IG fixed income becomes more attractive if ECB succeeds in anchoring inflation below 3.5%.
“The ECB is sanguine about how we need to hike to protect inflation expectations from going higher. Let's hike a couple times and if we do have a very negative growth in fact, we will cut rates again next year.” ▶ 31:53
The Bank of England has missed its 2% inflation target for nearly five years and risks worsening inflation further if it looks through second-round effects, with the MLIV Strategist highlighting the long end of UK gilts as the key vulnerability.
causal🗣 MLIV Strategistconviction: highBank of EnglandUK gilts
So what: UK long gilts carry asymmetric downside: if BoE stays too dovish while core inflation exceeds 4%, inflation risk premium in the long end expands sharply — a short-gilts position has positive carry in this scenario.
≈ “The Bank of England has missed its inflation target for the better part of five years now and if they look through the second round effects, that will worsen inflation in the U.K., especially at the long end.” paraphrase
Pilar Gomez-Bravo views any significant gilt yield spike in response to a potential Andy Burnham UK election victory as a buying opportunity, arguing that political fiscal space in the UK is constrained regardless of rhetoric.
contrarian🗣 Pilar Gomez-Bravoconviction: mediumAndy BurnhamUK giltsBank of EnglandMFS Investment Management
So what: A Burnham-victory sell-off in gilts would be a tactical long entry for those who believe UK fiscal arithmetic limits actual policy deviation; watch for 20-30bp widening as the trigger.
“If you do have a significant move up in yields in the U.K., we might see that as an opportunity, frankly, because the U.K. is somewhat constrained when it comes to the politics of what might be realistic when you are in power.” ▶ 34:09
Japan's PPI rose 0.9% month-on-month and at its fastest annual pace in three years, with weak demand at the 30-year JGB auction (weakest in ~1 year), reinforcing Bank of Japan rate-hike expectations and making Japanese bank stocks an outperformer vs tech within the TOPIX.
data_point🗣 Winnie Hsu (Bloomberg Hong Kong)conviction: highBank of JapanTOPIXJGBJapan PPI
So what: Rising PPI with a weak 30-year auction is the classic setup for BOJ normalization trades: long Japanese bank stocks, short JGB duration; the trend is supported by fundamental inflation data.
China's CPI came in at 1.2% for May, missing the 1.3% estimate, while PPI rose 3.9%, widening the divergence between input-cost inflation and consumer-price transmission, indicating manufacturers cannot pass through cost pressures and complicating China's reflation narrative.
data_point🗣 Winnie Hsu (Bloomberg Hong Kong)conviction: highChina CPIChina PPIPeople's Bank of China
So what: The PPI-CPI squeeze compresses Chinese industrial margins; consumer-facing A-share sectors and Chinese credit spreads on manufacturing issuers face continued fundamental pressure.
“It goes to show how manufacturers have been struggling to pass through these higher input costs to the consumers and this is also complicating the reflationary picture in China.” ▶ 9:02
Bank of Indonesia delivered a surprise rate hike to support the rupiah, sending Indonesian equities up 2.3% and making them the best-performing Asian market on the day in a session where most indices declined sharply.
data_point🗣 Winnie Hsu (Bloomberg Hong Kong)conviction: highBank of IndonesiaIndonesian rupiahJakarta Composite
So what: Surprise EM central bank rate hikes that stabilize currencies can create sharp near-term equity rallies; Indonesia's outperformance in a risk-off session illustrates the FX-equity linkage for EM investors.
ASML Monopoly Discount and Semiconductor Supply Chain 3
ASML shares are up 64% year-to-date but trade at their cheapest relative valuation in years because the company has chosen not to raise prices on its EUV machines despite holding an effective global monopoly, limiting revenue upside relative to peers benefiting from the AI chip boom.
contrarian🗣 Bloomberg Amsterdam Bureau Chiefconviction: highASMLEUVsemiconductor
So what: ASML's deliberate pricing restraint is a value-destroying strategic choice; if management shifts policy and starts monetizing the monopoly, the re-rating potential is substantial — watch for any pricing commentary in guidance.
“They've decided not to raise prices, and so it's kind of like they're sitting on this amazing tool that is the only one of its kind in the world but they are not quite capitalizing to the runway market success.” ▶ 21:35
ASML faces not only pricing restraint but also production capacity constraints; it makes extremely complex machines that take longer to ramp than hoped, though Bank of America recently indicated ASML will be able to sell more machines than the prior year.
causal🗣 Bloomberg Amsterdam Bureau Chiefconviction: mediumASMLBank of America
So what: BofA's positive supply ramp signal vs. continued valuation discount creates a potential setup; if volume growth accelerates without price increases, revenue growth is volume-constrained and multiple expansion is modest.
Within the semiconductor supply chain, capital is being allocated away from EUV lithography toward etching and advanced packaging, which are capturing a larger share of AI infrastructure spending than ASML's machines.
causal🗣 Bloomberg Amsterdam Bureau Chiefconviction: mediumASMLEUVadvanced packagingsemiconductor
So what: Advanced packaging and etching equipment makers (e.g., players in the HBM and CoWoS supply chain) are capturing the AI capex tailwind more directly than ASML; a relative trade — long packaging/etching vs. short ASML — is implied.
Anthropic AI Model Strategy and AI Industry Dynamics 4
Anthropic is launching its latest model with full safety guardrails alongside a parallel version with reduced guardrails under its 'Project Glass Wing' initiative, explicitly targeting enterprise cybersecurity use cases where it found flaws in prior models that had gone undetected for multiple years.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg Senior Strategistconviction: highAnthropicProject Glass WingOpenAIGoogleAlphabet
So what: A dual-model strategy (guardrailed consumer + unguardrailed enterprise) monetizes both market segments while addressing cybersecurity discovery as a product differentiator — relevant for enterprise security software incumbents and investors evaluating AI model moat depth.
Alphabet invested $300 million for a 10% stake in Anthropic at a $3 billion valuation; at Anthropic's implied current valuation, that 10% stake would be worth approximately $100 billion, representing a 300x return on investment.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg Senior Strategistconviction: mediumAlphabetGoogleAnthropic
So what: Alphabet's strategic minority stake in Anthropic has become one of the best-performing venture investments in corporate history; it also underpins Google's motivation to backstop Anthropic data center financing as a way to protect and extend that embedded value.
“They invested $300 million to get a 10% stake in the company at a 3 billion-dollar valuation. Based on recent reports, that means they made more than 300 times their money with that investment.” ▶ 16:08
Anthropic CEO argues that enterprise AI — focused on curing diseases, efficient energy, and pharma — fundamentally aligns business incentives with safety values, contrasting with consumer AI that maximizes engagement through advertising, which he views as structurally misaligned.
thesis🗣 Anthropic CEO (via Bloomberg Originals clip)conviction: highAnthropicAIenterprise software
So what: Anthropic's enterprise-first strategy is a differentiated positioning story vs. consumer-ad-driven AI; investors should monitor whether enterprise contract growth outpaces consumer AI adoption metrics as a valuation driver.
“If you pick a business model that fundamentally conflicts with your values, you're going to have a hard time. Either you betray your own values or they become irrelevant.” ▶ 17:11
Anthropic CEO predicts AI will make the software industry larger in aggregate, not smaller, while warning that incumbents who fail to adapt may lose significant value or go out of business entirely.
prediction🗣 Anthropic CEO (via Bloomberg Originals clip)conviction: highAnthropicsoftware industryAI
So what: The 'pie expansion' thesis is bullish for new AI-native entrants and bearish for legacy software companies without credible AI roadmaps; the key investment question is which incumbent categories face the sharpest displacement.
“With AI, the pie's getting bigger. The existing incumbents maybe smaller in relative terms, some of them may go down in value or may even go out of business if they don't adapt in the right way.” ▶ 18:38
Private Credit Markets and Data Center Financing 5
Julian Salisbury (6th Street Co-CIO) states that 6th Street's operating environment has never been better, as the stress in retail-focused semi-liquid private credit vehicles (Cliffwater, Blackstone BDCs) creates opportunity for long-duration drawdown-structure platforms that are not subject to redemption pressure.
thesis🗣 Julian Salisburyconviction: high6th StreetCliffwaterBlackstoneprivate credit
So what: Structural differentiation in private credit is widening: retail-facing semi-liquid vehicles face redemption-driven fire sales, while closed-end drawdown structures can selectively acquire assets at distressed spreads — a key LP due-diligence distinction.
“It's never been so easy.” ▶ 37:42
As the private credit industry doubles over five years from a base of historically high deployment, Salisbury expects an increasing number of defaults and headline credit events over the next two years, while noting underlying company performance remains generally healthy.
prediction🗣 Julian Salisburyconviction: high6th Streetprivate creditreal estatesoftware
So what: Rising headline defaults in private credit are likely to be idiosyncratic rather than systemic; spreads may widen on sentiment before fundamentals deteriorate, creating entry points for sophisticated credit buyers.
“When an industry grows as fast as private credit, it will double over the last five years... you will see an increasing number of defaults and headlines associated with that over the next years.” ▶ 38:42
6th Street's data center investments are outperforming internal expectations quarter-over-quarter, with capital needs spanning equity for development platforms, stabilized asset financing, and leverage on data center loans — collectively at a scale Salisbury describes as unprecedented.
data_point🗣 Julian Salisburyconviction: high6th Streetdata centersAI infrastructure
So what: The AI data center complex is generating multi-layer private capital deployment opportunities (equity / senior debt / leveraged loans); investors who can access all three layers capture diversified return profiles across the capital stack.
Salisbury identifies the core long-term challenge in data center financing as the lack of a deep liquid market for stabilized data center asset take-outs; development platforms are being built to sell at 6–6.5% cap rates, but the stabilized buyer market is only in early formation.
causal🗣 Julian Salisburyconviction: high6th Streetdata centersprivate credit
So what: Illiquid take-out markets for stabilized data centers create refinancing and exit risk for development-stage investors; the 6-6.5% cap rate assumption could prove optimistic if institutional buyer depth does not expand fast enough.
“The longer challenge will be to stabilize take-out. Ultimately, a long-term market needs to evolve for the purchasing of stabilized assets. There are early signs of that happening, but it is in the very early days.” ▶ 43:59
As AI-related IPOs generate realized returns in private markets, Salisbury expects substantial capital recycling back into next-generation growth companies, potentially sustaining the private market growth cycle beyond the current data center buildout.
thesis🗣 Julian Salisburyconviction: medium6th Streetprivate marketsAIIPO
So what: IPO proceeds recycled into private VC/growth equity creates a self-reinforcing funding cycle; monitor IPO pipeline (Q3-Q4 2026) as a leading indicator for private market LP re-commitment capacity.
Asian Equity and Macro Dislocations 1
South Korea's KOSPI fell as much as 6% intraday on June 10, 2026 — reversing a 1% gain from the prior session — making it the worst performer in Asia and dragging the MSCI Asia Pacific index down 2.5-2.6%, driven by chip stock pressure and geopolitical risk-off.
data_point🗣 Winnie Hsu (Bloomberg Hong Kong)conviction: highKOSPISouth KoreaMSCI Asia Pacificchip stocks
So what: KOSPI's extreme intraday reversal (-6% from +1% prior day) signals fragile sentiment around semiconductor names exposed to US-Iran supply-chain risk and Fed hike repricing; high volatility creates options strategies opportunities.
“It was up a percent yesterday so lots of volatility in the world's best performing stock so far this year.” ▶ 7:12
Market Call: Barry Schwartz's outlook on North American Large Cap Stocks (June 9, 2026)45 min28 insights2026-06-09
▶ Watch on YouTube
Barry Schwartz (Baskin Wealth Management) fields live stock questions on BNN Bloomberg's Market Call during a day when the Nasdaq drops 4% on a chip-stock selloff. He consistently steers away from speculative AI-adjacent plays toward high-quality, profitable businesses trading at reasonable multiples, names Meta, National Bank, and Constellation Software as top picks, and argues Canadian banks now deserve structurally higher valuations due to fee/recurring-revenue mix shifts.
AI hype bubble vs. quality compounders · 5Taiwan Semiconductor — highest-conviction semi holding · 3Canadian banks — structural re-rating thesis · 3Constellation Software — software compounder top pick · 2Proprietary data moats vs. AI disruption risk in software/info-services · 4Oil & gas — quality over commodity leverage · 3Sector-specific stock views and past-pick review · 8
AI hype bubble vs. quality compounders 5
Barry Schwartz argues AI chip stocks are in a speculative bubble analogous to the railroad and 1999 fibre booms, citing South Korean retail investors borrowing against homes to buy memory stocks and Korean markets falling 8% as evidence of crowd mania.
contrarian🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highMicronNasdaqSouth Koreamemory stocks
So what: Elevated retail-leverage signals and momentum-driven volatility in semis suggest mean-reversion risk; overweight quality over pure-play chip names.
“YOU'RE HEARING STORIES ABOUT KOREAN SOUTH KOREAN HOUSEWIVES BORROWING MONEY AGAINST THEIR HOUSES TO BUY MEMORY STOCKS IN KOREA” ▶ 1:02
Schwartz frames AI capital spending at a potential $1 trillion in 2027 as a double-edged signal: enormous demand confirms the theme but the good news may already be fully priced into chip stocks.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highAI infrastructureMicronNvidia
So what: Watch for valuation compression in semis even as capex cycle continues; crowded positioning is the risk, not the end-market.
“WE'RE LOOKING AT MAYBE $1 TRILLION SPENT ON POTENTIAL AI INFRASTRUCTURE IN 2027 ... AT SOME POINT, YOU'RE OVERPAYING FOR THE FUTURE. AND ALL THE GOOD NEWS IS PRICED IN.” ▶ 1:24
Schwartz says upcoming loss-making mega-IPOs — OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX — will be priced for perfection and will pull oxygen from profitable incumbents like Visa and Microsoft that are being ignored because they lack 'AI hype.'
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXVisaMicrosoft
So what: IPO supply of loss-making AI names is a headwind for speculative capital; creates relative-value opportunity in high-quality profitable peers.
≈ “NONE OF THESE ARE PROFITABLE. ALL OF THESE ARE GOING TO BE PRICED AS IF PERFECTION HAS TO HAPPEN. AND IN THE MEANTIME HIGH QUALITY PROFITABLE NAMES MAKING TONS OF MONEY TODAY ARE BEING IGNORED.” paraphrase
Schwartz prefers owning hyperscalers like Microsoft over pure chip plays, framing them as the railroad-builders of AI who will profit for years even if individual AI applications fail — a 'picks and shovels gold rush' framework.
framework🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highMicrosoftMicronNvidiahyperscalers
So what: Long hyperscalers with durable moats (cloud, software subscription) over cyclical hardware names as a lower-volatility AI expression.
“THE COMPANIES THAT HAVE BUILT THE RAILROADS ARE GOING TO BENEFIT FOR YEARS AND YEARS AND YEARS.” ▶ 19:00
Schwartz explicitly avoids 'adjacent AI plays' like copper miners or Caterpillar, warning that derivative exposure to AI demand is diluted and vulnerable to non-AI cyclicality; he prefers going 'right to the source' via Taiwan Semi or Nvidia.
framework🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highCaterpillarcopperTeck ResourcesHudbayTaiwan SemiconductorNvidia
So what: Avoid commodity and industrial proxies for AI demand; purity of exposure matters when demand pull-forward risk rises.
“I'M NOT REALLY BETTING ON ADJACENT AI PLAYS. IF I WANT TO BET ON AI, I'M JUST GOING TO GO RIGHT TO THE SOURCE TAIWAN, SEMI OR NVIDIA.” ▶ 29:31
Taiwan Semiconductor — highest-conviction semi holding 3
Taiwan Semiconductor returned 91% from Schwartz's September 2025 recommendation at $228 to $432 as of June 2026; Schwartz is still holding and views TSMC as 'the complete bottleneck to AI' — the single most important company globally for chip production.
data_point🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highTaiwan SemiconductorTSMCAMDNvidiaQualcommIntel
So what: TSMC's monopoly position in advanced node fabrication means any AI capex wave flows through it regardless of which chip designer wins; preferred over Nvidia for risk-adjusted exposure.
“THIS IS THE COMPLETE BOTTLENECK TO AI. THERE'S NO MORE IMPORTANT COMPANY IN THE WORLD, IN OUR OPINION, THAN TAIWAN SEMICONDUCTOR.” ▶ 21:04
Schwartz dismisses China-invasion risk for TSMC partly because China itself intends to spend $290 billion on chips over five years and has no alternative supplier — China buying from TSMC reduces the probability of military action.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: mediumTaiwan SemiconductorChina
So what: China's own chip demand creates an economic deterrent to invasion; geopolitical risk may be overstated in TSMC's valuation.
≈ “CHINA ANNOUNCED IT WANTS TO SPEND $290 BILLION ON CHIPS OVER THE NEXT FIVE YEARS ... WHERE ARE THEY GOING TO BE BUYING THEM FROM. IT'S TAIWAN SEMI. THEY'RE THE ONLY GAME IN TOWN.” paraphrase
Schwartz states TSMC provides roughly 15% of its production to China and that TSMC has five-year demand visibility baked in, supporting a long hold even after a near-doubling.
data_point🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highTaiwan SemiconductorTSMC
So what: Diversified customer base limits single-country revenue risk; five-year visibility justifies holding despite elevated absolute price.
≈ “TAIWAN SEMI PROVIDES ABOUT 15% OF ITS PRODUCTION TO CHINA ... THEY SAY THEY HAVE VISIBILITY FOR THE NEXT FIVE YEARS INTO DEMAND.” paraphrase ▶ 22:09
Canadian banks — structural re-rating thesis 3
Schwartz argues Canadian banks deserve structurally higher P/E multiples than historically because they have successfully navigated housing, mortgage, and tariff fears without credit losses, and have shifted their revenue mix substantially toward fee and recurring-revenue streams.
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highRoyal BankNational BankCanadian banks
So what: Re-rating thesis: buy Canadian bank stocks at historically elevated valuations rather than waiting for a pullback that may never come as mix-shift to fee income reduces earnings cyclicality.
≈ “I THINK THE BANKS HAVE TRANSFORMED THEIR BUSINESSES TO BE A LOT MORE FEE AND RECURRING REVENUE. AND I THINK THEY ARE WORTH POSSIBLY HIGHER VALUATIONS THAN IN THE PAST.” paraphrase
Schwartz picks National Bank (top pick) at ~15x 2027 earnings, citing its Canadian Western Bank acquisition as well-timed exposure to the recovering Alberta/oil-and-gas market and its wealth management growth potential among Western Canadian clients.
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highNational BankCanadian Western BankAlberta
So what: National Bank offers a cheaper entry into the Canadian bank re-rating story with an earnings catalyst from cross-selling wealth services to newly acquired Western Canadian depositors.
“I LOVE THE CANADIAN WESTERN BANK DEAL THAT NATIONAL DID ... THEY'LL BE ABLE TO SELL MORE WEALTH MANAGEMENT AND OTHER SERVICES TO ALBERTA AND WESTERN CLIENTS.” ▶ 41:24
Schwartz expects National Bank to deliver high-single-digit to low-double-digit total returns over five years and grow earnings at double-digit rates for a prolonged period.
prediction🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: mediumNational Bank
So what: Modest but reliable return expectation; useful as a benchmark when sizing Canadian bank positions versus more speculative alternatives.
Constellation Software — software compounder top pick 2
Schwartz names Constellation Software a top pick and calls it 'insanely cheap,' emphasising that it does not dilute shareholders with stock-based compensation and is deploying capital aggressively via software acquisitions faster than anyone expected, even at less than halfway through 2026.
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highConstellation SoftwareMark Leonard
So what: Combination of no SBC dilution, disciplined capital allocation, and contrarian M&A in a depressed software market is a mispriced compounder; watch acquisition pace as a KPI.
≈ “IT'S INSANELY CHEAP AND UNLIKE ANY OTHER SOFTWARE COMPANY, NOT DILUTING INVESTORS WITH SHARE COMPENSATION, CONTINUING TO BE AGGRESSIVE WHEN OTHERS ARE PULLING BACK.” paraphrase
Constellation is actively embedding AI services into its acquired software products while simultaneously using AI-induced fear to buy software companies at depressed prices — a dual-sided beneficiary of the AI disruption narrative.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highConstellation Software
So what: Unlike software peers at risk of AI substitution, Constellation is both adopting AI and acquiring distressed AI-fear victims; asymmetric positioning.
≈ “WHEN OTHER PEOPLE ARE SAYING AI IS GOING TO RUIN SOFTWARE, THEY'RE GOING SO QUICKLY TRYING TO IMPLEMENT AI SERVICES INTO ALL THEIR SOFTWARE, AS WELL AS TAKE ADVANTAGE OF DEPRESSED PRICES FOR SOFTWARE COMPANIES.” paraphrase
Proprietary data moats vs. AI disruption risk in software/info-services 4
Schwartz's framework for AI-resilient software companies centres on ownership of proprietary data that AI cannot access or replicate — naming Moody's, FICO (Fair Isaac), TMX Group, MSCI, and Thomson Reuters as examples of this protected-data archetype.
framework🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highMoody'sFair IsaacFICOTMX GroupMSCIThomson Reuters
So what: Screening for software/data companies whose core datasets are legally or structurally protected from AI training provides a durable filter for AI-resilient longs.
“WE THINK THE MOST IMPORTANT THING IN THE AGE OF AI IS OWNING THE PROPRIETARY DATA, HAVING DATA THAT NO AI CAN GET ACCESS TO.” ▶ 30:12
Schwartz sold Adobe earlier in 2026, citing growing competition from Canva, Figma, and Meta/Google generative-AI creative tools that could allow advertisers to bypass Adobe's products entirely.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highAdobeCanvaFigmaMetaGoogle
So what: Adobe is a short or avoid: no proprietary data moat, substitution risk accelerating as platform incumbents build native generative tools and offer end-to-end ad creation.
“EVENTUALLY THESE COMPANIES ARE GOING TO SAY, YOU DON'T EVEN NEED TO MAKE ADS. WE'LL DO IT ALL FOR YOU. SO THAT'S THE RISK WITH ADOBE. AND THAT'S WHY WE SOLD THE STOCK EARLIER THIS YEAR.” ▶ 33:56
TMX Group pulled back alongside global exchanges on news that the US government is considering allowing perpetual futures trading on prediction markets, raising fears of volume migration away from regulated options markets where TMX earns significant revenue.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: mediumTMX GroupCBOEJohn McKenzie
So what: Fear-driven pullback in TMX is a buying opportunity at ~20x earnings; monitor prediction-market regulation as the key risk variable.
“WORRIES THAT MAYBE IT WILL TAKE SOME OF THE OPTION TRADING AWAY FROM THE TMX GROUP, WHICH HAS A BIG OPTIONS BUSINESS.” ▶ 35:07
Schwartz is actively buying TMX Group at the $50 range and expects double-digit earnings growth for many years, backed by recurring infotech revenue and recent acquisition of the Canadian and Australian options business from CBOE.
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highTMX GroupCBOEJohn McKenzie
So what: Schwartz is adding at current prices, signalling a high-conviction entry point for patient investors with a 3–5 year horizon.
“WE'RE ACTIVELY BUYING IT HERE AT THE $50 RANGE.” ▶ 35:53
Oil & gas — quality over commodity leverage 3
Schwartz holds Canadian Natural Resources not for oil price exposure but for management quality, production growth of 3–5% per year, dividend growth of 5–10% per year, and its very long reserve life — explicitly stating the commodity price level is irrelevant to the investment thesis.
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highCanadian Natural ResourcesCNQ
So what: CNQ is positioned as a quality compounder within energy, not a commodity trade; suitable for investors who want oil exposure with downside protection from strong balance sheet and reserve depth.
“THE REASON WHY WE OWN CANADIAN NATURAL RESOURCES HAS NOTHING TO DO WITH THE PRICE OF OIL. IT HAS EVERYTHING TO DO WITH THE QUALITY OF THE MANAGEMENT, ITS ABILITY TO GROW PRODUCTION EVERY YEAR.” ▶ 16:25
Schwartz warns that long-term EV adoption — visible in China having 'mostly gone EV' — will structurally erode oil demand, and investors should not confuse current geopolitical-driven high prices with durable fundamentals.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: mediumCanadian Natural ResourcesChinaEV
So what: EV penetration is a secular headwind for oil demand; prefer long-reserve, low-cost producers over high-leverage names as the demand ceiling becomes clearer.
≈ “WE SEE CHINA NOW MOSTLY GONE. EV ... THOSE THINGS WONT CHANGE OVERNIGHT. BUT OVER THE LONG TERM, THAT'S GOING TO BE A HEADWIND FOR OIL AND GAS.” paraphrase ▶ 16:42
Schwartz prefers Canadian Natural Resources and Suncor over Shell because large integrated majors like Shell must perpetually acquire assets just to maintain production, whereas CNQ and Suncor have long-life oil sands reserves that don't require constant replacement capex.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highCanadian Natural ResourcesSuncorShellChevronExxon
So what: Avoid large integrated oil majors with high-depletion asset bases; favour oil-sands producers with decades of visible reserve life.
“SHELL HAS A PROBLEM LIKE A LOT OF LARGE COMPANIES IS SO BIG, IT HAS TO KEEP ACQUIRING SO MANY ASSETS JUST TO ... STAND STILL.” ▶ 27:02
Sector-specific stock views and past-pick review 8
Schwartz sold Copart at mid-$30s after noting its growth has slowed because competitor IAA (owned by Ritchie Bros.) is linked to a faster-growing insurance partner, and because record US uninsured driving rates are reducing the total flow of totaled cars into the salvage auction system.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highCopartIAARitchie Brothers
So what: Watch US auto-insurance penetration data as a leading indicator for salvage auction volume; rising uninsured rates structurally cap Copart's addressable market.
“IN THE US, BECAUSE THINGS ARE SO EXPENSIVE, FEWER PEOPLE ARE SIGNING UP FOR CAR INSURANCE ... IF YOU TOTAL YOUR CAR, THERE'S NO INSURANCE COMPANY TO SEND THE CAR TO COPART.” ▶ 12:51
Stryker is trading at roughly 20x 2027 EPS of ~$15/share with ~10% annual top-line growth; Schwartz views it as attractively valued because a cybersecurity attack that disrupted manufacturing for a few weeks has been fully resolved and full-year guidance was reiterated.
data_point🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highStryker
So what: Cybersecurity-driven sell-off in Stryker created a valuation entry point; aging demographics provide a structural demand floor for hip and knee replacements.
“WE SHOULD SEE IT DO ABOUT $15 A SHARE OF EARNINGS IN 2027. SO IT'S AROUND 20 TIMES EARNINGS FOR A COMPANY THAT'S GROWING TOP LINE NEAR 10% A YEAR.” ▶ 15:17
TFI International returned 78% from the September 2025 recommendation (bought at $130.46, now $230.72); Schwartz attributes the re-rating to an inflection in trucking volumes and US government actions restricting non-domestic truck drivers, which disproportionately benefit existing licensed carriers.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highTFI International
So what: Regulatory barriers to new driver supply are a durable tailwind for TFI and other established North American trucking operators; structural rather than cyclical re-rating.
≈ “SOME OF THE GOVERNMENT ACTIONS IN THE US HAVE RESTRICTED US DRIVERS AND CAUSING HUGE BENEFITS FOR THE EXISTING COMPANIES.” paraphrase
Meta is Schwartz's top pick at under 20x this year's earnings (~$30/share EPS), framing it as uniquely positioned because unlike Microsoft, Google, or Amazon, Meta is not selling its compute externally but using all its AI investment to directly drive its own advertising revenue growth.
thesis🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highMetaMark ZuckerbergGoogleAmazonMicrosoft
So what: Meta's AI investment is 100% internally monetised through ad-revenue acceleration, making it a purer AI beneficiary per dollar of capex than peers who must resell compute at cloud margins.
“META UNLIKE GOOGLE, UNLIKE AMAZON, UNLIKE MICROSOFT, IS NOT RESELLING ITS COMPUTE AT THE MOMENT. IT'S ONLY USING THE COMPUTE FOR ITSELF. AND YOU'RE SEEING IT IN THE REVENUE GROWTH.” ▶ 39:39
Schwartz is bearish on Bombardier at current valuations, which he characterises as pricing a rosy, uninterrupted cycle for business jets — a segment he flags as inherently cyclical and still exposed to tariff risk.
contrarian🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highBombardier
So what: Avoid Bombardier at current levels; wait for cyclical correction or tariff clarity before entering what is otherwise a high-quality service-driven aerospace compounder.
“YOU'RE PAYING A HIGH PRICE RIGHT NOW FOR STILL A CYCLICAL BUSINESS WHERE BAD THINGS CAN HAPPEN.” ▶ 37:16
MDA Space trades at 40x current-year earnings, which Schwartz calls pure hype for a capital-intensive satellite company with only double-digit revenue growth and growing competition from SpaceX and Blue Origin; he would only be attracted below ~20x earnings.
contrarian🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highMDA SpaceSpaceXBlue OriginJeff Bezos
So what: MDA Space is meaningfully overvalued relative to its growth rate and competitive dynamics; target entry under 20x EPS for a reasonable margin of safety.
“IT'S TRADING AT 40 TIMES THIS YEAR'S EARNINGS. THAT IS JUST HYPE.” ▶ 38:05
On GLP-1 pharmaceuticals, Schwartz notes that Novo Nordisk — the originator of the drug class — has seen its stock fall more than 50% as competition intensified, illustrating that first-mover status in blockbuster drugs does not protect equity value when generic/competitor entry accelerates.
causal🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highNovo NordiskEli LillyTevaGLP-1
So what: GLP-1 originator equity risk is a cautionary template: avoid pharma innovators once competitive moat is breached; prefer distribution/supply-chain plays (Cardinal Health, McKesson) over discovery-stage names.
“YOU WOULD HAVE THOUGHT NOVO NORDISK, THEY WERE THE ONES THAT CAME UP WITH THE GLP DRUG. THAT WOULD BE A HUGE WINNER. AND YOU LOOK AT THE STOCK PRICE, IT'S FALLEN MORE IN HALF.” ▶ 11:17
Gold has fallen 20% since the Iran war started and the Strait of Hormuz closure, defying consensus that geopolitical crises are bullish for gold — Schwartz uses this as evidence that precious metals prices are essentially random and unforecastable.
