data_point🗣 Barry Schwartzconviction: highgoldsilverWheaton Precious MetalsPan American SilverFranco-NevadaIran
So what: Gold's counter-intuitive weakness in an active Middle East war with Strait of Hormuz disruption is a clear signal to avoid using macro narratives to time commodity entries.
“IF YOU WOULD HAVE TOLD ME THAT THE IRAN WAR WOULD HAVE HAPPENED AND THE STRAITS OF HORMUZ WOULD BE CLOSED FOR MONTHS ... AND THE PRICE OF GOLD IS DOWN 20% SINCE THE WAR STARTED.” ▶ 7:13
Taiwan Eyes China AI Chip Sales Curbs; OpenAI Files for IPO | Bloomberg Tech 6/09/202644 min30 insights2026-06-09
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg Tech's June 9 2026 daily show covers Taiwan weighing criminal penalties for exporting advanced AI chips to China, OpenAI's confidential SEC IPO filing, and a looming $3.6 trillion wave of AI/space IPOs (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic) with analysts flagging OpenAI's weak unit economics, SpaceX's extreme dual-class governance, and historical precedents showing ~55% average first-year drawdowns for mega-cap tech IPOs.
Taiwan Chip Export Criminalisation & US-China Tech Tensions · 4OpenAI IPO: Filing Mechanics, Business Quality & Competitive Race · 7Mega-IPO Wave: $3.6T in Upcoming Listings & Historical Drawdown Risks · 6SpaceX Governance Risk & Elon Musk Voting Control · 3Apple Siri AI: WWDC 2026 & EU Regulatory Friction · 3AI Infrastructure Investment Thesis & Vertical Integration · 3US Industrial Robotics: Standard Bots & Manufacturing Competitiveness · 4
Taiwan Chip Export Criminalisation & US-China Tech Tensions 4
Taiwan is weighing new legislation that would make it a criminal offense to export advanced AI chips to China, giving Taiwanese authorities enforcement tools beyond the existing administrative measures — currently violations could only be prosecuted through document-falsification charges, not direct smuggling statutes.
data_point🗣 Peter Elstromconviction: highTaiwanTSMCChinaNvidia
So what: If enacted, this materially closes a key enforcement loophole and hardens the semiconductor supply-chain firewall; near-term negative for any entity routing advanced chips through Taiwan to Chinese buyers, and signals Taiwan's President is aligning tightly with Washington.
“They did they attain their first person because of these export restrictions a while ago, but they had to do it because of falsifying documents, not because it is a crime right now to smuggle chips out of the country.” ▶ 2:16
The Trump administration has signaled it would permit Nvidia to sell the H200 chip in China, but China is ambivalent — Beijing wants to preserve momentum behind its domestic semiconductor champions such as Huawei rather than rely on US supply.
causal🗣 Peter Elstromconviction: mediumTrump administrationNvidiaChinaHuaweiH200
So what: A potential US export-license relaxation for Nvidia H200 into China may be commercially limited by Chinese policy preference for domestic suppliers; investors should not bank on a large China revenue recovery for Nvidia.
“The Trump Administration has signaled it would allow Nvidia to sell the H 200 trips in China. China is not sure it wants that.” ▶ 3:04
China is weighing a plan to invest 2 trillion yuan (~$300 billion) in domestic data centers over roughly five years (~$60 billion/year), compared to US hyperscaler capex of ~$725 billion in 2026 alone — a roughly 10-to-1 US advantage on an annual basis.
data_point🗣 Peter Elstromconviction: highChinaUS hyperscalersdata centers
So what: China's AI infrastructure spend is a fraction of US levels; while data centers are cheaper to build in China, this gap suggests US compute superiority will persist for years, supporting the moat thesis for US cloud/AI incumbents.
“300 billion dollars works out to $6 billion a year over five years. They are aiming for $725 billion this year, the hyperscalers, so it is a 10 to 1 ratio.” ▶ 4:34
The Pentagon re-escalated concerns about Chinese tech companies' ties to the military concurrent with Taiwan's chip curb deliberations, reinforcing that US-China tech decoupling is broadening rather than narrowing despite Trump-Xi diplomacy.
causal🗣 Caroline Hydeconviction: mediumPentagonChinaUSTaiwan
So what: Investors in companies with China-linked supply chains or revenue should model continued tightening; any near-term diplomatic headline relief is unlikely to alter the structural trajectory.
OpenAI IPO: Filing Mechanics, Business Quality & Competitive Race 7
OpenAI confidentially filed an S-1 with the SEC for a potential public listing later in 2026 but has not set a timeline, framing it as optionality while it completes goals it still wants to pursue as a private company.
data_point🗣 Shirin Ghaffaryconviction: highOpenAISECSam Altman
So what: A confidential filing gives OpenAI a path to list within ~180 days; the lack of timeline commitment preserves flexibility to watch how Anthropic and SpaceX trade before committing.
PitchBook's Harrison Rolfes rates OpenAI only 4.8 out of 10 on his AI Business Quality framework across five dimensions — revenue quality, computing independence, moat durability, governance optionality, and capital efficiency — citing heavy compute costs that prevent profitability.
framework🗣 Harrison Rolfesconviction: highOpenAIPitchBook
So what: A sub-5 BQ score on a structured five-factor framework is a concrete red flag for public-market buyers; investors should scrutinize gross-margin disclosures and Microsoft revenue-sharing mechanics before buying the IPO.
≈ “When looking at OpenAI, we want to see the Microsoft turns, we want to see how the accounting works, we want to see how the enterprise revenue as a share total and their net revenue retention, the annual compute obligation and the party disclosures to really figure out exactly where they stand as a true business.” paraphrase
OpenAI's revenue-share arrangement with Microsoft is unique in that capping the obligation frees up incremental revenue, but post-2030 treatment is uncertain — that uncertainty alone makes it very difficult for public-market investors to value OpenAI beyond a four-year horizon.
causal🗣 Harrison Rolfesconviction: highOpenAIMicrosoft
So what: The Microsoft dependency creates a hard valuation wall at 2030; any DCF-based valuation is essentially a guess, making the IPO a momentum/sentiment trade rather than a fundamental one.
“It's still unsure about what's going to happen after 2030. And that uncertainty is going to make it very hard for investors to look past 2030.” ▶ 15:10
Sam Altman holds no significant equity in OpenAI, and the shift from nonprofit to for-profit structure has generated investor and board backlash; public-market investors are essentially betting on Altman's personal ambition rather than conventional founder-alignment incentives.
contrarian🗣 Harrison Rolfesconviction: mediumOpenAISam Altman
So what: Absence of meaningful CEO equity is an unusual governance risk; if Altman departs, there is no financial anchor keeping him at OpenAI — a key tail risk for long-horizon holders.
Filing last in the IPO race (after Anthropic and SpaceX) gives OpenAI the tactical advantage of reading competitors' S-1 disclosures to identify and correct weaknesses — including gross margin presentation and compute-cost breakdowns — before its own offering.
framework🗣 Harrison Rolfesconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicSpaceX
So what: OpenAI may present cleaner, better-benchmarked financials than earlier filers; investors should compare disclosures carefully rather than assuming the last filing is more transparent.
“I think by filing last actually gives OpenAI the opportunity to learn from their competitors.” ▶ 16:45
The AI lab competitive race has compressed to new model releases every few weeks (from every few months a year prior), and is simultaneous across technology, ideology (safety positioning), and IPO timing — described as the most competitive race one Bloomberg AI reporter has ever covered.
data_point🗣 Shirin Ghaffaryconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicAI labs
So what: Acceleration in release cadence signals rapidly rising R&D intensity; companies that cannot match this pace risk rapid commoditisation of their model offerings.
“It's one of the closest races I've ever covered in the tech industry, and extremely competitive right now on every level.” ▶ 12:33
Anthropic published a blog post calling for a potential pause in AI development if self-improving models — where AI systems are used to program the next-generation model — prove to be genuinely recursive, signalling the lab's view that near-term runaway improvement is a real risk.
causal🗣 Shirin Ghaffaryconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAISam Altman
So what: A public pause call from Anthropic ahead of its IPO is a double-edged sword: it differentiates on safety branding but could slow its own development pace if regulators adopt the framing.
“We saw Anthropic come out with a blog post calling for a potential pause, if this turns out to really be the case, that these models are improving so quickly and getting out of control.” ▶ 11:28
Mega-IPO Wave: $3.6T in Upcoming Listings & Historical Drawdown Risks 6
Three upcoming IPOs — OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX — represent a combined potential market cap of $3.6 trillion, roughly 10% of the Nasdaq's total market cap, which is already heavily concentrated with the top 10 names taking more than 40% of the index.
data_point🗣 Songyee Yoonconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXNasdaq
So what: Adding $3.6T to an already-concentrated index mechanically increases passive-fund exposure and correlation risk; index investors will be involuntarily overweight AI if these companies list at scale.
“If you think about the total market cap, it constitutes about 10% of the Nasdaq.” ▶ 27:53
Truist data on 30 large tech IPOs over the past 15 years shows an average maximum drawdown of 55% in the first year of public trading, as lockup expirations and insider sales add supply against retail-driven demand.
data_point🗣 Carmen Reinickeconviction: highTruisttech IPOs
So what: A 55% average first-year drawdown on mega-cap tech IPOs suggests patient investors waiting 6-12 months post-listing have historically had far better entry points — directly relevant to SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic.
“The entire group has a maximum -- an average drawdown of 55% in the first year of trading, which is incredible.” ▶ 24:30
CoreWeave is the current poster-child of AI IPO volatility: it hit a 400% gain from IPO then suffered a 65% drawdown from peak, with Magnetar offloading shares; it still trades more than 150% above its IPO price, but earnings reports caused repeated investor sell-offs.
anecdote🗣 Carmen Reinickeconviction: highCoreWeaveMagnetar
So what: CoreWeave's path illustrates the extreme choppiness pattern: early hyperbolic gains, insider selling at peaks, earnings disappointment-driven resets; expect similar dynamics for SpaceX and OpenAI.
“Coreweave was the poster child of extreme volatility. It hit the threshold again in March and it was up 400% at one point and then had a drawdown of 65 percent from that point.” ▶ 25:31
Circular dealmaking — where Anthropic buys compute from SpaceX, and SpaceX sells compute to Microsoft and Alphabet — creates related-party revenue loops that will enter the public markets simultaneously, a dynamic one analyst explicitly flagged as a 'bubble element.'
contrarian🗣 Songyee Yoonconviction: mediumAnthropicSpaceXMicrosoftAlphabet
So what: Investors should scrutinise S-1 related-party disclosures carefully; circular revenue inflation could flatter top-line growth metrics for multiple filers simultaneously.
“That is one of the reasons why people point out that there is a bubble element in today's AI marketplace.” ▶ 28:40
Meta (then Facebook) fell 30% in its first year post-IPO; Tesla is up approximately 25,000% since listing — illustrating the extreme dispersion of long-term outcomes and the importance of business-model evolution rather than entry-day price.
data_point🗣 Carmen Reinickeconviction: highMetaTesla
So what: The Tesla/Meta comparison argues for holding quality AI names through first-year drawdowns rather than trading them, but only if the underlying business model has genuine durable revenue — the key question for OpenAI given its compute costs.
Bank of America was telling clients the day of the broadcast that there are too many red flags in big tech valuations and to take profits, coinciding with a 1,000-point intraday swing on the Nasdaq 100 (from ~29,800 to ~28,800) driven by selling in Apple, Nvidia, Micron, and Tesla.
data_point🗣 Caroline Hydeconviction: mediumBank of AmericaNasdaq 100AppleNvidiaMicronTesla
So what: BofA's public sell signal combined with a 1,000-point intraday swing suggests institutional de-risking is already under way ahead of the IPO supply wave; near-term technical conditions are deteriorating.
“Bank of America have been saying to clients, too many red flags. Take profits while you can.” ▶ 43:41
SpaceX Governance Risk & Elon Musk Voting Control 3
SpaceX's IPO structure gives Elon Musk roughly 80% of voting rights, which the New York City Controller described as 'a new level of disregard for regular shareholders' rights' and 'way beyond' anything seen from other founder-controlled public companies.
data_point🗣 New York City Controller (Brad Lander, via reporter)conviction: highSpaceXElon MuskNew York City pension fund
So what: An 80% voting bloc makes SpaceX effectively ungovernable by outside shareholders; ESG-mandated institutions, and any fund with a one-share-one-vote policy, face a binary choice to exclude or to accept structural subordination.
≈ “He understands that we're in an era of founders wanting more control, but that what Musk is planning with SpaceX is way beyond what we've seen.” paraphrase
Pension funds in Denmark and the UK have stated they will sit out the SpaceX IPO and adjust their funds to avoid SpaceX exposure, with at least one UK-focused investor calling the governance structure 'catastrophic.'
data_point🗣 Reporter (unnamed)conviction: highSpaceXDenmark pension fundsUK pension fundsElon Musk
So what: European institutional exclusions will constrain SpaceX's free float and reduce index inclusion weights over time, concentrating ownership among US retail and growth-oriented funds and potentially increasing volatility.
“One U.K.-focused investor specifically said that they called the governance structure catastrophic and that it would not be prudent to have their investors exposed to it.” ▶ 42:19
The New York City pension fund's preferred approach is engagement rather than divestment — the Controller co-signed a letter to Musk requesting dialogue — but has received no response, and excluding a single company would itself be unprecedented for the fund's governance structure.
causal🗣 New York City Controller (via reporter)conviction: highSpaceXNew York City pension fundElon MuskNew York State pension fund
So what: The fund will likely be forced to hold SpaceX as an index constituent regardless of governance concerns, providing a floor of passive buying — but the lack of Musk engagement also signals he is not interested in standard institutional shareholder relations.
Apple Siri AI: WWDC 2026 & EU Regulatory Friction 3
Apple at WWDC 2026 unveiled iOS 27 with a rebuilt Siri AI leveraging new underlying LLM techniques; initial developer-beta response has been 'mostly positive,' with Siri moving from 'completely subpar to completely adequate' — not a leap ahead of Google, OpenAI, or Anthropic, but functional enough for daily use.
data_point🗣 Mark Gurmanconviction: highAppleSiriiOS 27WWDCGoogleOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Apple's Siri upgrade eliminates the 'zero' baseline but does not create a competitive AI moat; the risk is that a built-in adequate LLM cannibalises ChatGPT and Anthropic's consumer distribution on the iOS platform.
“What Apple's doing here is they're going from completely subpar to completely adequate.” ▶ 19:09
Apple is in an open dispute with the EU over Siri AI's rollout timeline; the EU's interoperability requirements are delaying EU users from accessing the new Siri features, following a similar pattern with Apple Intelligence last year — with Apple publicly blaming EU rules rather than its own development timeline.
causal🗣 Mark Gurmanconviction: highAppleEUSiri AIApple Intelligence
So what: EU regulatory overhang continues to fragment Apple's product launches geographically, delaying monetisation of AI features across ~450 million EU users and creating an ongoing source of negative headlines.
Apple's foldable iPhone is expected to launch in September 2026; iOS 27 contains multiple hints pointing to the device, which Gurman described as a significant product debut he has been tracking for an extended period.
prediction🗣 Mark Gurmanconviction: highApplefoldable iPhoneiOS 27
So what: A September foldable iPhone launch would be a hardware catalyst for Apple shares heading into Q4 earnings; watch iOS 27 developer beta leaks for confirmed foldable-specific APIs.
≈ “The foldable phone is coming in September. We've been talking about this for a while. iOS 27 is chalk full of hints to this.” paraphrase
AI Infrastructure Investment Thesis & Vertical Integration 3
AllianceBernstein's Lei Qiu argues that rapid token-utilisation growth in 2026 validates the massive hyperscaler capex cycle, viewing it as 'building a road to somewhere' with early adoption signs justifying the spend.
thesis🗣 Lei Qiuconviction: highAllianceBernsteinhyperscalers
So what: A credible institutional voice endorsing the capex cycle as demand-justified reduces the risk of a sudden capex cut; positive for data-center REITs, power infrastructure, and AI accelerator suppliers.
“The fact that tokens is being utilized and adopted at such a rapid pace is a good sign that AI is being adopted, is being used.” ▶ 8:19
Lei Qiu at AllianceBernstein argues that vertical integration — owning compute, models, and applications — is a structurally compelling investment angle in the current capital-intensive AI cycle, citing SpaceX as the clearest example, and noting that vertical integration historically makes positions hard to replicate.
thesis🗣 Lei Qiuconviction: mediumAllianceBernsteinSpaceXMicrosoftAlphabetAnthropic
So what: Investors should give a premium to AI companies pursuing full-stack vertical integration (compute + model + application); fragmented pure-play application companies face greater commoditisation risk.
“Vertical integration is something very interesting that is worth considering from an investment perspective.” ▶ 7:34
Elon Musk revealed SpaceX plans to build an AI data-center satellite network as part of a ~1 million satellite constellation to perform complex AI computing in Earth orbit, alongside a joint chip-manufacturing facility with Tesla targeting US-based semiconductor production.
data_point🗣 Caroline Hyde (narrating Musk's presentation)conviction: highSpaceXElon MuskTeslaStarshipxAI
So what: In-orbit AI compute and domestic chip fabrication with Tesla are long-duration bets that, if successful, would give SpaceX a unique cost and latency profile unavailable to terrestrial competitors — but both are years from revenue.
US Industrial Robotics: Standard Bots & Manufacturing Competitiveness 4
Standard Bots raised $200 million at a $1 billion valuation to quadruple its manufacturing footprint (currently 70,000 sq ft outside NYC) and expand R&D; it claims to be America's largest AI-native industrial robot manufacturer with robots deployed in every US state.
data_point🗣 Evan Beardconviction: highStandard BotsEvan Beard
So what: A $200M raise at $1B for an industrial robotics company with actual production deployments (not pilots) signals that capital is flowing into real-world AI automation at scale, not just software models.
Standard Bots claims 85% of its deployed robots are doing active production work — not pilots — across aerospace, oil and gas, data centers, and small/medium manufacturers, differentiating it from robotics firms still in commercial testing phases.
data_point🗣 Evan Beardconviction: highStandard Bots
So what: Production-rate deployment at 85% is the key distinction between a revenue-generating robotics business and a demo-stage company; this is the metric to benchmark against public comps like ABB, Fanuc, and Teradyne.
“85% of our robots are doing production work. So these are not pilots, these are not just testing things out.” ▶ 35:48
Standard Bots uses demonstration-based AI training — workers physically show the robot a task using a handheld device — eliminating the need for expert programmers and allowing average assembly workers to train and redeploy robots, which enables automation of jobs that were previously too variable to automate.
framework🗣 Evan Beardconviction: highStandard Bots
So what: Democratised robot programming lowers adoption barriers for SME manufacturers and accelerates the total addressable market expansion; this is the key moat to evaluate against Nvidia's broader physical-AI push.
“You show them what to do. We have a hand held device for that and you can perform the task and you don't have to be an expert.” ▶ 36:52
Standard Bots' CEO told Congress that US manufacturing is currently 5-10x more expensive than China and other countries, framing AI-native industrial robots as the foundational technology needed to restore American manufacturing competitiveness and justify tariff policy.
causal🗣 Evan Beardconviction: highStandard BotsUS manufacturingChina
So what: The 5-10x cost gap frames robotics not as productivity enhancement but as existential necessity for US industrial policy; this narrative is likely to attract government contracts and potentially tax incentives, creating a policy-driven tailwind.
“If you look at the United States against China and other countries it, can be five to 10X cheaper to make your parts in those countries.” ▶ 36:08

Macro Voices · Erik Townsend 2 episode(s) · 83 insights

MacroVoices #534 Dr. Pippa Malmgren: Superpower War or Superpower Hug?110 min43 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dr. Pippa Malmgren argues the US-Iran standoff over near-weapons-grade uranium is one piece of a larger US-China-Russia superpower 'Rubik's cube' negotiation resolving multiple global flashpoints simultaneously, with Trump believing the uranium carries forensic proof of prior US administration complicity in Iran's nuclear program. Jim Bianco provides a bearish counterpoint, warning that cheap disposable drones have fundamentally negated great-power forcing mechanisms, oil inventories have weeks not months of buffer, and markets are dangerously complacent from a learned-helplessness pattern of 'buying every dip.' Both guests agree the Strait of Hormuz must reopen but disagree sharply on how quickly that happens and how much time the US truly has.
Iran Uranium Standoff — Forensic Motive & Superpower Rubik's Cube · 8Cheap Drone Warfare Has Nullified Great-Power Forcing Mechanisms · 5Oil Market — Inventory Runway, Demand Destruction Threshold & $150–$200 Scenario · 6Fed, Inflation & Bond Market Under Oil-Price Shock · 3Nuclear Energy Transition — SMRs, TRISO Fuel & Genesis Mission · 6AI, Robotics & the Post-Job Economy · 6Equity Markets — AI Bubble Concentration, Breadth Divergence & Sector Rotation · 4Gold, Disappearing Scientists & UAP Declassification · 5
Iran Uranium Standoff — Forensic Motive & Superpower Rubik's Cube 8
Pippa Malmgren contends Trump's insistence on physical possession of Iran's enriched uranium is primarily driven by his belief that the material can be forensically traced to US-facilitated transfers made during the Obama/Clinton/Biden administrations, not merely to prevent Iran going nuclear.
thesis🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highIranDonald TrumpBarack ObamaHillary ClintonJoe BidenIAEA
So what: If true, this reframes the negotiation as a political accountability probe rather than a pure non-proliferation deal, meaning Trump may never accept a deal that doesn't include forensic access—raising the floor on how long the strait stays closed.
“one of the reasons that he is insisting on a physical access to this material is because of that and that the people on the other side know this. So they're holding out for the highest possible price.”
Malmgren frames the Iran conflict, Ukraine, Taiwan, Cuba, and Venezuela as a single interlocking 'Rubik's cube' negotiation between the US, China, and Russia where no issue can be resolved independently—and says Cuba and Venezuela are already effectively capitulating to US terms.
framework🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highUSAChinaRussiaIranUkraineTaiwanCubaVenezuela
So what: Investors pricing individual geopolitical risks in isolation (Iran premium in oil, Taiwan risk in semis) may be mis-sizing them; resolution of one node could cascade rapidly across others.
“the superpowers are negotiating between themselves right the US China particularly Russia as well and they are attempting to resolve all of the outstanding geopolitical issues all at once this is a kind of Rubik's cube approach”
Iran's IRGC has been deliberately decentralized into 32 independent command-and-control regions, each engaged autonomously in a religious war, making top-down deal-making nearly impossible even if a nominal leadership agrees.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highIranIRGC
So what: Even a signed ceasefire deal may not stop drone and missile attacks on shipping; the operational risk to the strait could persist well after any political agreement.
“the guys that have the drones and the missiles right next door to the strait are the Iranian IRGC which has been by its design separated and decentralized into 32 different command and control regions that all independently are engaged in a religious war”
Iran has 441 kg of uranium enriched to 60%—enough for up to 9–10 nuclear bombs if further enriched to weapons-grade 90%—and this has been physically verified by IAEA inspectors, rendering any claims that Iran lacks a nuclear weapons program accurate but meaningfully misleading.
data_point🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highIranIAEA
So what: Market participants who discount the nuclear dimension as resolved or overstated are underestimating the irreducible US red line; no deal closes until this 441 kg is physically transferred or demonstrably destroyed.
“Iran has done 99% of the enrichment activity necessary in order to get enough enriched material to make several nuclear bombs from”
Jim Bianco argues the uranium buried under hundreds of feet of rock from the US bombing campaign means recovering and transferring the material is a multi-month engineering project (building a landing strip, excavating), so any 30–60 day negotiating window the Trump administration outlined is structurally insufficient for actual delivery.
causal🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highIranDonald TrumpJim Bianco
So what: Oil markets pricing in a swift resolution face a hard physical constraint; a 'deal' on paper could still leave the strait effectively closed for months while engineering work proceeds.
“the first step in getting the nuclear material might very well be to send in the engineers to build a landing strip so we could land heavy equipment to dig down and get the nuclear material. And we're not going to give them reparations or money or any other deal until we get that. And that might not be till next year.”
Malmgren argues that what remains of Iran's 'leadership' is actually a collection of competing factions jockeying to be the group that shakes hands with the US, analogous to Syria's post-conflict dynamic, and that Russia offered Moscow apartments and pensions to the most senior IRGC figures who accepted—leaving behind a less controllable remnant.
anecdote🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: mediumIranIRGCRussiaSyria
So what: The party the US is de facto negotiating with may lack the authority to enforce any agreement, compounding Bianco's concern that there is no forcing mechanism.
The US strategy toward Iran mirrors its approach to Syria: not regime change followed by state collapse, but 'conversion'—creating conditions so favorable economically that remaining hostile is not worth it—and Trump's Abraham Accords expansion is designed to make every regional nation an enforcer of that logic.
framework🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highIranUSADonald TrumpAbraham AccordsSyria
So what: This framework implies a longer but more durable resolution path, which is bearish for near-term oil but potentially bullish for regional reconstruction and investment themes once a deal lands.
“The strategy is not regime change. The strategy is conversion. It is to create the conditions that are so favorable that it's not worth remaining where you were.”
China explicitly agreed during the Trump-Xi summit that it does not support Iranian nuclear weapons and is actively pressuring Iran to reopen the strait because the strait closure hurts China most; the first ships that transited after the crisis opened were all Chinese oil-carrying vessels, under an apparent unannounced US-China side deal.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highChinaUSAIranStrait of HormuzXi JinpingDonald Trump
So what: US-China alignment on Iran means sanctions/pressure can come from both sides simultaneously; but it also means China retains an implicit 'oil passage' carve-out which limits the completeness of any blockade.
“notice that the first ships that came through are all Chinese oil carrying vessels”
Cheap Drone Warfare Has Nullified Great-Power Forcing Mechanisms 5
Jim Bianco argues that cheap, attritable drones have fundamentally changed warfare such that no major power—not the US, France, UK, Russia, or China—currently has the ability to force open a contested waterway by conventional naval power, citing the US failure to keep the Strait open with three destroyers and 100 aircraft in Operation Protect Freedom at a cost exceeding $100 million in two days.
thesis🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highUSAIranIRGCStrait of HormuzOperation Protect Freedom
So what: Markets that assume great-power resolve can force a quick resolution to the strait closure are wrong; the military forcing mechanism simply doesn't exist, making the base case for prolonged closure materially higher than priced.
≈ “War has changed a lot right now. And I don't think we're really appreciating how much it's changed. And that's why we have no forcing mechanism to open the straight.” paraphrase
A single US destroyer costs $3 billion and carries 96 vertically-launched missiles; an adversary needs only ~100 cheap drones to exhaust those magazines, because even with 100% hit rate the last 4 get through—and adversaries just add 20–30 more to overwhelm close-in weapon systems.
framework🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highUSAIranIRGC
So what: Defense sector investors should reassess the value of legacy surface-fleet platforms; the cost asymmetry heavily favors cheap drone producers over expensive missile-defense integrators.
“the average US destroyer which costs $3 billion has 96 vertically launched missiles. And even if they're 100% my last four get through.”
During Operation Protect Freedom, two of three US destroyers had to withdraw from the Strait after just two days because they exhausted their missile magazines, and Gulf Cooperation Council nations revoked US air-base access after the US could not protect their infrastructure from concurrent Iranian drone attacks.
data_point🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highUSAIranGCCOperation Protect Freedom
So what: The US cannot simultaneously defend its own warships and protect allied infrastructure in the Gulf—a critical operational constraint that investors and policymakers are underweighting.
“two of them had to come back out because they ran out of missiles and they had to go get reloaded”
Bianco extends the drone-warfare thesis to Taiwan: he believes China is not currently capable of invading Taiwan even if it wanted to, because cheap drone swarms could defeat any amphibious assault fleet—implying Taiwan invasion risk is lower than feared but also that the US could not conventionally defend Taiwan either.
contrarian🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: mediumChinaTaiwanUSA
So what: This cuts both ways for Taiwan-exposed equities like TSMC: lower invasion probability is bullish, but US ability to deter is also lower, keeping the risk premium structurally elevated.
“I don't think that China is in a position to be able to attack Taiwan even if they wanted to”
The Houthis successfully closed the Bab el-Mandeb at the southern Red Sea using cheap drones and the French and British navies failed to reopen it; Bianco notes that to date it remains closed, establishing a precedent that minor actors can permanently deny transit through strategic waterways.
data_point🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highHouthisRed SeaBab el-MandebFranceUK
So what: The world may be structurally entering an era of two permanently constrained waterways (Bab el-Mandeb and Hormuz) affecting ~30–40% of seaborne energy—a structural shift for LNG, shipping, and energy-import-dependent economies.
“the Houthies closed the Babel Mandab at the southern point of the Red Sea and the French and the British went in and they tried to open it and they couldn't and to this day it's still not open”
Oil Market — Inventory Runway, Demand Destruction Threshold & $150–$200 Scenario 6
Approximately 13 million barrels per day of production has been shut in by the Strait of Hormuz closure; fewer than 10% of normal traffic levels are transiting, and current demand/supply mismatch is being bridged by drawing down private and strategic petroleum reserve inventories with only roughly one month of buffer remaining.
data_point🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highStrait of HormuzWTISPR
So what: The runway to a price spike is measured in weeks (June/July), not months; oil traders need to set June/July as the decision horizon for whether demand destruction pricing kicks in.
Most analysts cited on the show put the required demand destruction at 10–15 million barrels per day, which they estimate requires $150–$200 crude oil; the lone outlier, Dr. Anna Al-Haji, estimates only ~3 million barrels of demand destruction is needed and that Saudi East-West pipeline workarounds have already absorbed much of the loss.
data_point🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: mediumWTISaudi ArabiaEast-West Pipeline
So what: There is a wide range of expert opinion on how severe the demand destruction price signal must be; even the most optimistic (Al-Haji) agrees prices go higher if the strait stays shut another month—the debate is whether it's $110 or $200.
Patrick Sesna describes oil as a 'buy on dip' even in a resolution scenario, because inventory replenishment cycles mean markets will remain tight for months post-reopening; he targets oil trading back toward $100+ from the current ~$89 level.
thesis🗣 Patrick Sesnaconviction: highWTIcrude oil
So what: Energy bulls have a two-sided trade: both the 'no-deal' scenario ($150+) and the 'deal' scenario (tight inventory replenishment) support higher oil prices—the bull case survives either outcome.
“I believe that oil has every opportunity to trade back toward 100 plus. So I'm using this weakness to be buying dips.”
WTI crude fell nearly 10% (to ~$88.68) in the week ending May 27, 2026, solely on optimism that a deal was 'hours away'; Bianco and Townsend argue no Iranian official has ever conceded one inch on uranium repatriation, making this selloff a mispriced risk-reward that re-rates sharply higher on the next escalation.
contrarian🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highWTIIrancrude oil
So what: The near-10% weekly crude selloff on unfounded deal optimism is a potential entry point for long energy; the market's 'Iran deal imminent' repricing is structurally unsupported by any actual negotiating progress.
Malmgren argues the US is deliberately comfortable with the strait remaining closed because it forces global buyers to purchase American molecules—including the deal to sell Alaskan oil to China struck at the Trump-Xi summit—giving the US two years of captive market share before the energy transition renders this edge obsolete.
thesis🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highUSAChinaAlaskaIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: This implies US diplomatic urgency to resolve the crisis is structurally lower than allies assume; European and Asian energy importers should plan for a prolonged US-as-swing-supplier regime.
“the United States is perfectly fine with the straight remaining closed because this forces the whole world to buy these molecules of oil and gas that are currently blocked in the straight. They have to buy them from America.”
Bianco warns that government attempts to cushion $150–$200 oil via gas-tax holidays or consumer subsidies will be counterproductive: if demand destruction is the only available supply-demand rebalancing mechanism and subsidies prevent price signals from reducing demand, oil prices must go even higher to achieve the required demand reduction.
causal🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highWTIUSADonald TrumpFederal Reserve
So what: Any policy subsidy of energy consumption during a supply shortage worsens the oil price spiral; watch for gas-tax holiday announcements as a contrarian 'buy more oil' signal.
≈ “if the world wants to consume 105 million barrels a day, which is roughly what it consumes and we're only capable of supplying 90 to 95 million barrels a day, we have a mismatch...if we're going to say...we're going to try and stimulate, cut rates, hand out subsidies...we could wind up just having oil prices spiral to the moon.” paraphrase
Fed, Inflation & Bond Market Under Oil-Price Shock 3
Kevin Warsh became Fed Chair less than a week before the podcast recording; his most recent public speech (from 2025) indicated a preference for rate cuts, but the bond market is pricing in rate hikes—creating an immediate policy credibility test if oil-driven inflation pushes CPI above 4%.
data_point🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshUS TreasuryCPI
So what: New Fed chair with dovish prior statements facing an inflationary oil shock is the single largest near-term uncertainty for US rates; watch Warsh's first post-appointment communication very closely.
≈ “we have a new Fed chairman who's been on the job for less than a week. His last public speech was last year. he wanted to cut rates. We assume he still wants to cut rates, but the market is pricing in rate hikes.” paraphrase
Bianco argues that if the Fed cuts rates while CPI is already above 4% and heading higher on oil, the bond market will interpret that as Fed tolerance for inflation, causing Treasury yields to drift further higher—creating a negative feedback loop between soft monetary policy and rising long rates.
causal🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highFederal ReserveUS TreasuryCPIKevin Warsh
So what: A Fed that blinks on inflation to support growth could cause a bond market selloff that tightens financial conditions anyway—making the easing signal self-defeating and potentially forcing a policy reversal.
“the problem with that is is that the market is not there for Fed the Fed to cut rates and it could actually backfire on them”
US 10-year Treasury yield was 4.51% at podcast recording date (May 27, 2026), with both Townsend and Sesna expecting it to drift back toward its recent highs if Iran remains unresolved and oil-driven inflation persists.
data_point🗣 Patrick Sesnaconviction: mediumUS Treasury10-year Treasury
So what: Duration exposure remains a tactical short; the 10-year has little room to rally while the energy shock is unresolved and fiscal stimulus is on the table.
Nuclear Energy Transition — SMRs, TRISO Fuel & Genesis Mission 6
Malmgren describes 'TRISO fuel' (tristructural isotropic fuel) as poppy-seed-sized pellets with near-indestructible shells that eliminate meltdown risk entirely; she cites a claim that a 'half-handful' of TRISO pellets can fuel 5,000 homes indefinitely, distinguishing next-gen SMRs fundamentally from Three Mile Island/Chernobyl-era reactors.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: mediumTRISO fuelSMRnuclear energyAlo
So what: If TRISO-fuel SMRs are broadly deployable, the nuclear energy investment thesis accelerates far beyond consensus timelines; watch for commercial-scale permitting and deployment data as near-term catalysts.
“it takes literally half a handful of trico pellets to fuel as I'm told 5,000 homes...indefinitely”
Valor Atomics (California) is building SMRs in a box 'half the size of a car'—a fully mobile nuclear power unit—while Alo (Austin, TX) built a small modular reactor with fewer than 300 people in under 365 days with Texas governor expedited approval, demonstrating sub-year deployment timelines previously thought impossible.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: mediumValor AtomicsAloTexasSMR
So what: Investors applying '$30 billion and 30 years' mental models to nuclear are using obsolete assumptions; watch the Valor/Alo private financing and licensing pipeline as leading indicators of the new cost and speed curve.
“They have built a small modular reactor with less than 300 people in less than 365 days”
The White House's 'Genesis Mission' has ordered all US national labs (Los Alamos, Lawrence Livermore, Argonne, etc.) to lift classification on their accumulated data, run AI over the combined datasets, and connect findings across labs—with the stated expectation of accelerating breakthrough technologies, particularly in new energy forms and nuclear.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highGenesis MissionLos AlamosLawrence LivermoreArgonneDonald TrumpAI
So what: If even a fraction of previously classified national-lab IP reaches commercial application via AI synthesis, it could dramatically compress the development curve for fusion, advanced fission, materials, and biotech—a secular bull case for deep-tech venture.
“the president said Right, this is going to stop. We're going to lift the lid off all of them. We're going to run AI over all the data, and we're going to connect the dots within the labs and across the labs.”
The uranium ETF (URA, Global X) was trading at $49.58 at recording; Patrick Sesna constructed a January 2027 bull call spread—buy $60 call for $5.70, sell $70 call for $3.50, net $2.20 debit—for a 3.5:1 risk/reward ($7.80 max gain) as the trade-of-the-week expression of the nuclear energy security theme.
data_point🗣 Patrick Sesnaconviction: mediumURAGlobal X Uranium ETFuraniumnuclear energy
So what: Defined-risk structure captures the nuclear thesis with 232 days of runway and limited downside; the spread profits if URA moves ~40% from current levels by Jan 2027—watch uranium spot price and SMR permitting news as catalysts.
Uranium spot (June contract) was up 65 bps to $85.10 at recording while gold, oil, copper, and gasoline were all down on the week, suggesting early rotation into the nuclear energy theme even amid broader commodity weakness.
data_point🗣 Patrick Sesnaconviction: mediumuraniumgoldWTIcopper
So what: Uranium's relative strength during a commodity selloff is a potential divergence signal that institutional money is beginning to price the nuclear transition theme ahead of the energy crisis narrative.
Malmgren frames the US energy strategy as a deliberate two-phase transition: maximize revenue from existing hydrocarbon dominance for approximately two years while the strait is closed (phase 1), then pivot to atoms—SMRs, nuclear fusion, and alternative clean energy—as the permanent solution (phase 2); the strait closure is therefore an accelerant, not a crisis, from Washington's perspective.
framework🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highUSASMRnuclear fusionStrait of Hormuzoil and gas
So what: This is bullish for both near-term US energy equities and long-term nuclear/SMR names—the White House has structural incentives to let oil stay high just long enough to fund the transition without crashing the economy.
“the US is delighted to be making a ton of money on the oil that it can sell to the world for the next couple of years in order to finance the transition from the old drill baby drill approach to let's just use new forms of clean energy”
AI, Robotics & the Post-Job Economy 6
Malmgren argues that AI and robotics are not primarily a jobs threat but a debt-resolution mechanism: the only way advanced economies can outrun their massive sovereign debt burdens is to achieve a step-change in production and reduce the cost of living to near-zero via automation and cheap energy—a goal that regular human labor cannot accomplish.
thesis🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highAIroboticsUSAEuropeChinaUK
So what: Investors framing AI as a deflationary threat to wages may be missing that it is simultaneously a sovereign debt sustainability tool—making AI adoption politically non-negotiable and its deployment pace likely to exceed market consensus.
“the superpowers are looking at this going, the only way that we can get ahead of this terrible hole that's been created is to go to a whole new level of production”
Icon (Austin, TX) is 3D-printing entire neighborhoods of three-bedroom homes that include Tesla Powerwalls; Malmgren estimates the fully loaded cost is approximately equivalent to existing Austin homes today but projects the price will 'collapse' to ~$100,000 per unit as the process scales—with embedded solar generation potentially covering a meaningful portion of the mortgage.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: mediumIconTeslaAustin TX3D printed homes
So what: If Icon-style 3D printing scales to $100k per home, it represents a structural deflationary shock to housing costs—bearish for traditional homebuilders and REIT-based housing exposure, bullish for consumer spending power.
“I would add in we're making big advances...3D printed home is only going to cost 100 grand because that's where we're heading”
Malmgren predicts that AI will enable a 'portfolio of activities' income model—multiple small income streams generated with minimal labor—replacing the single-employer paycheck as the dominant economic unit, using the example of a person earning $10,000/month by uploading rain sounds to music platforms and using AI agents to market ASMR content.
prediction🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: mediumAISonos
So what: Platform businesses enabling micro-monetization (music, content, data licensing) have a large untapped secular growth driver as AI lowers the skill floor for passive income generation.
“he could record the sound of the rain and then uploaded as a track onto Sono, set up AI agents to go market this thing...suddenly he's making like 10 grand a month”
TSMC indicated that Taiwan's role as the epicenter of cutting-edge semiconductor production will last only another 18 months to 2 years before capacity shifts primarily to the US (Texas and Arizona); critics say 10 years, but Malmgren argues that with AI-accelerated engineering transfer and most key Taiwanese engineers already relocated to the US, the 18-month timeline is credible.
contrarian🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: mediumTSMCTaiwanTexasArizonasemiconductors
So what: If TSMC's US facilities achieve cutting-edge production within 2 years, Taiwan geopolitical risk in the semiconductor supply chain largely dissolves—bullish for US fab real estate, engineering services, and utilities in TX/AZ; bearish for Taiwan-centric supply chain premiums.
“TMATH came out a few days ago and said Yeah, it's we've only got to rely on Taiwan for another 18 months, maybe two years”
The Helium shortage triggered when IRGC struck semiconductor production plants in Qatar—which briefly threatened global semiconductor output—was resolved within three weeks when South Korean chipmakers (Hynix, Samsung) signed supply deals with Texas, demonstrating supply-chain resilience that markets underweight.
anecdote🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highHynixSamsungTexasQatarIRGCsemiconductorshelium
So what: The speed of US helium-for-chips substitution is a concrete data point that supply chains are more reroutable than feared; similar resilience may be underpriced in other apparent bottlenecks from the strait closure.
“Highex, Samsung, semiconductors close the deal with Texas. So we It's like people don't understand you can just shift the supply chain.”
Japan has achieved a significant breakthrough in space-based solar power and wireless energy transmission—an area Malmgren cites as one of several converging energy technologies that will make distributed power independent of the grid—alongside SMRs and green ammonia fertilizer alternatives.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: lowJapanspace-based solarwireless energygreen ammonia
So what: Investors tracking energy transition themes should add space-based solar and wireless transmission to their monitoring list; Japan's breakthrough, if commercializable, would eliminate geographic constraints on solar energy deployment.
Equity Markets — AI Bubble Concentration, Breadth Divergence & Sector Rotation 4
The S&P 500 hit 7,520 as of May 27, 2026 (new all-time highs) with market breadth near 50%—far below the 70–80% participation typical at healthy bull-market highs—driven almost entirely by semiconductor and AI names, creating an unusually fragile concentration.
data_point🗣 Patrick Sesnaconviction: highS&P 500semiconductorsNASDAQAI
So what: New all-time highs on 50% breadth is historically a late-cycle warning; a sector rotation scenario is more likely than a broadening, suggesting reducing concentration in top-weighted AI names while adding to laggards (healthcare, defense, financials).
≈ “Rarely do we see a market that is trading at 52-week highs for such a prolonged period with the breath of the market not participating. Usually, we can get up to 70 80% of the stock market participating.” paraphrase
Bianco identifies the market's Iran complacency as a product of learned helplessness: investors were burned by selling at the bottom during COVID, 9% inflation, SVB failure, and Liberation Day tariffs—four successive false alarms—so they are now reflexively buying every dip and dismissing Hormuz as another false alarm.
causal🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highS&P 500Silicon Valley BankCOVID-19IranStrait of Hormuz
So what: The behavioral pattern that is suppressing the risk premium on Iran is the same one that caused investors to stay long through 2007 subprime; when the cycle breaks it breaks fast—position sizing for tail risk in energy is cheap relative to the complacency discount.
“They were warned liberation day was a big deal. They sold the bottom of the market. Now they're being warned that the straight of vermoose being closed is a big deal. And they're saying, 'Uh-huh. I'm not going to fall for that again.'”
Bianco notes that the dominant near-term stock market narrative is AI—specifically anticipated IPOs of SpaceX, Anthropic, and possibly OpenAI—which he expects to continue outcompeting the Iran oil story for investor attention even if crude prices keep rising.
prediction🗣 Jim Biancoconviction: highSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIAINASDAQS&P 500
So what: Pre-IPO positioning in AI infrastructure and cloud names remains the crowded trade most likely to persist; the IPO catalyst pipeline (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI) provides a concrete timeline for continued AI equity demand.
“the dominant theme in the stock market right now is still AI...the coming IPOs of SpaceX and anthropic and possibly open AI”
Townsend is explicitly uncertain whether a severe oil shock ($150–$200 crude) would crash the stock market or cause it to rally on expectations of government stimulus—a sign that the traditional macro playbook for oil shocks may be broken by the era of reflex fiscal intervention.
contrarian🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: lowS&P 500WTIFederal ReserveUSA
So what: The old rule (oil spike → stock market crash) may not hold if the Fed and fiscal authorities reflexively announce stimulus; this creates a non-linear payoff for energy longs that is not offset by equity shorts.
“I'm on the fence about whether that is going to necessarily cause a stock market crash or if the stock market might actually just celebrate expectations of government stimulus”
Gold, Disappearing Scientists & UAP Declassification 5
Gold was trading at $4,481 (down 190 bps on the week) and exhibiting 'distributive price action,' with silver seeing a false breakout fully reversed, platinum near monthly lows, and gold miners under stress—Sesna and Townsend see no evidence of institutional buying support in the entire precious metals complex in late May.
data_point🗣 Patrick Sesnaconviction: mediumgoldsilverplatinumgold miners
So what: Precious metals bulls should wait for July/August as the likely catalyst window; a confirmed Iran deal or a confirmed escalation with $150+ oil would both eventually be gold-bullish, but neither is imminent enough to justify buying the current distribution.
“At some stage, this is going to be a screaming buy on dip. But as of this moment, there's very little evidence that this is that moment here in late May.”
Townsend notes that 12–15 of the most senior American nuclear scientists (including people running the Air Force Research Lab) have recently 'disappeared or suddenly been killed in very odd ways, statistically unlikely ways,' with no official investigation—and Malmgren says the same pattern is occurring in China with senior scientists dying under strange circumstances.
data_point🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: lowUSAChinaAir Force Research Labnuclear scientists
So what: If the disappearances are adversarial (foreign intelligence operations), it implies active sabotage of the US and Chinese nuclear/advanced-tech ecosystems—a tail risk for the Genesis Mission's knowledge-declassification timeline.
“we have maybe now something between 12 and 15 of the most senior American nuclear scientists...have literally just disappeared or suddenly been killed in very odd ways, statistically unlikely ways”
Malmgren links the White House's UAP/UFO declassification initiative (alien.gov and aliens.gov websites, one billion views cited) to a potential strategy for nullifying private-sector patents on technologies derived from classified non-human-origin research—under US law, patents whose underlying IP has non-human origin may be invalid.
thesis🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: lowDonald TrumpUSAUAPalien.gov
So what: Exploratory but strategically interesting: if UAP declassification is used to challenge patents held by defense contractors on classified-origin technologies, it could materially disrupt IP valuations for major defense and materials companies.
“under US law if the origin of a technology is not human then the patents are not valid”
Malmgren observes that AI has recently decoded a whale language and discovered that whales have shared nicknames for specific marine biologists who study them—using this as a philosophical framing for her broader thesis that non-human intelligence is pervasive and humanity's 'alone in intelligence' assumption is empirically wrong.
anecdote🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highAI
So what: Primarily a philosophical/framing point, but the underlying AI-to-animal-language research has applied implications for biosecurity, ecological modeling, and long-term AI alignment research.
“AI has recently cracked one of the whale languages. They discovered that these whales have specific and shared nicknames for the marine biologists who study them.”
Malmgren argues that Trump's strategy toward the defense and pharmaceutical industries follows the same template: force cost reductions by threatening the monopoly structures that have historically insulated defense contractors (stock buyback culture, cost-plus pricing) and pharma (US price discrimination), with the defense community already put on notice in a presidential speech.
causal🗣 Pippa Malmgrenconviction: highDonald Trumpdefense contractorspharmaUSA
So what: Defense and pharma equities face structural margin compression from executive pressure independent of legislative outcomes; investors should monitor contract re-pricing trends and R&D spending as early signals of this squeeze.
“You guys have been optimizing for sure buybacks and for profits and this is going to end. You're now going to optimize for the creation of defense equipment that actually works at a reasonable price.”
MacroVoices #535 Michael Every: NAFTA and NAPTHA – Warcraft & Fartcraft97 min40 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Rabobank global strategist Michael Every and oil analyst Rory Johnston dissect the Strait of Hormuz blockade as a catalyst for a US shift from economic policy to 'economic statecraft,' arguing the crisis may force radical energy-market restructuring (a NAFTHA closed-loop trade bloc), Fed redesign under Kevin Warsh, and stablecoin-based dollar geopolitics. Johnston provides granular oil-flow data showing 13–15 mb/d shut in, Chinese crude imports halved to ~6 mb/d, and buffers drawing at the fastest pace on record, yet prices remain below $100 because speculators have been scared out by Trump's Truth Social jawboning.
Strait of Hormuz supply shock: scale, buffers, and price paradox · 8China's hidden oil balance: invisible SPR and geopolitical optionality · 7Economic statecraft: NAFTHA, swap lines, and the dollar architecture shift · 6Federal Reserve transformation under Kevin Warsh · 5Geopolitical outcomes: Iran 'loss' scenarios and US credibility · 5Gold, dollar, and asset-market implications · 7Physical-economy rebuild trade: PAVE ETF construction · 2
Strait of Hormuz supply shock: scale, buffers, and price paradox 8
Approximately 13–15 million barrels per day of oil production remains shut in because trapped vessels in the Persian Gulf cannot export, constituting the core supply crisis from the Hormuz closure.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highStrait of HormuzPersian GulfOPEC
So what: This is the largest supply shock ever recorded; any resumption of trade or deal timeline directly drives the magnitude and duration of price upside.
“That 13 to 15 million barrel a day is the core supply crisis or production crisis really that is currently still gripping the oil market and crossings out of the straight are not going to ameliate that issue.”
Roughly 2,000 vessels are trapped in the Persian Gulf, of which 800 are large merchant ships; only 1 to ~12 ships per day have been crossing, slowly draining what is effectively a captive strategic petroleum reserve of trapped oil.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highPersian GulfArabian GulfStrait of Hormuz
So what: Each ship crossing is a marginal release of dead inventory, not new production; the ceiling on this relief flow is physically constrained and far too small to offset the shut-in barrels.
“We still have hundreds of vessels trapped in the Gulf, uh the Persian Gulf, the Arabian Gulf... 2,000 vessels or so. Now 800 of those are the large merchant ships.”
Even if a Hormuz deal is signed immediately, clearing stranded ships and restarting shut-in production would take weeks to months before oil flows normalize, meaning the supply shock has a multi-month tail regardless of the diplomatic outcome.
causal🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highStrait of HormuzIran
So what: A peace deal headline should not be treated as an immediate oil supply relief event; logistical timelines mean prices stay elevated for months post-deal.
“there will be a process that will need to be started of clearing long stranded ships out of the Gulf and then getting new ships into the Gulf. And that process is going to take weeks and more likely months before you can even start the monthsl long process of restarting oil production that's been shut in.”
Global commercial oil inventories are drawing down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record, yet crude prices were below $100/bbl (WTI ~$96, Brent ~$98) at the time of recording, a historically anomalous divergence.
contrarian🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highWTIBrentEIA
So what: The draw rate implies an eventual violent price correction; the disconnect signals either structural market changes or a hidden buffer (China's invisible SPR) that makes timing uncertain.
“We are now drawing that down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record. If that was happening in any other moment in the market, the market would be way more panicked than it is right now.”
Speculator washout caused by Trump's Truth Social jawboning is a primary reason oil prices have not reacted proportionally to the supply shock; presidential tweets have knocked $20+ from crude in under 10 minutes, scaring systematic traders out of long positions.
causal🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highWTITruth SocialDonald Trump
So what: Once physical inventory draws exhaust buffers, no tweet can offset real-market rebalancing; this creates an asymmetric long setup once the speculator clearing is complete.
“they learned the power of the truth social post, which is Trump can knock $20 plus out of the price of crude oil in 10 minutes... the speculators got knocked out of this market”
Rory Johnston's updated base case is oil reaching ~$150/bbl if the Strait of Hormuz remains substantially closed through Labor Day (early September 2026); maintaining sub-$100 oil through that timeline would require him to 'rip up the entire way I thought I understood the market.'
prediction🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highWTIBrentStrait of Hormuz
So what: Provides a credible, data-grounded price target and timeline; the $150 level represents a physical-rebalancing floor, not a speculative spike.
“I don't see a way to avoid substantially higher prices. 150 kind of would be my updated assumption around what that would be like on that timeline.”
Dr. Anas Alhajji argued the effective net supply deficit is only ~3–4 mb/d rather than 13 mb/d because mitigations (demand response, inventory drawdowns, rerouting) have offset most of the gross shut-in, and he expects oil below $100 in 2027 with prices peaking in 2026.
contrarian🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumAnas AlhajjiWTI
So what: The most bullish (least fearful) credible bear case from an oil-market veteran still implies a spike to ~$150 before a 2026 peak — the bull floor is high even in the benign scenario.
“it's only about 3 or 4 million barrels, not 13, that are a deficit in the net... He thinks oil prices below 100 in 27, but maybe uh they're not done going up in 26.”
The Strait of Bab-el-Mandeb closure by Houthi proxies is identified as a key tail-risk accelerant: much of the oil blocked from Hormuz is being re-routed via Saudi pipeline to the Red Sea, so closing Bab-el-Mandeb would effectively shut the last meaningful relief valve.
causal🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumBab-el-MandebRed SeaHouthisSaudi ArabiaIran
So what: Monitor Israel-Lebanon escalation as a leading indicator for Houthi re-engagement; a Bab-el-Mandeb closure would collapse the pipeline workaround and produce an extreme price shock.
≈ “if that gets closed too and it is well within the possibility of the Houthis and Iran's proxies that could definitely escalate things and escalate things and escalate them very quickly.” paraphrase
China's hidden oil balance: invisible SPR and geopolitical optionality 7
Chinese crude oil imports collapsed approximately 50% from pre-war levels (~12 mb/d seaborne) to ~6 mb/d, representing a volume roughly equal to half the total Hormuz supply shock — yet Chinese economic activity data (trucking, road congestion, flights) shows no corresponding demand destruction.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highChinaKeplerOPEC
So what: China is drawing from invisible (underground or unreported) strategic reserves, not suffering economic damage; this creates enormous uncertainty about how long China can sustain low imports and when the buying impulse reverses.
“Chinese crude oil imports have fallen by roughly 50%. Relative to prewar levels. Prior to that in terms of seaborn imports, they were importing ballparks 12 million barrels a day of crude. Now that you know as of the latest uh you know four-week average, we're looking at 6 million barrels a day of crude.”
Chinese refinery runs have collapsed even as domestic fuel consumption data (mobility, trucking) remains stable, implying China is releasing refined product from invisible stockpiles rather than processing new crude — likely built up during the 2023 'demand surge' that was actually strategic pre-war stockpiling.
causal🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaKepler
So what: China's visible refining collapse is a signal of strategic inventory deployment, not economic weakness; when those refined-product stocks deplete, refining and crude imports will surge simultaneously, tightening global balances sharply.
≈ “One of the theories at the time was that it wasn't real demand at all. It was it was strategic stockpiling of refined products potentially in anticipation of a war or invasion of Taiwan.” paraphrase
Chinese domestic fuel retail prices were capped at a level implying only ~30% price increase versus a global doubling, crushing refinery margins to deeply negative levels and incentivizing a halt to production and export of refined products.
causal🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highChinaBeijing
So what: Price-cap policy provides a purely commercial (not just geopolitical) explanation for China's refinery run collapse; lifting the cap or deep negative margins eventually forces Beijing to either raise prices or boost refining subsidies.
“Chinese basically fuel cap fuel price caps on pump prices for retail consumers have dramatically shielded China Chinese consumers from the explosion in global prices. Like you've seen a rise in I think Beijing's um retail prices for gasoline of like maybe 30% versus a global doubling.”
China's pullback from crude purchasing directly freed up incremental supply for Korean, Japanese, and Australian importers, enabling those US-allied Asian economies to cushion their own supply shock; Johnston interprets this as China positioning itself as a 'multilateral good-guy' in Asia as US credibility under Trump erodes.
causal🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaJapanSouth KoreaAustraliaBeijing
So what: Beijing is running a soft-power play via oil market behavior; watch Chinese import levels as a leading indicator of whether China is tightening or loosening its regional influence campaign.
“I think part of that is that they could be trying to step into a bit more of a multilateral governance good guy role in a part of the world that now sees Trump as someone who left them for slaughter.”
Satellite-based inventory trackers (Kepler) show China holds well over 1 billion barrels of visible crude oil inventories, which continued rising through most of the Hormuz crisis — the drawdowns are coming from underground or statistically invisible stocks that cannot be monitored.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaKepler
So what: The opacity of China's true reserve levels is the single largest unresolvable uncertainty in the global oil market; any intelligence on underground storage status would be highly market-moving.
“we do have up until you know recent crisis a pretty unprecedented degree of visibility into global at least inventory of crude oil... we know that China has well over a billion barrels of crude oil across the country”
Johnston's key forward trigger: when Chinese crude imports begin rising again from the current 5–6 mb/d floor while Hormuz is still closed, that combination of tighter realized balances (drawn inventories) plus renewed Chinese demand will catalyze the next large oil price rip — likely by end of June to July 2026.
prediction🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaWTIBrentEIA
So what: Watch Chinese weekly crude import data as the primary leading indicator for the next oil price spike; the trigger is the delta turning from loosening to tightening.
“when the delta and the impulse shifts from loosening back to tightening. I think that's probably when we do that next rip higher”
Michael Every raised the scenario that China, if its strategic reserves are as depleted as they may actually be, could be forced to cooperate with the US against Iran — accepting geostrategic damage to its Iranian alliance to avoid catastrophic domestic energy disruption.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: exploratoryChinaIranUSBeijing
So what: A China-US backchannel deal on Hormuz would be the ultimate bull-killer for oil prices; watch for any unusual diplomatic signaling between Washington and Beijing on energy.
“part of this resolution could be, ironically, China having to work with the US against its own ally, Iran, because it realizes that the potential damage to it from this crisis is even larger than losing Iran as an ally.”
Economic statecraft: NAFTHA, swap lines, and the dollar architecture shift 6
Michael Every has argued since Trump's re-election that the US would shift from 'economic policy' to 'economic statecraft,' and the US government is now explicitly using that phrase publicly — the Iran crisis is accelerating this transition.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUSDonald TrumpScott BessantRabobank
So what: The framing shift has direct implications for how every instrument of US power — Fed, Treasury, dollar, military — is coordinated; investors pricing markets as if the old neoliberal policy framework persists are mispricing regime risk.
“the US is openly now saying publicly that economic statecraft is what it's pursuing. And that's absolutely part and parcel of the argument I was just making then about this energy crisis”
Every proposed a 'NAFTHA' scenario — a North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Association — where the US creates a closed-loop energy bloc with Mexico, Canada, Venezuela, Guyana, key Latin American producers, select Middle Eastern partners (UAE), and refining-capable US-allied Asian economies, allowing these countries to weather the Hormuz crisis while the rest of the world faces catastrophic shortages.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: exploratoryUSMexicoCanadaVenezuelaGuyanaUAEUSMCA
So what: If implemented, NAFTHA would bifurcate global oil pricing along geopolitical lines, making energy access a function of alliance status — a structurally higher risk premium for non-US-aligned importers.
“Instead of having NAFTA, you have NAFTA, which is the North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association where effectively you have again this closed loop for energy”
Dollar swap lines are being weaponized as economic statecraft: the Fed has offered swap lines to the UAE (which has a dollar-pegged currency, large energy reserves, and recently left OPEC+), signaling a shift from swap lines as an Old Boys' Club financial stability tool to a quid-pro-quo geopolitical reward for physical-commodity allies.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highFederal ReserveUAEOPECOPEC+Kevin Warsh
So what: Countries that align with US energy statecraft gain access to the ultimate financial backstop; those that don't lose dollar liquidity insurance — a massive asymmetry favoring US-aligned commodity producers.
“swap lines... have now been offered to the UAE which have is itself very cashri has a currency pegged to the dollar and has an awful lot of energy and is no longer in OPEC and OPEC plus”
Kevin Warsh himself stated publicly that Fed swap lines are not subject to Fed independence because they are geopolitical transactions — giving the presidency and Treasury de facto control over swap line allocation.
data_point🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveUS Treasury
So what: Warsh's framing legally and institutionally opens the door for the White House to direct swap line policy; this undermines the traditional non-political use of swap lines and could be used as leverage in trade and energy negotiations.
“Walsh when he was asked what do you think about dollar swap lines... He gave an interesting answer where he alluded to the fact that he thought Fed independence didn't apply to them because they were a geopolitical transaction”
The US stablecoin strategy serves as a 'buy one get one free' dollar extension tool: countries willing to align with US tariff policy, technology supply chain separation from China, and defense cooperation will accept dollar-pegged stablecoins, which give the US free seigniorage and sort allies from adversaries.
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUSScott BessantDonald TrumpChina
So what: Stablecoin adoption by allied nations is a proxy indicator for deepening US economic alignment; companies building stablecoin infrastructure for cross-border settlement in allied nations are positioned at the intersection of finance and geopolitics.
“they also have a sorting effect and that the countries that will trade with you that will put tariffs on China and share a common external tariff with you against them and split their technology supply chains... They will be the ones who will accept them”
Canada's rhetoric shifted dramatically from Prime Minister Carney saying in January 2026 'we're in favor of a new world order working with China' to saying in late May 2026 'we want to form a new partnership with the US to make America great again' — Every attributes this to the coercive power of the looming energy crisis logic.
data_point🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highCanadaMark CarneyUSChina
So what: Demonstrates that economic statecraft coercion is already reshaping allied country positions without military action; similar pivots could emerge from other wavering allies under energy-crisis pressure.
“you had the prime minister, you know, Carney saying that we're in favor of a new world order working with China... to last week saying we want to form a new partnership with the US to make America great again. And that's a direct quote.”
Federal Reserve transformation under Kevin Warsh 5
Every argues that an independent central bank targeting 2% CPI is logically incompatible with a state pursuing economic statecraft; he expects the Fed under Warsh, who was backed for the role by Bessant and Trump, to gradually align with the statecraft agenda.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshScott BessantDonald Trump
So what: Markets that price Fed policy as an independent technocratic process are missing the regime shift; rate and QE decisions may increasingly reflect geopolitical objectives rather than inflation targeting alone.
“The Fed will from my particular perspective of global strategy gradually be moving more towards the economic statecraft field”
Every anticipates the Warsh Fed may introduce sector-specific interest rates or balance sheet support — e.g., lower rates or QE for physical-economy sectors (mining, ports, shipbuilding, military supply chains) while withdrawing support from financial-sector assets.
prediction🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: A differentiated-rate or sectoral-QE framework would be a radical structural break for financial markets; sectors tied to physical-economy reindustrialization would outperform financial-asset-heavy sectors under this regime.
“The Fed might be having to think right do we really need to be offering QE style balance sheet support to the financial part of the economy which effectively doesn't do anything in terms of actual physical bang for the buck... do we need to be looking at some kind of acronym tacit balance sheet support... for the physical economy”
There is a fundamental contradiction in Warsh's stated agenda: he wants to reduce the Fed balance sheet while Trump wants to expand the Pentagon budget by $500 billion next year — Every frames this as an unresolved square-circle problem.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshDonald TrumpPentagon
So what: The fiscal-monetary tension implies either the Fed balance sheet reduction fails (bullish risk assets, dollar negative) or the defense expansion is unfunded (bearish bonds), with limited room for both simultaneously.
“Walsh is openly talking about trying to reduce the Fed balance sheet which itself would really set the cat among the pigeons... at the same time Trump is talking about expanding the Pentagon budget next year by $500 billion well how do you square that circle”
Every's broader critique of central banking: existing models were built for demand-management and cannot process structural supply-side shocks; he compares the Fed's fitness for current conditions to 'a fish understanding a bicycle,' meaning no central bank rate policy is the correct solution to an energy supply shock.
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highFederal ReserveECBBOJ
So what: Investors should not rely on central bank intervention to stabilize markets during the Hormuz crisis; the policy backstop that has historically capped downside does not apply to supply-side structural shocks.
“like central banks understand supply side or structural supply side shocks like a fish understands a bicycle. They're just entirely different things.”
Warsh's public statements before taking the Fed chair position focused on AI being very deflationary, implying rate cuts ahead — making the Iran supply-side inflation shock a direct intellectual curveball for his stated thesis.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: A Warsh Fed caught between AI-deflation thesis (dovish) and energy-supply inflation (hawkish) may produce erratic, contradictory signals — watch for unusual Fed communication inconsistency as a regime-transition signal.
“he wanted to change the Fed... looking at the fact that AI in his mind from in public statements is likely to be very deflationary and as a result, you know, there's room for the rate cuts there”
Geopolitical outcomes: Iran 'loss' scenarios and US credibility 5
Every defines a US 'loss' in the Iran conflict as a 'taco' (full retreat) — not military defeat but geostrategic capitulation where Trump withdraws the blockade, leaving Iran controlling Hormuz; he frames this as a potential 1956-Suez-Crisis-level demotion for the US from hyperpower to merely 'a power.'
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUSIranStrait of HormuzDonald TrumpEdward Luttwak
So what: A US tactical retreat would reprice geopolitical risk premiums globally, reduce confidence in US security guarantees to Gulf allies, and likely push them toward Iranian accommodation — structurally bullish energy prices and bearish US dollar credibility.
“what it would do at a meta level is show that even when you have a very ostensibly gung-ho president like Trump... the US would be in a state that uh the geostrategist Edward Ludback refers to as posh heroic”
A US retreat from Hormuz would likely force Gulf states (UAE, Saudi Arabia) to negotiate terms with Iran despite hostility, but not unconditionally — UAE and Israel would likely continue covert operations, meaning structurally high energy prices could persist even post-retreat.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumUAEIsraelIranSaudi ArabiaStrait of Hormuz
So what: Even the 'benign' US retreat scenario doesn't produce clean energy-price normalization; the Gulf remains contested, maintaining a geopolitical risk premium in oil.
“Countries that are permanently based there may not accept the fact that that happens and say we're not going to give up”
Every argues the US is 'post-heroic' (casualty-phobic) in the Luttwak sense — possessing a technologically superior but politically constrained military that cannot achieve all its geostrategic objectives, as demonstrated by inability to decisively defeat Iran without ground troops that have no domestic political support.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUSIranIsraelHamas
So what: Investors should assign non-trivial probability to US de-escalation or retreat even under Trump; the administration's military maximalism is constrained by casualty-aversion, limiting the upside of betting on full military resolution.
