“the US is so casualty phobic that while it has this magnificent technological military machine... It's limited in what it can achieve. It can do a lot, but it can't achieve everything it needs to.”
Trump's Labor Day comment ('the blockade could be lifted as soon as Labor Day') is treated by Both Johnston and Townsend as a highly significant tell — confirming a 3-month timeline for the crisis baseline, not an imminent resolution.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highDonald TrumpStrait of HormuzIran
So what: Trump's own framing gives the crisis a ~95-day runway (to Labor Day ~Sept 1, 2026), over which inventory buffers will be seriously depleted — pricing should be building in a mid-Q3 2026 crunch.
“President Trump even made the comment that the US blockade of Iran could be lifted as soon as Labor Day. As soon as Labor Day. That's 95 days from now, more than a quarter of a year away.”
Iran's consistent position is maintaining 'nuclear threshold state' status — possessing enriched uranium sufficient to build a weapon if desired but not having made that decision — while the US/Israel demand surrender of enriched material; this fundamental gap makes a near-term deal structurally unlikely.
thesis🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highIranIsraelUSDonald Trump
So what: Trump's repeated 'Iran agreed to no nukes' tweets are not new information; the sticking point is enriched uranium ownership, not a weapons-program pledge — traders taking those tweets as deal signals are being deliberately misled.
“Iran agreed to no nuclear weapon years ago. That hasn't changed... What they've said consistently is they do not have nor do they intend to develop a nuclear weapon. However, they are not willing to give up their enriched uranium”
Gold, dollar, and asset-market implications 7
Gold has been anomalously weak during the Iran conflict — failing to rally on missile strikes — which Every partially explains by Middle Eastern high-net-worth individuals selling gold for dollars to flee the region, finding fiat currency more portable in an active war zone than physical metals.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumGoldMiddle EastUSD
So what: The gold weakness is partly structural (war-zone liquidation) not just macro (rising yields/dollar); this selling pressure dissipates post-conflict, which could be a catalyst for gold's recovery.
“people were finding that they were having to sell gold for dollars to try and then move them abroad and then buy other assets again. So when it actually came push came to shove in the Middle East you actually had missiles flying in a region which historically likes gold a lot.”
Patrick Szersna identifies gold's 200-day moving average at ~$4,415 as the critical technical support; if that level breaks, he expects an accelerated move lower and does not see a catalyst for gold bulls until Q3 2026 at the earliest.
data_point🗣 Patrick Szersnaconviction: mediumGold
So what: Technical and macro headwinds (strengthening dollar, rising yields) together with war-liquidation selling argue for staying underweight gold until the Iran resolution clarifies the geopolitical architecture.
“So far, the 200 day moving average has held when we saw that test right around 4,400... I won't be surprised though if the Iran situation continues to persist if that does not hold.”
Every argues the dollar cannot be abandoned by the US because the eurodollar system grants enormous power, but that same system de-industrialized the US by incentivizing financial asset accumulation over productive capacity — the statecraft agenda is to reform the dollar's structure while retaining its global role.
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUSDUSScott BessantDonald Trump
So what: The dollar is not being abandoned but re-engineered; investors should watch for instruments that extend dollar reach into physical-commodity networks (stablecoins, new swap lines) rather than just financial assets.
“clearly there's absolutely no appetite from Bess or from Trump to give up the global power of the dollar. But there's a full understanding that the Euro dollar system... is also responsible for de-industrializing it”
The S&P 500 rallied almost 20% trough-to-peak in roughly 2 months as of early June 2026, reaching ~7,553; Szersna flags the RSI as 'incredibly overbought' on short-term metrics with only four comparable rallies in 20 years, and warns of poor risk/reward for adding longs.
data_point🗣 Patrick Szersnaconviction: highS&P 500RSI
So what: Equity markets are pricing a benign oil/Iran outcome while commodity experts see a structural crisis; oil breaking above $100 is flagged as a likely catalyst for equity distribution/correction.
“Going back uh almost 20 years, there's only four times we've seen a rally of this magnitude. And certainly, you know, the market is stretched on almost every metric.”
10-year US Treasury yields were at 4.48% and appearing to have a path-of-least-resistance toward a 5% handle, with oil and inflation expectations driving the correlation; there is no identified bond-buying opportunity at current levels.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumUS Treasury10-year yield
So what: If oil breaks above $100, inflation expectations re-accelerate and could push the 10-year back to or above 5%, compressing equity multiples and increasing fiscal stress on the large US deficit.
“can we see a 5% handle on the 10-year yield. It's certainly at this point uh the path of least resistance the way these charts look at this stage.”
Copper was trading at 52-week highs (~$6.50/lb) with old dips being bought; Szersna identifies copper as the most bullish commodity chart with a potential run toward $7, consistent with industrial-reindustrialization demand themes.
data_point🗣 Patrick Szersnaconviction: mediumCopperLME
So what: Copper's strength while oil is contained suggests markets are positioning for physical-economy rebuild (infrastructure, defense, EVs) independent of the Iran resolution; long copper is an expression of the statecraft/re-industrialization thesis.
“copper markets, which continued to trade at 52- week highs... will be interesting to see whether or not copper can break to that fresh new high and make a gun for the seven level on the upside.”
Uranium was quiet and failing to participate in the broader commodity rally despite a pop on the Nuclear Fuels Conference that fully retraced; seasonally weak until late August, with a potential catalyst at the WNA conference (September 7–11, 2026).
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumUraniumWNA
So what: Long-term uranium bull thesis intact but near-term seasonal weakness and failure to participate in commodity rally suggests waiting for WNA conference as a re-entry catalyst.
“seasonally, this is a weak period until late August or so. Maybe we'll hold steady, but I don't think we should expect a big rally or all-time highs or any of that until we get into the seasonal buying period, which normally starts right around the WNA conference”
Physical-economy rebuild trade: PAVE ETF construction 2
Patrick Szersna's trade-of-the-week operationalizes Every's physical-economy rebuild thesis via a long position in the PAVE ETF (~$57.24) paired with a July 17, 2026 $55 put at ~$1.25 as a short-term hedge; total effective cost basis ~$58.49, defined short-term floor at $55 (~6% max risk through expiry).
framework🗣 Patrick Szersnaconviction: mediumPAVES&P 500
So what: Provides a specific, actionable structure to express the re-industrialization theme with capped near-term downside given an overbought market; the $55 strike aligns with the 50-day moving average (~$55.06) as a natural technical support.
“I'm looking at the July 17th, 2026 $55 put, which has roughly 43 days to expiration, which is trading around $1.25. That strike sits almost directly on the 50-day moving average currently at 5506”
Every's and Szersna's shared thesis is that policy is shifting from broad financial-asset QE support to targeted support for infrastructure, ports, shipbuilding, mining, supply chains, and strategic manufacturing — an 'entire generation of financial market participants' has never experienced a market where liquidity flows selectively to some sectors and not others.
contrarian🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highPAVEFederal ReserveUS
So what: Portfolios built for the 2010s QE regime (financial assets, tech, growth stocks as primary beneficiaries of Fed liquidity) face structural underperformance if selective sectoral liquidity becomes the new normal.
“I don't think people have in markets broadly got their heads around what it would mean to have extra liquidity that goes to some sectors, but it doesn't flow to all the favored ones, which is what an entire generation, if not two, of financial market participants have grown up expecting as normal.”

Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy 1 episode(s) · 45 insights

Why the AI Boom Is Just Getting Started80 min45 insights2026-06-09
▶ Watch on YouTube
Alex from Whale Rock Capital lays out the firm's conviction on Anthropic as its highest-conviction position, grounded in a thesis that enterprise AI is less than 1% penetrated and the foundational-model market has consolidated into a three-player oligopoly. The conversation is a master class on S-curve investing, covering when to buy and sell technology adoption cycles, the 'decommoditization' of the AI hardware supply chain, and why Whale Rock has sold almost all legacy enterprise software while going net short the sector.
Anthropic: Investment Thesis and Conviction · 6AI Foundational Model Oligopoly and Competitive Dynamics · 5Coding Market as AI's Primary Revenue Unlock · 4S-Curve Framework: Buying, Holding, and Selling Technology Cycles · 6Enterprise AI Penetration and the 'L-Curve' Demand Regime · 4AI Hardware Supply Chain: Decommoditization and Investment Opportunities · 10Enterprise Software Disruption: Short Thesis and AI-Native Risk · 5Private Markets Access, Portfolio Construction, and Firm Evolution · 5
Anthropic: Investment Thesis and Conviction 6
Whale Rock invested in Anthropic at a $180 million valuation in August 2025, when the company was targeting a $9 billion valuation; the investment has since produced returns described as 'a total home run' as revenue grew from roughly $100 million to $1 billion on the way to $9 billion.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicWhale Rock Capital
So what: The entry multiple and growth trajectory validate the firm's framework: buying before enterprise recognition and before the coding-market inflection crystallized was the edge.
≈ “we made the investment at the 180 valuation... the numbers were like nothing we'd ever seen before. 100 to a billion on the way to 9.” paraphrase
Whale Rock passed on Anthropic's $60 billion round, then re-engaged later after getting comfortable with management quality, near-zero employee turnover, and the coding-market explosion — then prepared a 90-page PowerPoint built using Claude Code to win allocation.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicWhale Rock CapitalClaude Code
So what: Illustrates the firm's relationship-driven process for accessing late-stage private rounds; using Claude Code to produce the pitch deck is itself a product testimonial.
“we did a 90-page PowerPoint deck where we used Claude Code to scour the internet for all the feedback we could about the coding market” ▶ 15:58
Anthropic's ~14–15 million DAUs represent only ~10 basis points of the world's knowledge workers, which Whale Rock characterizes as the 'tinkerer' phase of an S-curve — before crossing to early adopters at 1–5% penetration over the next four years.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicSundar Pichai
So what: Implies massive sequential growth in paid tokens ahead; any pricing power maintained through this inflection would compound revenue exponentially.
≈ “Sunder said it's 10 bips of the knowledge workers the world. So Anthropic has something like 14 or 15 million DAUs.” paraphrase
Anthropic has achieved 'escape velocity' in Whale Rock's view: 10x revenue growth and strong fundraising ability give it scale, brand recognition with CIOs, and critical IP that is differentiated from competitors (especially for finance and private-equity use cases).
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropic
So what: Escape velocity means the competitive moat is now compounding — late entrants face a capital-intensity bar that few can clear.
“with their 10x sales growth... it looks like they've reached escape velocity.” ▶ 37:43
Anthropic is building an ecosystem around its API — including the SDK, Claude for co-work, an orchestration layer, and a software 'harness' — analogous to how AWS evolved from commodity servers to a sticky platform with hundreds of proprietary products.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicAWSAmazon
So what: Platform lock-in from the harness layer could sustain margins even if base-model prices compress, similar to the margin structure AWS built above commodity compute.
“They're building a whole monopoly or whole ecosystem of products around the API... sort of a harness which is the software around the API that gets the most out of the model.” ▶ 9:11
Anthropic's recursive self-improvement loop — feeding Claude-generated code back into its own model training — is accelerating the pace of innovation; Whale Rock views this as a potential 'liftoff stage' for model capability.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: mediumAnthropic
So what: If the feedback loop holds, Anthropic's lead in coding benchmarks widens rather than erodes, making open-source catch-up harder over time.
“Anthropic now that they're leading in code, they set that code back onto their model and it's this concept of the recursive improvement.” ▶ 37:55
AI Foundational Model Oligopoly and Competitive Dynamics 5
The foundational-model market evolved from ~60 competing startups in 2023 to a three-horse race among OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google Gemini — mirroring how cloud computing consolidated around three hyperscalers that now underpin all of SaaS.
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGoogleGeminiAmazonMeta
So what: Oligopoly structure suggests pricing power is sustainable; the three incumbents can invest in R&D at a rate that precludes new entrants and limits open-source leapfrogging.
≈ “It really started to look like a three-horse race and somewhat of an oligopoly very similar to how the cloud market evolved.” paraphrase
Open-source AI models cannot leapfrog frontier labs because they lack the compute to close the last 5 percentage points of benchmark quality — going from 80% to 85% is described as a 'huge unlock' that open-source consistently fails to achieve.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGoogle
So what: Sustained benchmark gaps justify enterprise procurement of proprietary models; the open-source risk that spooked early investors looks structurally limited.
≈ “going from 80 to 85 is a huge unlock and the open-source guys they don't have as much compute so they can come close to the leading edge but they can't leapfrog it.” paraphrase
AI foundational models are not commodities despite the existence of routing layers that switch between them; each model has differentiated strengths (Anthropic excels at finance and private equity; Google at PDF ingestion), which creates durable switching costs.
contrarian🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicGoogleGeminiOpenAI
So what: Model differentiation means market share is not purely price-driven, supporting margin structure and arguing against the race-to-zero scenario many analysts fear.
“anthropic, they're very good for anything that has to do with private equity and finance. Google's very good for ingesting PDF.” ▶ 8:39
Meta attempted to compete in the foundational-model layer but 'basically their effort faltered and they had to do a total reboot,' and Amazon 'really never really showed up' — reinforcing the view that only the current top three are viable long-term.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: mediumMetaAmazonAnthropicOpenAIGoogle
So what: Failure of two of the world's largest companies narrows the competitive threat; the moat is more durable than feared even for well-resourced challengers.
The key risk to the foundational-model investment thesis is a plateau in model improvement: if scaling laws stall, open-source models would catch up and the market could devolve into a race to zero that destroys the stocks while still benefiting chip companies.
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAINvidia
So what: Investors should monitor benchmark progression as a leading indicator of moat preservation; any multi-quarter stall in frontier model improvements is a material sell signal.
≈ “If anthropic sort of hits a wall and stops improving or open AI then the open source models will catch up and it might be a race to the bottom.” paraphrase
Coding Market as AI's Primary Revenue Unlock 4
The agentic coding market alone represents a ~$500 billion TAM: with 20 million coders globally each spending $20,000–$30,000 per year on tokens at current usage rates, and this estimate was based on 7–9 month old technology at time of investment.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicClaude Code
So what: Even a fraction of this TAM accruing to Anthropic justifies the current valuation; the estimate was constructed before the most recent model capability jumps, implying it is likely understated.
“if you think about how many coders there are in the world, 20 million, you've got a half a trillion dollar market just from coding alone.” ▶ 6:22
Andrej Karpathy and Linus Torvalds both reversed their skepticism about AI coding tools: Karpathy stated last year that AI wrote 20% of code with 80% handwritten, but after the latest model release he has not written a single line of code except in English.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicClaude CodeAndrej KarpathyLinus Torvalds
So what: Two of the most credible validators of coding quality have flipped; if the most expert coders are deferring to AI, enterprise adoption of agentic coding is likely to accelerate rapidly.
≈ “Karpathy said you know last year's code tools could write 20% and 80% would be handwritten that flipped when the latest model came out and now he hasn't written a line of code not except in English.” paraphrase
Within Anthropic, individual employees were spending $100 per day on tokens at time of Whale Rock's investment — implying $20,000–$30,000 per year per user, and this spending was on model technology that was already 7–9 months old.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropic
So what: Internal usage intensity is a leading indicator of external enterprise adoption curves; a $100/day run rate inside the company validated product-market fit before wider release.
“people were spending $100 a day on tokens which if you do the math comes out to 20 or $30,000 a year.” ▶ 6:14
Claude Code has transitioned to being 'almost completely agentic' — a qualitative step-change from the first generation (Microsoft Copilot at ~$20/month that could fix bugs and write code blocks) to fully autonomous coding workflows.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicClaude CodeMicrosoftCopilot
So what: Agentic capability drives order-of-magnitude increases in token consumption per user; revenue scales with task complexity, not merely seat count.
“claude code has gone to almost completely agentic” ▶ 7:14
S-Curve Framework: Buying, Holding, and Selling Technology Cycles 6
Whale Rock's three-part investment framework is: (1) identify the right position on the S-curve for exponential unit growth, (2) find the company with the strongest competitive advantage within that S-curve, and (3) buy when long-term earnings power is underappreciated — enabling purchases at apparent P/Es of 4–5x on future earnings (Nvidia in 2023 at 4x, Tesla in 2019 at 5x, Apple at 4x, AWS gotten 'for free').
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highNvidiaTeslaAppleAmazonAWSWhale Rock Capital
So what: The framework systematically exploits the market's inability to think exponentially; it explains why seemingly expensive tech can be bought at historically low earnings multiples if the S-curve is correctly mapped.
≈ “When we were buying Nvidia in 2023, we were paying four times earnings. When we bought Tesla in 2019 for the car s-curve, we were paying five times earnings.” paraphrase
The general sell signal is when S-curve penetration reaches 30–40%, at which point exponential growth gives way to linear growth, sell-side consensus catches up, and earnings beats disappear — Apple was sold around 50% US smartphone penetration.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highApple
So what: For investors using S-curve frameworks, tracking absolute penetration rates provides a concrete exit trigger independent of valuation multiples.
≈ “generally we like the high growth... we sold in 2012 when it got to sort of 50% of the US had a smartphone.” paraphrase
Andy Grove's principle that strategic inflection points cannot be trusted to quantitative data alone — they require intuition and anecdotal pattern recognition — is a core operating principle; examples include spotting mobile gaming's inflection from watching a child play in China and spotting AWS's inflection from standing-room-only sessions at Gartner IT Symposium.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAndy GroveAWSGartner
So what: Primary-source qualitative checks (conferences, customer conversations, watching actual usage) are non-replicable edges that quantitative investors systematically miss.
“when you have strategic inflection points, you can't trust the data... strategic inflection points are about intuition, anecdotal evidence.” ▶ 26:42
Consumer-facing technologies adopt like a radio (7 years to near-100% penetration); B2B technologies adopt like a dishwasher (slow because they must be 'plugged into' existing enterprise systems) — AI is unusual because browser-based access makes it more like radio despite being B2B.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: high
So what: AI's frictionless access model (open a browser and it is there) removes the main historical brake on B2B adoption speed, supporting the 'L-curve' rather than S-curve trajectory thesis.
≈ “with AI you just at least with consumers or even business you just open up the browser and it's there... that's why we're getting this straight up.” paraphrase
Missing the first 1–3 years of an S-curve is acceptable because if the top of the curve is half a trillion dollars, growth continues long enough to generate large returns even from a late entry — Peter Lynch's advice was to 'white out the chart' and focus on the future.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highPeter Lynch
So what: Removes the psychological pressure to catch inflections perfectly; investors should focus on confirming the TAM ceiling and competitive position rather than entry-timing precision.
≈ “It's okay to miss the first 100%. Peter Lynch... said, 'White out the chart. It's all about the future.'” paraphrase ▶ 28:43
The EV S-curve hit a wall at 10–15% penetration rather than continuing toward 40–50%, demonstrating that S-curves can stall and must be actively monitored; Whale Rock views this as a cautionary case requiring continuous thesis re-evaluation.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highTesla
So what: Not all S-curves complete; the EV example argues for setting explicit penetration thresholds and exiting positions when adoption evidence diverges from the original underwrite.
≈ “at the time we thought probably maybe 40 to 50% of the cars would go electric, but it did hit a big wall at 10 or 15%.” paraphrase
Enterprise AI Penetration and the 'L-Curve' Demand Regime 4
Whale Rock characterizes the enterprise AI application market as less than 1% penetrated and calls the demand trajectory an 'L-curve' (straight up from the base) rather than a traditional S-curve, because browser-based access eliminated the friction that historically slowed B2B adoption.
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAIGoogle
So what: If the L-curve thesis holds, revenue forecasts built on traditional S-curve slopes will systematically understate growth for the next 2–4 years, creating persistent consensus beats.
“the enterprise AI or enterprise application AI market is less than 1% penetrated and we've never seen... we call this an L curve, just straight up.” ▶ 0:26
The AI infrastructure layer is itself only ~10 basis points penetrated — and is already sold out; there is not enough compute in the world, with Anthropic having only half the compute it needs before the large wave of enterprise adoption has even begun.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicNvidia
So what: Structural compute shortage means capex spend at hyperscalers and independent data centers is likely to remain elevated far longer than consensus models; chip supply chains are the binding constraint.
“Anthropic has half of what they need right now and that's before this huge takeup. So Mark Andre said in the next four years one thing he's sure of is there's not going to be enough compute.” ▶ 12:12
The 2025 'light switch moment' in enterprise AI occurred when CIOs broadly recognized the need to move now; Whale Rock compares it to knowing you needed a website in 1998 — urgency is high even if implementation is still early.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Enterprise budget prioritization is already shifting toward AI tokens over legacy software licenses; this accelerates the software disruption thesis and expands the token TAM.
“a light switch this year went off in the enterprise where everybody realizes they need to do this now and do it fast.” ▶ 11:20
AI adoption pace could face a cultural bottleneck in large enterprises: security concerns, slow IT approval processes, lack of internal evangelists, and cultural resistance are the same structural friction that delayed cloud adoption — suggesting the B2B ramp may be uneven despite the L-curve thesis.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Enterprise software and systems-integration companies with AI expertise could become unexpected beneficiaries if large companies need outside help navigating adoption barriers.
“you really need a few evangelists to push it through and the top management needs to push it through but then the IT's saying this is this is risky.” ▶ 30:46
AI Hardware Supply Chain: Decommoditization and Investment Opportunities 10
AI workloads are growing 10x per year and pushing every component of the server to physical limits, triggering what Whale Rock calls the 'decommoditization of the hardware industry' — after 40 years of commoditization, every layer from PCBs to memory to networking is now requiring intensive innovation and generating pricing power.
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highNvidiaBroadcomIntel
So what: Supply-chain companies that previously traded at commodity multiples are re-rating to technology multiples; the opportunity is widest in components that are still misclassified by generalist analysts.
“you go to AI and the workloads are growing 10x every year and they're pushing every single aspect of this hardware to the physical limits of what it can do... we call it the decommoditization of the hardware industry.” ▶ 49:38
Celestica, a contract manufacturer originally dismissed as a commodity business, turned out to be the sole supplier of the Google TPU server and holds 50–60% share of cloud Ethernet switching — discovered when the stock was trading at 8x earnings three years ago.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highCelesticaGoogleBroadcomNvidia
So what: The pattern of hidden competitive advantages inside 'commodity' supply chain companies is repeating across the AI infrastructure build-out; deep supply-chain research produces asymmetric opportunities.
“we noticed they were the sole supplier of the Google TPU server. We're like, 'Oh my god, this was like three years ago. The stock was trading at eight times earnings.'” ▶ 51:33
High-bandwidth memory (HBM) — now stacked 10 chips high with 10x the I/O of prior generations — took Samsung years to develop and is a critical, constantly upgrading component designed in coordination with Nvidia three to four chip generations in advance.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highSamsungNvidiaSK Hynix
So what: HBM suppliers like SK Hynix have multi-year design-in relationships that create durable revenue visibility; this argues against treating memory as a commodity cycle play.
“this high bandwidth memory is stacked 10 chips on top... the input outputs are 10x what they were before... took Samsung for years to do it and it's a critical critical piece.” ▶ 50:43
Advanced PCBs (printed circuit boards) for AI servers require 40 layers versus 10 for a standard server, with very few suppliers capable of producing them; PCB units, layer counts, and ASPs are all growing simultaneously, producing Whale Rock's estimate of a 50–60% CAGR in that market.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highElite Materials
So what: PCB companies with AI-server exposure are among the most under-owned AI infrastructure plays; the triple-compound growth driver (units + layers + ASP) is rarely modeled correctly by sell-side analysts.
≈ “a regular server you need 10 layers. These AI servers you need a 40 layer and there's very few PCB suppliers that can make this... you've got like a 50 to 60% kager just in the units and then the ASPs are rising.” paraphrase ▶ 53:32
Corning dominates fiber supply for data centers — a single Microsoft data center contains enough fiber to circle the world 4.5 times — and faces a potential 2–3x revenue uplift when GPU-to-GPU connections within racks migrate from copper to fiber ('scale up' networking).
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highCorningMicrosoft
So what: Corning is a classic 'picks and shovels' compounder with high market share in a capacity-constrained market; the intra-rack copper-to-fiber migration is a discrete, time-bounded catalyst that could double the addressable market.
≈ “I was reading this Microsoft data center they just built. There's enough fiber to circle the world four and a half times in that one thing... when that happens that two to three X's Corning's opportunity.” paraphrase
Power supply ASPs are rising 40% per year for multiple years because each Nvidia chip or rack uses 50–125% more power than prior-generation hardware, directly driving unit economics for Delta and Advanced Energy.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highNvidiaDelta ElectronicsAdvanced Energy
So what: Power supply companies exposed to AI data centers have a multi-year compounding ASP tailwind that is structurally different from cyclical hardware demand; margins should expand alongside revenue.
≈ “every Nvidia chip or rack uses 50 to 125% more power... that drives the ASPs of Delta and Advanced Energy... your ASPs are going to like go up 40% for the next four years in a row and it's higher margin.” paraphrase
AI servers cost $200,000–$300,000 each versus $5,000 for a traditional server; if one fails the entire system goes down, making the server manufacturer mission-critical infrastructure that cannot be swapped out — dramatically improving pricing power and customer retention.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highCelesticaNvidia
So what: Server assembly and liquid cooling expertise becomes a durable moat rather than a commodity skill; sole-source relationships have the stickiness of aerospace component suppliers.
“You'll never get swapped out. And then they they it turned out they were quite good at liquid cooling and you know a lot of other people tried to do it and failed.” ▶ 52:29
The broader AI infrastructure supply chain — DRAM, NAND, PCBs — is already running at a 30% structural shortage versus current demand, before the L-curve enterprise adoption wave has even begun.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highSamsungSK HynixMicron
So what: Even without further demand acceleration, existing shortages will sustain elevated pricing across memory and PCB markets; a supply response will take 2–3 years to close the gap.
≈ “We're already short... the DRAM market, the NAND market, the PCB. We're already like 30% short all these things as we are now.” paraphrase ▶ 56:44
Whale Rock uses a modified 'Rule of 40' for evaluating hardware companies in the AI cycle: (% of revenue that is AI) + (market share in that AI category) = score; a company with 30% AI revenue and 30% market share scores 60, which is a high-quality starting point.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: high
So what: This screen rapidly identifies which hardware companies have both scale and positioning in the AI cycle, filtering out commodity players that have revenue exposure without defensible share.
“if what percent of your sales are AI, say 30%, and what's your market share in that category? Say 30%. You'd be 60. That's a great place to look.” ▶ 45:52
Ethernet upgrade cycles in AI data centers have compressed from 7-year intervals (100G → 400G → 800G) to annual upgrades; Celestica's team co-authored the open-source SONiC networking software that runs these white-box switches, creating IP-based lock-in inside a market share of 50–60%.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highCelesticaBroadcom
So what: Annual upgrade cadence means Celestica generates near-recurring revenue in what was historically a lumpy capital equipment market; SONiC co-authorship is a sticky IP moat that is hard for competitors to replicate.
≈ “They're upgrading every year and that's really hard to do. The guys at Celestica invented were some of the people that wrote that open-source software.” paraphrase
Enterprise Software Disruption: Short Thesis and AI-Native Risk 5
Whale Rock sold almost all of its enterprise software holdings and entered 2025 net short the sector, a position that 'really helped in the first quarter'; five years ago software was 40–50% of the portfolio.
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highSalesforceWhale Rock Capital
So what: One of the most active long-only/long-short tech managers has a net short position in legacy enterprise software — a strong sell signal for the sector heading into the enterprise AI adoption wave.
≈ “We basically sold almost all of our software, almost all of our application software... entering this year, we were actually net net short. And it really helped us in the first quarter.” paraphrase ▶ 41:31
Enterprise software faces a four-pronged headwind from AI: (1) AI tokens are displacing software on CIO priority lists, (2) token spending compresses software budgets, (3) vendors are now afraid to raise prices, and (4) agentic AI could reduce seat counts as companies cut headcount or freeze hiring.
causal🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highSalesforceWorkdaySlack
So what: Multi-vector pressure on software revenue means even bull cases require heroic AI-driven offsetting growth that most incumbents have not yet demonstrated.
Salesforce has ~$40 billion in annual sales but only ~$500–700 million in AI ARR — less than 2% penetration — making the base so large that even strong AI growth takes years to move overall revenue growth rates, while the core business faces competitive pressure.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highSalesforce
So what: Salesforce's AI revenue is a rounding error on its base, yet the stock is priced for significant AI upside; the risk-reward favors being short rather than waiting for AI to rescue the multiple.
≈ “if you're a company like Salesforce, you've got 40 billion in sales and now you might have 500 of ARR 700 of AR of AI. So you've got this huge base.” paraphrase
There is a potential bull case for incumbent software with strong network effects: AI agents may operate inside existing platforms (Slack, Workday, CRM) rather than replacing them, solidifying the platform as the system of record for agent activity — Whale Rock describes this as 'half-baked' but worth monitoring.
contrarian🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: lowSlackWorkdaySalesforce
So what: The agent-inside-incumbent thesis is a low-conviction alternative to the pure disruption thesis; it would require AI agents adopting a tool-use model rather than replacing the software outright.
“maybe these agents, maybe the next wave of AI will be these agents that use tools and they might operate inside of the existing incumbent software tools to use them like a human being would.” ▶ 46:36
The AI application layer is still unclear and 'a little bit dangerous' for investment; Whale Rock is watching Brett Taylor's Sierra as the highest-quality example of an AI-native enterprise application company but is not invested, preferring to wait until the ecosystem clarifies.
thesis🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: mediumSierraBrett TaylorSalesforceGoogle
So what: The firm's explicit restraint from application-layer investing signals that the winner-identification problem at that layer is not yet solved; the opportunity is real but premature for conviction bets.
“we really were watching Brett Taylor at Sierra... he's building this fantastic company called Sierra... It usually doesn't start in the first 3 or 4 years. It comes a little bit later.” ▶ 1:04:46
Private Markets Access, Portfolio Construction, and Firm Evolution 5
The private unicorn market is now larger than most European stock markets individually — definitely larger than Germany and the UK — making direct private market participation a prerequisite for understanding and investing in the most important technology companies.
data_point🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highStripeAnthropicOpenAIDatabricks
So what: Asset allocators without private exposure are systematically underweight the largest and fastest-growing technology businesses; the case for a dedicated private technology sleeve is structural.
“the unicorn market is bigger than most stock markets in Europe, maybe even combined. It's definitely bigger than Germany. It's definitely bigger than the UK.” ▶ 16:34
Whale Rock bought a $100 million secondary block in Stripe during COVID in April 2020 at a ~$35 billion valuation, having inferred the business quality (TPV, take rate, margins) from public disclosures and channel checks without full financials; the actual take rate and TPV (~$1 trillion) were both higher than assumed.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highStripeWhale Rock Capital
So what: Demonstrates the research-driven mosaic approach to private market valuation; deep channel-check work on adjacent companies (Adyen in this case) can substitute for full financial disclosure.
“we didn't have the full financials, but we knew enough that at that valuation, I think it was 35 billion... It turned out the take rate was higher. It turned out they were being modest about their TPV.” ▶ 18:26
Whale Rock launched a Mega Cap Tech Fund focused on the top 30 global market caps, selecting 12–13 of the best, explicitly targeting the large structural underweight of institutional investors (endowments, hedge funds) in the largest technology companies.
anecdote🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highWhale Rock CapitalNvidiaTSMCSK HynixASML
So what: Institutional demand for large-cap tech concentrated exposure is growing; the product targets a systematic allocation gap that has cost endowments returns over the past decade.
“we created the Whale Rock Mega Cap Tech Fund which is the top 30 the universe is the top 30 market caps globally and then we pick you know the 12 or 13 that are the best.” ▶ 1:13:43
Whale Rock conducts 2,500–3,000 face-to-face meetings with management teams per year (10–15% with private companies) and describes its edge as a 'learning machine' compounding 20 years of technology sector knowledge using Philip Fisher's scuttlebutt approach.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highWhale Rock CapitalPhilip Fisher
So what: The firm's competitive advantage is relationship density and experiential depth, not quantitative screening; this confirms that primary research intensity is still the most defensible edge in long-term technology investing.
≈ “we do 2500 3,000 face-to-face meetings with management teams a year... We've been compounding that knowledge for 20 years.” paraphrase
AI tools at Whale Rock are enhancing but not yet supplanting analyst work: AI can rapidly get analysts up to speed on new technical areas (e.g., ABF substrates, PCBs) and write better factual notes, but cannot replace the judgment layer — identifying what has changed, assessing management quality, or developing conviction through relationships.
framework🗣 Alex (Whale Rock Capital)conviction: highWhale Rock CapitalAnthropic
So what: Firms that combine AI-enhanced research productivity with deep relationship networks will widen their edge over pure quantitative or pure fundamental shops; the bottleneck has shifted from information gathering to interpretation.
≈ “the AI can be a great reporter. It can't quite pick into the future.” paraphrase

Adam Taggart | Thoughtful Money 1 episode(s) · 40 insights

Extreme Valuations + Rising Volatility = 'Wild Ride' Ahead For Markets | Jonathan Wellum72 min40 insights2026-06-09
▶ Watch on YouTube
Jonathan Wellum of Rocklink Investment Partners argues that the US market faces a 'wild ride' ahead due to extreme valuations, AI-capex overbuilding parallels to 2000, and sovereign debt pressures, while recommending rotation into cheap insurers, commodities, and picks-and-shovels infrastructure. He endorses Trump's supply-side economic policies as directionally correct but warns the intractable global debt crisis limits their impact. The Iran conflict is seen as transitory with energy prices likely to fall, while gold and silver remain core portfolio hedges.
Market Volatility & Stretched Valuations · 6AI Overbuilding Risk & Correction Playbook · 4SpaceX IPO & Mega-IPO Caution · 5Rocklink Portfolio Positioning: Specific Holdings · 8Precious Metals, Gold & Commodities · 7Iran Conflict & Energy Price Outlook · 4Macro Policy: US Debt, Trump Economics & Global Growth · 4Investor Advice & Portfolio Construction · 2
Market Volatility & Stretched Valuations 6
Jonathan Wellum identifies five parallel conditions between today's market and the 2000 tech bubble: explosive capex spending, sky-high valuations, market concentration among a few players, speculative FOMO fever, and uncertainty about earnings delivery and potential overbuild.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highNASDAQAIhyperscalers
So what: A five-for-five match with 2000 conditions is a systematic checklist for timing defensive repositioning; investors should track which conditions are deteriorating fastest.
≈ “explosive capex spending, sky-high valuations, market concentration amongst players, speculative fever, FOMO investing, and then the fifth is uncertainty in terms of the earnings delivery and the potential overbuild that that we're seeing the same five things” paraphrase
The NASDAQ dropped 78% from its 2000 peak of ~5,100 and took 15 years to recover that level; Wellum explicitly invokes this as a non-trivial risk scenario for current AI-driven tech valuations.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumNASDAQ
So what: If a comparable drawdown occurs in AI-concentrated indices, an investor holding at-peak would need 15+ years to break even — underscoring the asymmetric risk of buying frothy names.
“when things peaked in 2000, the NASDAQ hit about 5100 and then it collapsed by 78% and it took 15 years to get back to 5100 again” ▶ 11:23
A strong US jobs report on the recording day was interpreted by markets as reducing the probability of Fed rate cuts and possibly signaling the need for hikes, triggering a sell-off in high-valuation tech stocks.
causal🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highFederal ReserveUS labor markettech stocks
So what: The 'good news is bad news' dynamic is alive in this market; continued labor-market resilience will keep rate-cut hopes in check and sustain pressure on high-multiple tech.
Wellum argues the market's perverse reaction to good jobs numbers is fundamentally wrong: private-sector growth paired with capital inflows, deregulation, and productivity gains should tame inflation over time rather than ignite it.
contrarian🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highFederal ReserveScott BessentKevin Warsh
So what: If Wellum is right, the Fed is operating on a stale Keynesian framework and may hold rates too high for too long, creating a policy error risk that could eventually trigger a sharp reversal.
“if you're actually driving the private sector and growth in the private sector and shrinking the public sector and you're having a massive amount of capital coming into your country so that there's increasing productivity that does not need to lead to inflation” ▶ 6:22
Hyperscalers are collectively proposing to spend approximately $1 trillion on AI capex in the near term; to justify a 15% return on equity, they would need to generate $150 billion in incremental profit — a figure Wellum treats as a serious arithmetic challenge.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highGoogleAmazonMicrosofthyperscalersAI
So what: The $1T capex / $150B required ROE math is a simple but powerful valuation stress-test; failure to deliver would trigger large-scale write-downs and equity repricing across the AI complex.
≈ “if you think of a trillion dollars which is what they're the hyperscalers are proposed to be spending this year next year uh in the trillion dollar range you know to gen to justify that they should be generating just say 15% return on equity that's $150 billion of profit” paraphrase
Hyperscalers have transitioned from low-capital, high-free-cash-flow businesses into high-capital-intensity businesses, fundamentally altering their operating models just as competitive pressure forces all of them to accelerate spending simultaneously.
causal🗣 Adam Taggartconviction: highGoogleAmazonMicrosofthyperscalers
So what: The forced capex race means even the best-managed hyperscaler cannot credibly signal restraint; capital allocation decisions are being driven by competitive fear rather than NPV analysis.
≈ “if you are in that cohort of companies you can't afford not to play right so if Google is going you know full bore on this and you're Amazon you've got to follow suit because the prize is so big that if your competitor were to take it your business model would be existentially at risk” paraphrase
AI Overbuilding Risk & Correction Playbook 4
If AI fails to deliver near-term earnings growth at the scale priced in, Wellum expects the AI-pure-plays and AI-adjacent names (like Anthropic) to drop substantially, dragging the broader market down, while Google and Amazon may hold up better given their large underlying cash-flow businesses.
prediction🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumAnthropicGoogleAmazonAI
So what: Diversified hyperscalers are a better risk-adjusted play than AI-pure-plays heading into a potential valuation reset; avoid names whose entire valuation rests on unproven AI revenue.
≈ “the anthropics and some of the AI companies and some of the other derivations if you will of the AI spend would drop a lot and I think it would take largely the whole market down for a period of time” paraphrase
Post the 2000-01 NASDAQ collapse, the best-performing sectors were energy, materials, consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare — all of which have low correlation to AI spending and are historically cheap relative to tech today.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highenergy sectormaterials sectorconsumer staplesutilitieshealthcare
So what: Historical sector rotation playbook after a tech bust points to commodities and defensives; positioning in these sectors now provides both a hedge and a potential recovery trade.
≈ “best performing sectors were energy, materials, uh consumer staples, utilities and healthcare after that draw down” paraphrase
AI ROI must justify itself at the 'grassroots level' across the entire global economy — i.e., Main Street businesses must actually pay for and benefit from AI services — before SpaceX and hyperscaler valuations can be sustained.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highSpaceXAIhyperscalers
So what: Watch enterprise AI adoption rates and corporate AI budget survey data as a leading indicator of whether the capex cycle can sustain itself or will reverse.
≈ “AI has to justify itself at the grassroots level right across all of the industry and all of the economy of our you know the global economy and I that still is yet to be seen how all that works out” paraphrase
AI will help offset dire demographic trends — particularly collapsing fertility rates (China at 0.87 TFR, North America just above 1) — through productivity enhancement and robotics, which provides one genuine long-term demand floor for AI investment.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumAIroboticsChina
So what: The demographic tailwind for AI-driven productivity is real and long-dated, making AI infrastructure a secular story even if there is a near-term valuation correction.
“the total fertility rates in China, you know, 0.87. I mean that means you're only replacing 42% of your people in every generational turn” ▶ 28:36
SpaceX IPO & Mega-IPO Caution 5
Large private companies including SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI are all slated for IPOs in 2026; Wellum believes this timing is at least partly explained by insiders wanting to monetize at peak valuations before market conditions deteriorate.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAI
So what: The wave of mega-IPOs hitting simultaneously is a classic late-cycle signal; IPO-window timing by sophisticated insiders with superior information is itself a contrarian sell-side indicator.
“I think there's a little bit of that. I think, well, maybe maybe a lot of that. Um, and so the markets are frothy. the excitement is there and these valuations are wonderful versus the book value” ▶ 15:09
Seymour Schulich (founder of Franco Nevada) told Wellum he 'seldom if ever' buys IPOs because 'the seller knows more than the buyer' — a heuristic Wellum applies directly to the current mega-IPO wave.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highFranco NevadaSeymour SchulichSpaceXAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Asymmetric information in IPOs is maximally acute for fast-changing, pre-profitability businesses like AI companies; retail investors should wait for 6–12 months of post-IPO price discovery.
≈ “the seller knows more than the buyer on IPOs, and I can never know enough about it” paraphrase
Ron Baron has publicly valued SpaceX at $10–20 trillion; Wellum cautions that a $20T valuation would require SpaceX to absorb a massive fraction of the global economy, representing serious execution and valuation risk.
contrarian🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highSpaceXElon MuskRon BaronBaron Funds
So what: The gap between Ron Baron's $20T target and the investable opportunity set means anyone buying SpaceX at or near IPO is implicitly funding the most optimistic possible scenario; calibrate position size accordingly.
≈ “for 20 trillion that has to absorb a massive amount of the economy to be able to justify that kind of valuation” paraphrase
Wellum's personal strategy for SpaceX is to wait for post-IPO price weakness rather than buy at the IPO price, citing average IPO underperformance at 6–12 months; he never recommends shorting Elon Musk but wants a better entry.
anecdote🗣 Adam Taggart / Jonathan Wellumconviction: highSpaceXElon Musk
So what: The optimal SpaceX strategy for long-term believers may be to let IPO euphoria fade, then buy into weakness — consistent with historical IPO return patterns.
≈ “that's my current strategy with SpaceX. I do actually want to own it, but I don't think I want to own it at the IPO price” paraphrase
Non-professionals (politicians, people entirely outside capital markets) are approaching Wellum at conferences asking how to get a piece of SpaceX — which Wellum reads as a classic froth signal.
contrarian🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highSpaceX
So what: Retail FOMO from non-investors is a late-stage bubble signal; historically this pattern precedes the final blow-off top and subsequent correction.
≈ “I've had people come up to me...individuals who are in politics or other completely outside of the capital markets. They say, 'Jonathan, I got to get a piece of that SpaceX.' When I hear that, I think just take a deep breath” paraphrase ▶ 18:38
Rocklink Portfolio Positioning: Specific Holdings 8
Rocklink owns Amazon as a cheaper AI/cloud proxy versus SpaceX; Wellum values Amazon for its AWS business, underlying retail, and as an emerging space competitor (Blue Origin) that is not priced into the current valuation.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumAmazonAWSBlue OriginSpaceX
So what: Amazon offers AI/cloud exposure with a built-in valuation discount relative to pure-play AI names; the optionality of Blue Origin growth is essentially free.
≈ “they're a cheaper version in some senses...you're not really paying for it in Amazon” paraphrase
Rocklink purchased ServiceNow after it got 'hammered' and added more on the way down; thesis is that ServiceNow will benefit from AI rather than be disrupted by it, as it helps large organizations integrate complex systems.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumServiceNow
So what: AI integration middleware plays are a more defensible AI bet than model developers; ServiceNow-type companies benefit regardless of which LLM model wins.
Rocklink owns Schneider Electric as a picks-and-shovels AI infrastructure play in the utility/data center buildout, alongside comparable Eaton; both benefit from the energy infrastructure required for data centers.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumSchneider ElectricEaton
So what: Industrial electrical infrastructure companies are a lower-risk way to participate in AI capex growth with more predictable revenue streams than the hyperscalers themselves.
Rocklink holds Prologis for data center real estate exposure, Brookfield infrastructure companies for general infrastructure, and recently trimmed its Rolls-Royce position.
anecdote🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumPrologisBrookfieldRolls-Royce
So what: Data center REITs and infrastructure conglomerates offer additional indirect AI exposure with more stable cash flows than technology stocks.
Rocklink owns Rolls-Royce for its three-part diversification: ~50% aero engine (oligopoly/duopoly), ~25% defense (benefiting from increasing global military spend), and ~25% power systems (data center energy demand), all at a reasonable industrial multiple.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumRolls-Royce
So what: Rolls-Royce is a diversified way to play AI power demand, defense spending growth, and commercial aviation recovery at a valuation that does not yet reflect AI data center exposure.
“all three components will benefit from a lot of these technological changes that are taking place and have unique growth in each one” ▶ 40:40
Rocklink has approximately 15% of assets in specialty insurance — specifically Markel and Kinsale — during the current soft market, buying when top-line growth is absent but combined ratios are exceptionally low (some at 60–70%), implying 30–40% underwriting margins.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highMarkelKinsale
So what: Soft insurance markets with sub-75 combined ratios and growing book value are textbook value setups; when the market hardens these names should re-rate significantly and book value compounds in the interim.
“we've got one that's running 60 70% combined ratio. So they're making 30 40% margins” ▶ 35:47
Markel holds hidden value because two hedge funds are pressuring the company to separate its operating businesses from insurance, similar to the Berkshire Hathaway model — separation could unlock the conglomerate discount.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumMarkel
So what: Activist pressure on Markel's business separation is a potential near-term catalyst on top of the underlying insurance value; worth watching for corporate action.
≈ “they've got a couple hedge funds that are really after them to separate them because they're not getting that value. So there's a lot of hidden value actually in Markel” paraphrase
Rocklink owns Cameco for uranium exposure, treating nuclear power as a critical energy source for AI data centers over the next 5–10 years.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumCamecouraniumnuclear energy
So what: Nuclear is a multi-year secular AI-power beneficiary; Cameco is the most liquid pure-play uranium equity.
Precious Metals, Gold & Commodities 7
Wellum has held gold for 16 years as a hedge against an intractable global debt crisis and currency debasement; he views gold as 'the ultimate monetary asset' for preserving purchasing power as deficits grow across all major economies.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highgoldprecious metals
So what: Gold's role as a portfolio hedge strengthens as sovereign balance sheets deteriorate; Wellum treats the allocation as strategic rather than tactical.
“there's an intractable debt crisis globally and we cannot get out of this without a lot of pain and gold is going to be a hedge in our view to that pain” ▶ 45:22
Silver demand has exceeded production for five to six consecutive years; Wellum views this persistent structural supply-demand deficit as justification for holding silver even given 50% drawdown volatility from short-term highs.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highsilver
So what: A multi-year supply deficit in silver creates a fundamental floor; short-term volatility should be used to add exposure rather than exit.
“the demand for silver is much larger than the production. And this has been going on for five, six years” ▶ 43:59
Copper has a structural supply shortfall that is already present today, driven by AI/data center buildout, electrification, and solar; Wellum also highlights the irony that solar panels in space would require large quantities of silver.
causal🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highcoppersilverAIsolar energy
So what: Copper and silver are double-beneficiaries of AI infrastructure build (direct materials demand) and legacy industrial/energy transition demand; the supply gap makes them asymmetric long ideas.
≈ “if we're going to be putting solar panels in the sky, I think that's going to take a lot of silver” paraphrase
Wheaton Precious Metals recently closed a $4 billion royalty deal with BHP, signaling major royalty companies as a vehicle to fund copper mine development; Wellum cites Rick Rule's view that large royalty companies will finance the copper mining expansion.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumWheaton Precious MetalsBHPFranco NevadaRick Rule
So what: Royalty companies are a lower-risk way to invest in the copper supply buildout needed for AI infrastructure; Wheaton/Franco Nevada-type businesses get royalty streams without taking direct mining capex risk.
“we wheat and precious metals a $4 billion, you know, royalty deal. It was just massive with BHP” ▶ 48:14
Rocklink owns Power Metallic (TSX: PNPN, US pink sheets: PNPNF), an unusual junior miner for them — a Quebec property showing a major copper, nickel, PGM, silver, and gold discovery backed by Eric Sprott, Robert McEwen, and other billionaire investors; it is considering a NASDAQ listing.
anecdote🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumPower MetallicEric SprottRobert McEwencopperNASDAQ
So what: Power Metallic (PNPN/PNPNF) is a specific high-conviction junior mining idea with billionaire backing, potential NASDAQ uplisting as a catalyst, and multi-metal exposure in a safe jurisdiction.
Wellum observes that the green-energy narrative has been almost entirely displaced by AI/hyperscaler spending urgency, making it now 'vogue to be a miner and dig holes' — a sharp reversal of the prior ESG-driven capital allocation regime.
contrarian🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highgreen energyAImininghyperscalers
So what: The ESG-to-AI narrative shift is driving capital back into hard-commodity mining; miners that were starved of capital during the green transition are now seeing renewed interest and capital flows.
≈ “we were pushing the green agenda... That's gone. They've forgotten that now...now it's vogue to be a minor and to dig holes” paraphrase
Wellum uses Doomsberg's thesis that commodity prices experience secular deflation over time due to technological efficiency gains; he expects AI to further accelerate this by improving exploration accuracy and mining productivity — but this is a 10+ year dynamic, not relevant to current 5-year investment theses.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumcommoditiesAIminingDoomsberg
So what: Long-term commodity investors should size for the current supply gap trade but remain aware that AI-driven mining efficiency will eventually compress returns; the 5-year window is bullish, the 20-year window is more uncertain.
“over time generally commodities continue to have deflation because of efficiencies” ▶ 52:36
Iran Conflict & Energy Price Outlook 4
Wellum expects the Iran conflict to settle within approximately 6 months; he believes the US and Israel have the upper hand and are pursuing an economic strangulation strategy (cutting energy revenues) rather than infrastructure destruction to force regime change.
prediction🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumIranIsraelUSIRGCenergy
So what: If correct, oil markets are pricing in a risk premium that will deflate by end-2026, creating a headwind for energy equities; Wellum explicitly recommends avoiding the energy sector unless companies can be profitable at $60–70/barrel.
≈ “I would say over the next 6 months or so that the US and Israel have the upper hand significantly in Iran” paraphrase
Despite the Strait of Hormuz closure curtailing major oil flows, crude prices have stayed around $90/barrel — Wellum and host both express surprise at this resilience, crediting rerouting, US production offsets, Canadian and Venezuelan supply, and energy efficiency.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumIranStrait of Hormuzcrude oilVenezuelaUS energy
So what: Energy market resilience in the face of Hormuz disruption demonstrates greater global supply flexibility than many feared; this argues against energy being a sustained inflation driver.
≈ “To think that the price of energy would be just in the $90 per barrel when you've basically curtailed most of that flow in the straight of Hormuz is quite shocking” paraphrase
Wellum recommends only owning energy companies that can generate profit at $60–70/barrel, not $95, because he expects energy prices to fall substantially as the Iran conflict resolves.
prediction🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumenergy sectorcrude oil
So what: Energy equity selection should be stress-tested at $60–70/barrel WTI; companies that need $90+ to be profitable carry significant downside risk on a conflict resolution scenario.
≈ “you can find companies that can make money at 60 and $70, not 95. because I think it's more likely to go back down substantially” paraphrase
The Hormuz disruption is permanently changing shipping and pipeline routes in ways that reduce long-term vulnerability to that chokepoint, and could accelerate uranium/nuclear adoption as a more resilient energy source.
causal🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: mediumStrait of Hormuzuraniumnuclear energypipelines
So what: The Iran conflict is structurally accelerating nuclear energy and pipeline infrastructure investments; uranium exposure benefits from both AI power demand and geopolitical energy security.
≈ “it's probably lowers risk overall in the world because it won't be as vulnerable to that strait and it's also going to I think have longer-term impacts on uranium and a nuclear” paraphrase
Macro Policy: US Debt, Trump Economics & Global Growth 4
Wellum endorses the Trump administration's economic package — tax cuts, deregulation, no-tax on tips/Social Security, 100% capital depreciation, tariff-driven reshoring — as directionally correct for growing the private sector and should produce US GDP growth of 4%+ absent the Iran conflict.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highTrump administrationScott BessentKevin WarshDOGEElon Musk
So what: A bullish macro backdrop for US private-sector investment supports a stronger-than-expected US economy in 2026, which has both inflation and rate-path implications for bond and equity markets.
≈ “if that wasn't happening in Iran which again we can you know everyone can argue whether that makes sense or not...the US economy would be you know probably more like 4% plus in terms of economic growth” paraphrase
The global sovereign debt crisis is described as 'intractable' — present in the US ($40T debt plus unfunded liabilities), Canada, Western Europe, and China — and the only viable near-term strategy is growing nominal GDP faster than the deficit, not actually paying it down.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highUS Treasurysovereign debtgold
So what: Currency debasement is the path of least resistance for all major governments; gold, real assets, and productive businesses with pricing power are the principal hedges.
“every you know these are smart individuals and everybody knows that there's a debt crisis I mean the debt crisis is massive” ▶ 6:46
Canada is likely already in a technical recession (two negative growth quarters), and Western Europe is described as having 'abysmal' growth, in stark contrast to the US — creating a global backdrop where the dollar and US assets face relative demand support.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highCanadaEuropeUK
So what: Divergent growth between the US and the rest of the developed world provides fundamental support for dollar-denominated assets and complicates the bear case for US equities on a relative basis.
≈ “I'm sitting up here in Canada, we got no growth. I would argue it's probably negative” paraphrase
Wellum uses a '5 Ds' investment framework — Digitization, Debt, Debasement, Deglobalization, Demographics — as the five mega-trends driving portfolio construction at Rocklink.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highRocklink Investment Partners
So what: The 5 Ds framework is a reusable screening lens: businesses that address or benefit from any of these structural forces are better positioned for the next decade than those exposed to their disruption.
≈ “we have what we call sort of our five Ds starting with D digitization, debt, debasement, deglobalization, and demographics” paraphrase
Investor Advice & Portfolio Construction 2
Wellum recommends holding extra cash earning 4%+ on US money market instruments as a hedge against volatility, enabling dollar-cost averaging into future corrections without forced selling.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highmoney market funds
So what: At 4%+ on cash, the opportunity cost of holding dry powder is low; maintaining liquidity provides both optionality and psychological protection against panic selling in a volatile market.
“there's nothing wrong with having a bit of a cash reserve with interest rates coming up too, especially in the United States. You can generate 4% plus on a lot of money market instruments” ▶ 1:06:03
Wellum warns that if you want to speculate in high-momentum names like SpaceX, you must be 'incredibly nimble' and ready to exit quickly, because the information flow will be varied and the unknowns will create wild swings in both directions.
framework🗣 Jonathan Wellumconviction: highSpaceX
So what: Short-term speculation in AI/mega-IPO names requires active risk management and stop-loss discipline; passive buy-and-hold is ill-suited for assets priced for perfection.
≈ “if you're going to be short-term you better be incredibly nimble because I think that there will be some wild swings” paraphrase

Cognitive Revolution · Nathan Labenz 1 episode(s) · 40 insights

Babysitting the Machine: Glean's Rebecca Hinds on the Hidden Human Labor of AI at Work106 min40 insights2026-06-10
▶ Watch on YouTube
Rebecca Hinds of Glean's Work AI Institute presents the Work AI Index 2026 (n=6,000 digital workers), finding that while 87% of workers use AI and report saving 13 hours/week, only 13% say their organization is performing significantly better — a gap explained by 6.4 hours/week of hidden 'bot-sitting' labor and a 69% rate of 'bot-shitting' (shipping AI-generated work the employee cannot defend). The episode explores how context fragmentation, perverse incentives, and misalignment between individual productivity and team coordination drive the paradox, and what enterprise AI platforms (specifically Glean's enterprise graph) plus cultural interventions can do about it.
The AI Productivity Paradox: 13-hour savings vs. 13% org improvement · 4Bot-sitting: the hidden 6.4-hour/week labor tax on AI adoption · 4Bot-shitting: 69% of workers ship AI work they cannot defend · 5Coordination neglect and the AI slop hamster wheel · 4Enterprise context graphs as the structural solution · 6AI cost escalation and the multimodel enterprise architecture · 2Org design: flattening, role rebundling, and mission as boss · 5AI detection, psychological safety, and talent retention plays · 7AI-native vs. legacy company bifurcation · 3
The AI Productivity Paradox: 13-hour savings vs. 13% org improvement 4
87% of the 6,000 knowledge workers surveyed use AI, 73% say it makes them more productive, and on average they report saving 13 hours per week — roughly a third of a full work week — yet only 13% say their organization is performing significantly better as a result.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highGleanWork AI InstituteWork AI Index 2026
So what: The productivity-to-performance gap is the central puzzle of enterprise AI ROI; it suggests that individual-level productivity gains are not automatically converting to organizational value, making pure headcount-reduction plays potentially premature.
“87% of workers now use AI. 73% say it makes them more productive. And on average, they report saving 13 hours per week, a third of a full work week. And yet only 13% say their organization is performing significantly better as a result.” ▶ 0:30
Depending on how time-savings questions are framed, reported savings range from 10 to 14 hours per week; when specifically asked about fully automated work output, the figure settles at approximately 11 hours per week.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: Self-reported productivity metrics are highly sensitive to question framing; investors and analysts should discount survey-based AI ROI claims and demand objective telemetry data for verification.
The survey was fielded in December 2025 and January 2026, covering 3,000 US workers, 1,500 in the UK, and 1,500 in Australia — meaning findings reflect a moment just before the latest generation of context-aware models became widely accessible.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026Glean
So what: Results likely represent a floor for AI productivity potential; model improvements in autonomous context retrieval expected in 2026 could meaningfully shift these numbers in a subsequent wave of the survey.
Approximately 36% of all AI sessions on the Glean platform fail — meaning the worker must start completely from scratch or do significant rework — representing a measurable drag on realized productivity.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highGlean
So what: A 36% failure rate is a concrete, objective benchmark for enterprise AI session success; platforms that reduce this rate have a quantifiable value proposition for procurement decisions.
“We're seeing about 36% of all AI sessions fail, meaning a worker goes to use the technology and it's not successful.” ▶ 29:11
Bot-sitting: the hidden 6.4-hour/week labor tax on AI adoption 4
Workers spend an average of 6.4 hours per week on 'bot-sitting' — feeding AI context, overseeing outputs, debugging, and cleaning up errors — consuming roughly half of all reported AI time savings and going largely untracked, unrewarded, and unincentivized.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: Any enterprise AI business case that counts raw productivity savings without netting out bot-sitting overhead is overstating ROI by approximately 2x; investors evaluating AI-driven efficiency plays should demand net figures.
“It's the feeding AI context. It's the overseeing the AI. It's the debugging the AI. It's the cleaning up after the AI. And it's a massive chunk of labor on average upwards of 6 hours per week.” ▶ 15:42
The highest 'exhaustion multiplier' in bot-sitting comes from two sources: (1) manually feeding AI context that a properly integrated system should already have, and (2) debugging probabilistic LLM outputs where the failure cause is opaque.
causal🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: Enterprise AI platforms that eliminate manual context injection (via organizational knowledge graphs) and improve output explainability capture the highest marginal value relative to point-solution AI tools.
“The highest exhaustion multiplier is associated with feeding AI context right because that is in the best case something your AI should know.” ▶ 30:28
Bot-sitting is correlated with both increased AI success (more bot-sitting associates with more effective AI use) and increased likelihood of leaving the organization, suggesting it simultaneously drives better outcomes and burns out the employees most capable of delivering those outcomes.
causal🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: mediumWork AI Index 2026
So what: Organizations that invest in reducing bot-sitting friction (better-integrated platforms, context graphs) are likely retaining their most productive AI-capable employees as a second-order benefit.
Employees who spend heavy time bot-sitting but whose organization lacks a visible AI strategy are more likely to seek employment elsewhere, interpreting the manual context-feeding burden as evidence that leadership has no coherent AI plan.
causal🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: mediumWork AI Index 2026
So what: AI tool fragmentation is a hidden retention risk: the top performers doing the most AI work are also the first to read the signal that the org is falling behind and start looking elsewhere.
“If you're wasting your days bots sitting and your organization is vocalizing in all hands in town halls that this is a transformative technology and we're transforming there's a disconnect.” ▶ 1:01:45
Bot-shitting: 69% of workers ship AI work they cannot defend 5
69% of surveyed workers admit to some form of 'bot-shitting' — passing off AI-generated work that they cannot explain or defend if questioned — a rate that has risen alongside AI adoption pressure.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: At a 69% rate, uninspected AI outputs are already embedded in organizational workflows; firms without quality-verification infrastructure are accumulating hidden operational and reputational risk at scale.
“69% admit to doing it, a number that reflects both the incredible progress that AIs have made and perhaps the amount of [meaningless] work that people are asked to do.” ▶ 1:46
A separate sub-measure finds 40-41% of employees say they ship AI-generated work that they could not explain if asked — distinguishing a harder core of unverified AI outputs from the broader bot-shitting category.
data_point🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: 40%+ of knowledge worker outputs may be unverified AI artifacts; this is a material governance and quality-control risk for regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal) that directly affects liability and compliance.
“We see 40 to 41% of employees saying they ship AI work that they couldn't explain if asked.” ▶ 17:05
Bot-shitting is driven by a cycle: AI adoption pressure → bot-sitting exhaustion → 'satisficing' (shipping the first good-enough output) → loss of ownership → disengagement, not primarily by individual laziness or bad intent.
framework🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: Penalizing individuals for bot-shitting without addressing the systemic drivers (tool fragmentation, lack of context, perverse metrics) will not solve the problem and may accelerate turnover of the most AI-capable employees.
“The bot sitting is a precursor to the bot [shitting]. It's the exhaustion and employees hit a breaking point.” ▶ 24:09
Bot-shitting is also correlated with employees being on the active job market, and the hypothesis is that extensive bot-shitting reflects broader organizational disengagement, with some high performers leaving because their market value has increased beyond what their current employer offers.
causal🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: mediumWork AI Index 2026
So what: Bot-shitting metrics can serve as a leading indicator of both quality risk and upcoming talent loss; organizations need a dual monitoring approach — output quality scoring plus employee engagement.
The most common visible form of bot-shitting is 'polished nonsense' — outputs that look finished and professional but are substantively hollow — which can pass quality filters precisely because AI is good at surface formatting.
framework🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: high
So what: Standard quality review processes built around formatting and completeness will systematically miss bot-shitting; organizations need substantive content evaluation, not just style checks.
“It looks polished, it looks finished, it looks like we've done the assignment. When we dive a little bit deeper, it's actually quite hollow.” ▶ 17:19
Coordination neglect and the AI slop hamster wheel 4
Individual AI productivity gains fail to translate to organizational improvement partly due to 'coordination neglect': one worker expands a bullet into a 5-page AI report, the next worker collapses it back to a bullet, both look productive, but net organizational value created is zero.
framework🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: Measuring AI ROI at the individual output level (tokens generated, tasks completed) will systematically overstate organizational value; enterprise ROI measurement must account for cross-team coordination efficiency.
“Each person looks productive. Each person looks like they've saved massive amounts of time. But when you put the pieces together, it's just this hamster wheel of AI slop.” ▶ 35:32
A substantial share of employees hide their AI usage from managers — specifically because disclosing time savings risks being assigned 6 more hours of work — creating an informal prisoner's dilemma that prevents organizations from capturing aggregate AI gains.
causal🗣 Rebecca Hindsconviction: highWork AI Index 2026
So what: Incentive structures that treat AI-generated time savings as 'slack to be filled' will suppress adoption transparency and prevent organizations from reallocating productivity gains toward higher-value work.
“You tell your manager or your organization you're getting 6 hours back probably you're going to then get 6 hours more of work and that is the wrong calculus.” ▶ 34:47
Measuring AI adoption by token consumption or tool-click counts — a practice still observed in organizations — is easily gamed and creates perverse incentives that reward symbolic rather than substantive AI use.
framework🗣 Nathan Labenzconviction: high
